<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]

**Why is the surface area of an irregular fruit such as a mango not measured by direct geometry?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A mango has an irregular, non-spherical shape with lumpy contours, so its surface area cannot be expressed by a single closed-form equation such as $A = \pi D^2$ for a sphere. Direct geometry requires a regular surface of known curvature. Therefore, surface area of a mango must be determined either by **direct measurement** (e.g., shellac paper, peel, or paraffin wax method) or by **geometric approximation** (e.g., treating the mango as a prolate spheroid with semi-axes $a$ and $b$ and using $A = 2\pi b^2 + \frac{2\pi a b}{e}\sin^{-1}(e)$). **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]

**Define the "geometric mean diameter" $D_g$ of an irregular fruit. State the formula for its surface area.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The geometric mean diameter $D_g$ is the diameter of an equivalent sphere whose volume equals the volume of the irregular fruit whose three principal semi-axes are $a$, $b$, $c$.

$$D_g = (a b c)^{1/3}$$

The surface area of the irregular fruit, approximated as this equivalent sphere, is:

$$A = \pi D_g^2 = \pi (a b c)^{2/3} \quad [\text{m}^2]$$

The dimensions $a, b, c$ are obtained by measuring the longest, intermediate, and shortest distances across the fruit using a Vernier caliper. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **shellac paper method** for measuring the surface area of an irregular fruit. Mention the procedure, instruments required, accuracy, and limitations.

**(b) [7 marks]** The three principal diameters of a guava are measured as $D_1 = 85$ mm, $D_2 = 70$ mm, $D_3 = 65$ mm. Calculate its surface area using the geometric mean diameter method. State any assumptions made.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
The shellac paper method is a *direct* method in which a thin, shellac-coated paper is wrapped around the fruit. When peeled off, the paper takes the exact shape of the fruit's surface. The 2D area of this paper is then measured with a **planimeter** or by counting squares on **graph paper**.

**2. Procedure (3 marks):**
- Select a thin, flexible paper uniformly coated with shellac.
- Cut the paper into small rectangular strips (≈ 1 cm × 2 cm).
- Moisten the paper to make it pliable.
- Apply each strip to a small portion of the fruit's surface, pressing gently so it conforms to the contour.
- Allow the shellac to dry and harden (≈ 30–60 seconds).
- Peel off the strips carefully; they retain the curvature of the fruit.
- Arrange the strips on graph paper, trace their outline, and measure total area with a planimeter or by counting squares.

**3. Instruments Required (1 mark):**
Shellac paper, planimeter (or graph paper), vernier caliper, brush, water, scissors.

**4. Accuracy and Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Accuracy:** ± 1–3 % for most fruits; considered the most accurate practical method.
- **Limitations:** Destructive; time-consuming; difficult on very small or highly hairy fruits; strips may overlap or leave gaps if not applied carefully.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Given:** $D_1 = 85$ mm = 0.085 m, $D_2 = 70$ mm = 0.070 m, $D_3 = 65$ mm = 0.065 m

**Step 1 — Compute the geometric mean diameter $D_g$ (3 marks):**

$$D_g = (D_1 \cdot D_2 \cdot D_3)^{1/3}$$

$$D_g = (0.085 \times 0.070 \times 0.065)^{1/3}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the product (2 marks):**

$$0.085 \times 0.070 = 0.00595$$

$$0.00595 \times 0.065 = 0.00038675$$

**Step 3 — Take the cube root (1 mark):**

$$D_g = (3.8675 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3} = 7.288 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \approx 0.0729 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute surface area (1 mark):**

$$A = \pi D_g^2 = \pi \times (0.0729)^2$$

$$A = \pi \times 0.005314 = 0.01670 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\,A \approx 0.0167 \text{ m}^2 = 167 \text{ cm}^2\,}$$

**Assumptions (additional, not counted separately):**
1. The guava is approximated as a smooth ellipsoid of revolution.
2. The three measured diameters are mutually perpendicular and pass through the centre.
3. Surface roughness and protuberances are neglected.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **photographic (silhouette) method** and the **paraffin wax method** for measuring the surface area of fruits. Compare their accuracy, advantages, and disadvantages in a tabular form.

**(b) [7 marks]** A cylindrical fruit of length $L = 18$ cm and diameter $D = 5$ cm is modelled as a cylinder with two hemispherical ends. Calculate:
   (i) The surface area of the fruit.
   (ii) The specific surface area (surface area to volume ratio) in m⁻¹.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**1. Photographic (Silhouette) Method (3 marks):**
- The fruit is suspended by a thin thread against a uniform white background.
- A photograph (or its shadow on a screen) is taken from a fixed distance with the camera axis perpendicular to the fruit's longest dimension.
- The 2D image area is measured with a planimeter or image-analysis software.
- The surface area is calculated assuming the body is a **surface of revolution** about its longest axis:

$$A \approx \dfrac{\pi L}{4} \times P^2$$

where $L$ is the length of the fruit and $P$ is the perimeter of its silhouette (measured from the photograph).

- **Advantage:** Non-destructive, fast, suitable for online sorting.
- **Disadvantage:** Accuracy depends on the revolution assumption; overlapping or hidden surface features are missed.

**2. Paraffin Wax Method (2 marks):**
- The fruit is dipped in molten paraffin wax (60–70 °C) for a few seconds.
- Wax solidifies into a thin uniform shell that conforms exactly to the surface.
- The wax shell is carefully peeled off, cut along one meridian, and flattened.
- The flattened 2D area is measured with a planimeter or by counting graph-paper squares.

- **Advantage:** Highly accurate (error < 2 %); suitable for soft, delicate fruits.
- **Disadvantage:** Destructive; wax may tear; the cut introduces a small seam error.

**3. Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Photographic Method | Paraffin Wax Method |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Nature | Non-destructive | Destructive |
| Accuracy | ± 3–7 % | ± 1–2 % |
| Time required | < 1 minute per fruit | 5–10 minutes per fruit |
| Equipment | Camera, planimeter, software | Wax bath, planimeter |
| Suitable for | Spherical, oblong, regular shapes | Any irregular shape |
| Limitation | Hidden surfaces not captured | Wax may tear on hairy fruits |

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Given:**
- Length of cylindrical portion: $L = 18$ cm = 0.18 m
- Diameter: $D = 5$ cm = 0.05 m
- Model: Central cylinder + two hemispherical ends

**Part (i) — Surface Area (4 marks):**

The total surface area is the lateral area of the cylinder plus the area of one full sphere (two hemispheres combined):

$$A = \pi D L + \pi D^2$$

Substitute values:

$$A = \pi \times 0.05 \times 0.18 + \pi \times (0.05)^2$$

$$A = \pi \times 0.009 + \pi \times 0.0025$$

$$A = \pi \times 0.0115$$

$$A = 0.03613 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\,A \approx 0.0361 \text{ m}^2 = 361.3 \text{ cm}^2\,}$$

**Part (ii) — Specific Surface Area (3 marks):**

Volume of the fruit = volume of cylinder + volume of sphere (two hemispheres):

$$V = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot L + \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^3$$

$$V = \dfrac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} \times 0.18 + \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (0.025)^3$$

$$V = \pi \times 0.000625 \times 0.18 + \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$V = 3.534 \times 10^{-4} + 6.545 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$V = 4.189 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

Specific surface area:

$$\dfrac{A}{V} = \dfrac{0.03613}{4.189 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\dfrac{A}{V} \approx 86.25 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{A}{V} \approx 86.3 \text{ m}^{-1}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget to include the **hemispherical end caps** when computing the surface area of a cylindrical fruit. If you write only $A = \pi D L$, you will lose 2 marks out of 7. Always add the two hemispheres (= one full sphere) when the question states the fruit is a "cylinder with two hemispherical ends." Also, ensure you convert all dimensions to **metres** before computing, since the final answer is expected in **m²**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam**

- [ ] **Definition:** Surface area of a food material is the external boundary area in contact with the environment; critical for heat and mass transfer.
- [ ] **Three Categories of Methods:** Direct (peel, shellac, wax), Indirect (photograph, silhouette, beta gauge), Geometric Approximation (sphere, ellipsoid, cylinder, geometric mean).
- [ ] **Most-Tested Formula (Memorize):** $A = \pi (a b c)^{2/3}$ where $a, b, c$ are the three principal semi-axes.
- [ ] **Sphere:** $A = \pi D^2$ (use when fruit is approximately round, e.g., apple, tomato).
- [ ] **Ellipsoid of Revolution (Prolate Spheroid):** $A = 2\pi b^2 + \frac{2\pi a b}{e}\sin^{-1}(e)$, where $e = \sqrt{1 - b^2/a^2}$.
- [ ] **Cylinder + 2 Hemispheres:** $A = \pi D L + \pi D^2$ (use for cucumber, banana, carrot).
- [ ] **Geometric Mean Diameter:** $D_g = (a b c)^{1/3}$; $A = \pi D_g^2$.
- [ ] **Shellac Paper Method:** Direct, accurate (±1–3%), destructive, time-consuming.
- [ ] **Photographic / Silhouette Method:** Non-destructive, fast, assumes surface of revolution; less accurate (±3–7%).
- [ ] **Beta Gauge Method:** Used for small seeds and grains; based on radiation attenuation.
- [ ] **Specific Surface Area:** $A/V$ in $\text{m}^{-1}$; useful for comparing drying rates.
- [ ] **Always convert** all linear dimensions to **metres** before computing area in $\text{m}^2$.
- [ ] **State assumptions clearly** in your answer — examiners award 1 mark for this.
- [ ] **Always draw a sketch** of the fruit with axes labelled $a$, $b$, $c$ before applying the formula.
- [ ] **Prolate vs Oblate:** Prolate = long and narrow (mango); Oblate = flat and wide (pumpkin slice).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Egg surface area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT526 - ENGINEERING PROPERTIES OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Egg surface area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Egg Surface Area (S):** The total area of the outer shell that bounds the three-dimensional volume occupied by an egg. It is a fundamental **geometric / shape-dependent engineering property** that governs the rate of heat transfer, mass transfer (moisture/gas exchange), and surface-coating operations during food processing.

For engineering analysis, an egg is mathematically modeled as a **prolate spheroid** (a sphere stretched along one axis), since its length ($L$) is always greater than its maximum breadth ($B$). The ratio $L/B$ for chicken eggs typically lies in the range **$1.20$ to $1.35$**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an egg as a tiny, closed **thermos flask**. Whatever you want to do to the egg from the outside — boiling it, coating it with wax, drying it, painting it with edible film, or letting it lose moisture — the operation happens *only* through its surface. Therefore, the **bigger the surface area, the faster the exchange** (heat in, water out, coating absorbed, etc.). Knowing this single number tells an engineer how much thermal energy to supply, how long a drying process will take, and how much coating material to spray.

### 1.3 Why It Matters in Food Engineering

- **Heat transfer** during boiling, pasteurization, or cooling.
- **Mass transfer** during brining, pickling, and dehydration.
- **Coating uniformity** in shellac, oil, or edible-film applications.
- **Packaging design** (cushioning volume = egg volume + shell surface).
- **Quality grading** (size class is partly derived from projected area).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Egg is the *standard pedagogical object* used in KTU 2024 Scheme to teach surface-area determination for *irregularly shaped food bodies* because it has measurable major and minor axes and a known mass.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Three Methods to Determine Egg Surface Area

A KTU student must be fluent in **three** independent routes; examiners alternate between them.

**Route 1 — Direct Measurement (Reference Method)**
- Strip the egg with aluminium foil or use a "peel-and-stick" film.
- Trace the foil onto graph paper or weigh the foil.
- Used as a **calibration standard** to verify empirical equations.

**Route 2 — Geometrical (Prolate-Spheroid) Model**
The egg is replaced by a prolate spheroid with:
- Semi-major axis $a = L/2$
- Semi-minor axis $b = B/2$

The **Why:** the prolate spheroid is the *simplest closed surface* whose two visible diameters match the egg's $L$ and $B$.

**Route 3 — Empirical (ASAE / Weight-Based) Correlation**
The **How:** when scales (not callipers) are available, mass $W$ alone is used. The ASAE correlation was fitted on thousands of eggs and is the KTU-recommended quick method.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $S = 2\pi b^{2} + \dfrac{2\pi a b}{e}\sin^{-1}(e)$ | Exact prolate-spheroid area | cm² | Numerical / derivations |
| 2 | $S \approx \pi L B$ | $L$ = length, $B$ = breadth | cm² | Quick approximation |
| 3 | $S = 4.835\,W^{0.662}$ | $W$ = egg mass | cm², g | ASAE empirical (KTU favourite) |
| 4 | $S = 3.978\,W^{2/3}$ | $W$ = mass | cm², g | Spherical-equivalent approx. |
| 5 | $e = \sqrt{1 - b^{2}/a^{2}}$ | Eccentricity | – | Needed for (1) |
| 6 | $V = \dfrac{\pi}{6} L B^{2}$ | Egg volume (spheroid) | cm³ | Mass ↔ density link |
| 7 | $\rho = \dfrac{W}{V}$ | Apparent density | g/cm³ | Quality check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always quote $L$ and $B$ in **cm** and $W$ in **g**. The surface area emerges in **cm²**. Forgetting unit conversion is the most common deduction.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- Designing **boiling/cooling time** of eggs in food plants (Biot number needs area).
- Calculating **shrink-wrap film** mass per tray.
- Estimating **water loss** during storage (Fick's law needs $A$).
- Computing **thermal resistance** of the shell in pasteurization lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Prolate-Spheroid Surface Area (Exact Formula)

A prolate spheroid is generated by rotating an ellipse (semi-axes $a > b$) about its major axis. Its surface area is the surface of revolution of the ellipse $x^{2}/a^{2} + y^{2}/b^{2} = 1$.

**Step 1 — Surface-of-revolution integral.**

For a curve $y(x)$ rotated about the $x$-axis, the surface area is

$$
S = 2\pi \int_{-a}^{a} y\,\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}}\,dx
$$

**Step 2 — Express $y$ and $dy/dx$ for the ellipse.**

From the ellipse equation:

$$
y = b\,\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}}
$$

Differentiate:

$$
\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\dfrac{b\,x}{a^{2}\,\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify the radical.**

After substituting and squaring the derivative, the integrand collapses to

$$
\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{a^{2} - e^{2}x^{2}}{a^{2} - x^{2}}}
$$

where the eccentricity is defined as

$$
e = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Change variable.** Let $x = a\sin\theta$, so $dx = a\cos\theta\,d\theta$. The integration domain $-a \le x \le a$ becomes $-\pi/2 \le \theta \le \pi/2$.

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral.**

The integrand simplifies neatly to a constant plus a sine term, giving

$$
S = 2\pi b^{2} + \dfrac{2\pi a b}{e}\,\sin^{-1}(e)
$$

This is the **exact** prolate-spheroid surface-area equation cited in the KTU formula sheet as **Formula 1**.

**Step 6 — Sphere check (consistency test).**
When $a = b$ (a perfect sphere), $e \to 0$. Using the limit $\lim_{e \to 0}\dfrac{\sin^{-1}(e)}{e} = 1$:

$$
\lim_{e \to 0} S = 2\pi a^{2} + 2\pi a^{2} = 4\pi a^{2} \quad \checkmark
$$

This matches the sphere's well-known area — confirming the formula is dimensionally and physically correct.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem.** A chicken egg has length $L = 5.70\,\text{cm}$, breadth $B = 4.20\,\text{cm}$, mass $W = 60\,\text{g}$. Determine its surface area by (a) the exact prolate-spheroid formula, (b) the $\pi L B$ approximation, and (c) the ASAE empirical correlation.

**Step 0 — Convert to semi-axes.**

$$
a = \dfrac{L}{2} = 2.85\;\text{cm}, \qquad b = \dfrac{B}{2} = 2.10\;\text{cm}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the eccentricity.**

$$
e = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}} = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{2.10^{2}}{2.85^{2}}} = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.41}{8.1225}} = \sqrt{0.4570} = 0.6760
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sin^{-1}(e)$.**

$$
\sin^{-1}(0.6760) = 0.7445\;\text{rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the exact formula (Formula 1).**

$$
S_{\text{exact}} = 2\pi b^{2} + \dfrac{2\pi a b}{e}\,\sin^{-1}(e)
$$

$$
S_{\text{exact}} = 2\pi(2.10)^{2} + \dfrac{2\pi(2.85)(2.10)}{0.6760}\,(0.7445)
$$

$$
S_{\text{exact}} = 27.704 + (45.638)(0.7445) = 27.704 + 33.978 = 61.68\;\text{cm}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the $\pi L B$ approximation (Formula 2).**

$$
S_{\text{approx}} = \pi L B = \pi (5.70)(4.20) = \pi (23.94) = 75.20\;\text{cm}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the ASAE correlation (Formula 3).**

$$
S_{\text{ASAE}} = 4.835\,W^{0.662} = 4.835\,(60)^{0.662}
$$

Compute $60^{0.662} = e^{0.662 \ln 60} = e^{0.662 \times 4.0943} = e^{2.7104} = 15.02$

$$
S_{\text{ASAE}} = 4.835 \times 15.02 = 72.62\;\text{cm}^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare and conclude.**

| Method | $S$ (cm²) | Deviation from exact |
|---|---|---|
| Exact prolate-spheroid | **61.68** | — (reference) |
| ASAE empirical | 72.62 | +17.7 % |
| $\pi L B$ | 75.20 | +21.9 % |

**Conclusion:** The exact formula is the most rigorous; the $\pi L B$ shortcut *over-predicts* because the egg is **less elongated** than the rectangle $L \times B$ it is inscribed in. The ASAE equation, fitted on real eggs, sits between the two and is the **engineering-recommended value**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Methods to Determine Egg Surface Area

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Egg Geometry] --> B{Measured Parameters Available?}
    B -- Length L and Breadth B --> C[Geometrical Method]
    B -- Mass W only --> D[Empirical ASAE Method]
    B -- Both --> E[Use Both and Cross-Check]
    C --> C1[Compute a = L/2, b = B/2]
    C1 --> C2[Compute e = sqrt 1 minus b squared over a squared]
    C2 --> C3[Apply exact prolate spheroid area formula]
    C3 --> F[Surface Area S in cm squared]
    D --> D1[Apply ASAE: S = 4.835 W to power 0.662]
    D1 --> F
    E --> C3
    E --> D1
    E --> G[Average or Validate against foil-strip method]
    G --> F
    F --> H{Engineering Use Case}
    H --> H1[Heat Transfer Rate Calculation]
    H --> H2[Mass Transfer / Drying]
    H --> H3[Coating and Packaging Design]
    F --> I[Output: Design Decision]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Prolate-Spheroid Surface-Area Logic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Input L and B in cm] --> I2[Semi axes a and b]
    I2 --> I3[Eccentricity e]
    I3 --> I4[Term 1: 2 pi b squared]
    I3 --> I5[Term 2: 2 pi a b over e times arcsin of e]
    I4 --> I6[Sum equals S exact]
    I5 --> I6
    I6 --> I7[Sanity check: e tends to 0 gives 4 pi a squared]
```

### 4.3 Architectural Block — Why Surface Area Matters in a Food Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Egg enters processing line] --> X1[Cleaning]
    X1 --> X2[Coating]
    X2 --> X3[Boiling or Pasteurization]
    X3 --> X4[Cooling]
    X4 --> X5[Packaging]
    X1 -.depends on.-> S[Egg Surface Area S]
    X2 -.depends on.-> S
    X3 -.depends on.-> S
    X4 -.depends on.-> S
    X5 -.depends on.-> S
    S --> Y[Process Time, Energy, Material Dosing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term *prolate spheroid*. Why is an egg modelled as one for surface-area calculations? **(3 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A *prolate spheroid* is the three-dimensional solid obtained by rotating an ellipse about its major axis, producing a shape that is elongated along one direction. An egg is modelled as a prolate spheroid because its length $L$ is consistently greater than its maximum breadth $B$ (with $L/B$ typically between $1.20$ and $1.35$), and the spheroid's two principal axes match these two measurable diameters exactly. The model is mathematically tractable and gives a closed-form surface-area expression that is sufficiently accurate for engineering design. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State the ASAE empirical equation for egg surface area. Under what conditions is it preferred over the prolate-spheroid formula? **(3 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The ASAE empirical equation is
> $$
> S = 4.835\,W^{0.662}
> $$
> where $S$ is in cm² and $W$ is the egg mass in grams. It is preferred when only a weighing scale (and not callipers) is available — for example, on a high-throughput grading line — because it gives a quick, accurate estimate from a single measurement. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Style]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **Q1 (a)** Derive the exact surface-area expression for a prolate spheroid in terms of its semi-axes $a$, $b$ and eccentricity $e$. State clearly the meaning of each symbol. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q1 (b)** A hen's egg has length $5.80\,\text{cm}$, breadth $4.30\,\text{cm}$ and mass $62\,\text{g}$. Calculate its surface area using (i) the exact prolate-spheroid formula, and (ii) the ASAE empirical correlation. Compare and comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q1 (a)

Starting from the surface-of-revolution integral of an ellipse rotated about its major axis:

$$
S = 2\pi \int_{-a}^{a} y\,\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}}\,dx
$$

The ellipse equation gives

$$
y = b\,\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}}, \qquad \dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\dfrac{b\,x}{a^{2}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}}}
$$

Define eccentricity

$$
e = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}
$$

Substituting and simplifying the integrand, then using $x = a\sin\theta$, the integral evaluates to

$$
\boxed{\,S = 2\pi b^{2} + \dfrac{2\pi a b}{e}\,\sin^{-1}(e)\,}
$$

where $a$ = semi-major axis ($L/2$), $b$ = semi-minor axis ($B/2$), and $e$ = eccentricity. Units: cm if $a,b$ are in cm, giving $S$ in cm². **(7 marks — full marks for integral setup, definition of $e$, change of variable and final closed form.)**

#### Model Solution — Q1 (b)

**Inputs:** $L = 5.80\,\text{cm}$, $B = 4.30\,\text{cm}$, $W = 62\,\text{g}$.

**Semi-axes:**

$$
a = 2.90\;\text{cm}, \qquad b = 2.15\;\text{cm}
$$

**Eccentricity:**

$$
e = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{2.15^{2}}{2.90^{2}}} = \sqrt{1 - 0.5497} = \sqrt{0.4503} = 0.6711
$$

**$\sin^{-1}(e)$:**

$$
\sin^{-1}(0.6711) = 0.7379\;\text{rad}
$$

**(i) Exact formula:**

$$
S_{\text{exact}} = 2\pi(2.15)^{2} + \dfrac{2\pi(2.90)(2.15)}{0.6711}\,(0.7379)
$$

$$
S_{\text{exact}} = 29.041 + (58.317)(0.7379) = 29.041 + 43.030 = 72.07\;\text{cm}^{2}
$$

**(ii) ASAE empirical:**

$$
S_{\text{ASAE}} = 4.835\,(62)^{0.662} = 4.835 \times 15.34 = 74.17\;\text{cm}^{2}
$$

**Comparison and comment:**

| Method | $S$ (cm²) |
|---|---|
| Exact prolate spheroid | 72.07 |
| ASAE empirical | 74.17 |
| Deviation | +2.9 % |

The two values differ by less than 3 %, confirming the ASAE correlation as a reliable engineering estimate. The empirical value is slightly higher because the ASAE fit implicitly accounts for shell curvature, the blunt ends, and the small equator bulge not captured by the smooth spheroid. **(7 marks — distribute as: 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for computation, 2 marks for comparison comment.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Paper Style]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
>
> **Q2 (a)** With the aid of a labelled diagram, explain how a prolate spheroid differs from a sphere. Derive the eccentricity $e$ of a prolate spheroid and state its allowable range. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q2 (b)** A batch of eggs has the following data: $L = 6.00\,\text{cm}$, $B = 4.40\,\text{cm}$, $W = 65\,\text{g}$. Compute the volume, the apparent density, and the surface area using the formula $S = \pi L B$. State one engineering scenario where this approximation is acceptable and one where it is **not** acceptable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q2 (a)

A **sphere** has all three semi-axes equal ($a = b = c$) and is perfectly symmetrical about every axis passing through its centre. A **prolate spheroid** has $a > b = c$; it is generated by rotating an ellipse about its major axis, so it is elongated along one direction only.

Eccentricity of a prolate spheroid:

$$
e = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}
$$

**Range:** $0 \le e < 1$. The lower bound $e = 0$ corresponds to a sphere ($a = b$); the upper bound $e \to 1$ corresponds to an infinitely elongated needle-like shape. For chicken eggs, $e$ lies between $0.55$ and $0.75$. **(7 marks — 3 for diagram/description, 2 for derivation, 2 for range and example values.)**

#### Model Solution — Q2 (b)

**Inputs:** $L = 6.00\,\text{cm}$, $B = 4.40\,\text{cm}$, $W = 65\,\text{g}$.

**Volume (spheroid model):**

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi}{6} L B^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{6} (6.00)(4.40)^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{6}(6.00)(19.36) = \dfrac{\pi}{6}(116.16) = 60.82\;\text{cm}^{3}
$$

**Apparent density:**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{65}{60.82} = 1.069\;\text{g/cm}^{3}
$$

**Surface area using $S = \pi L B$:**

$$
S = \pi (6.00)(4.40) = \pi (26.40) = 82.94\;\text{cm}^{2}
$$

**Acceptable scenario:** Quick sizing of packaging trays or estimating the foil-strip area for classroom demonstrations, where a few percent error is tolerable.

**Not acceptable scenario:** Precise heat-transfer calculation in a continuous pasteurization line, where the over-prediction (≈20 %) would lead to undersized heat exchangers and unsafe processing. **(7 marks — 2 for volume, 2 for density, 2 for area, 1 for scenario justification.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit mix-up:** $L$ and $B$ must be in **cm**, $W$ in **g**, to obtain $S$ in **cm²**. Mixing inches or kg silently loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sin^{-1}$ in radians:** The function in the exact formula is *arcsine*, not arcsin-of-degree. Marks are deducted if degrees are used.
> 3. **Eccentricity undefined when $a < b$:** If the candidate accidentally swaps axes, the square root becomes imaginary — a *guaranteed zero* for that sub-part.
> 4. **Sphere limit check:** Examiners award bonus marks if the candidate verifies that the formula collapses to $4\pi a^{2}$ when $a = b$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Egg shape:** prolate spheroid, $L > B$, $L/B$ ratio ≈ 1.20 – 1.35.
- **Three master formulas** to memorize for KTU 2024:
  - Exact: $S = 2\pi b^{2} + \dfrac{2\pi a b}{e}\sin^{-1}(e)$
  - Quick: $S = \pi L B$
  - Empirical (ASAE): $S = 4.835\,W^{0.662}$
- **Eccentricity:** $e = \sqrt{1 - b^{2}/a^{2}}$; range $0 \le e < 1$.
- **Volume companion:** $V = \dfrac{\pi}{6} L B^{2}$, used with $W$ to find density.
- **Engineering drivers:** heat transfer, drying, coating, packaging — all scale with $S$.
- **Sanity check:** $S$ for a chicken egg should be **60 – 80 cm²**; outside this range, suspect a unit or input error.
- **Method selection rule:** $L, B$ known → use exact; $W$ only → use ASAE; both available → cross-check.
- **Common slip to avoid:** $\pi L B$ **overestimates** the true area by ~15–22 % because the egg's rounded ends waste the corners of the bounding rectangle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pore surface area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT526 - ENGINEERING PROPERTIES OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pore surface area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pore Surface Area (PSA)** is defined as the total internal interfacial area (per unit mass or per unit volume of material) enclosed within the void spaces, capillaries, and cavities of a porous solid that are accessible to an external fluid phase (gas or liquid).

In the context of food materials engineering, pore surface area is a **structural–functional property** that quantifies the extent of solid–fluid interface generated by the network of pores within a food matrix. It is conventionally expressed in two engineering forms:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Specific (Mass) Pore Surface Area | $S_m$ | **m²/g** |
| Volumetric Pore Surface Area | $S_v$ | **m²/m³** or **m²/cm³** |

Mathematically, the relationship between the two forms is governed by the bulk (apparent) density of the food material $\rho_b$:

$$S_v = \rho_b \cdot S_m$$

### 1.1.1 Pore Classification (IUPAC Convention)

The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classifies pores based on their width ($w$):

- **Micropores:** $w < 2$ nm — relevant in low-moisture powders, amorphous starch matrices
- **Mesopores:** $2 \text{ nm} \leq w \leq 50$ nm — typical of freeze-dried foods, porous cereals
- **Macropores:** $w > 50$ nm — characteristic of bread crumb, extruded snacks, puffed products

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Food materials exhibit **hierarchical porosity** — a wide distribution spanning all three IUPAC classes — which directly influences water activity, sorption isotherms, and drying kinetics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a single raisin versus a slice of fresh bread. The raisin feels *hard* and *dense* because its internal pore surface is small and collapsed. The bread feels *light* and *spongy* because its crumb exposes an enormous internal surface to your fingers and to the air. **Pore surface area is the "hidden skin" inside a food** — the area that is not visible to the eye but is critically available for water migration, oil absorption, flavor retention, and gas exchange.

A teaspoon (≈ 5 g) of freeze-dried coffee can have a pore surface area equivalent to **half a football field** (≈ 2,000–3,000 m²/g), precisely because sublimation during freeze-drying preserves the void network left by ice crystals.

## 1.3 Relevance in Food Engineering

| Application Domain | Role of Pore Surface Area |
|---|---|
| **Dehydration / Drying** | Governs capillary-driven moisture transport and effective diffusivity |
| **Rehydration** | Determines kinetics of water uptake in dried fruits, pasta, instant powders |
| **Sorption Isotherms** | BET monolayer capacity is a direct function of accessible surface |
| **Textural Quality** | Influences crispness, hardness, and acoustic emission in crispy foods |
| **Shelf-Life & Packaging** | Affects oxygen permeability and oxidation kinetics in porous powders |
| **Extrusion & Baking** | Predicts expansion ratio and final porosity development |
| **Encapsulation** | Carriers (maltodextrin, modified starch) require high surface for core loading |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The "Why" and the "How"

The quantification of pore surface area proceeds along three fundamental engineering steps:

1. **Step 1 — Probe Selection:** A molecular probe (typically N₂ at 77 K, CO₂ at 273 K, or Kr at 77 K) is allowed to physically contact the accessible internal surface. The probe must be inert, of known molecular cross-section, and small enough to enter the pore of interest.
2. **Step 2 — Physisorption Quantification:** The amount of probe gas adsorbed at successive relative pressures $P/P_0$ is measured gravimetrically or volumetrically. The data points generate an **adsorption isotherm**.
3. **Step 3 — Model Inversion:** The isotherm is fitted to a theoretical model (BET, Langmuir, Kelvin–Washburn, BJH, DFT) to back-calculate surface area, pore volume, and pore size distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why multiple models exist:** No single model captures the full complexity of a food matrix. BET captures the **monolayer regime** ($P/P_0 < 0.35$); the Kelvin equation captures **capillary condensation** in mesopores; mercury intrusion captures **macropores and throats**.

## 2.2 BET Theory — Multi-Molecular Layer Adsorption

The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) model extends the Langmuir monolayer concept to admit **stacked molecular layers** above the surface. The model assumes:

- Heat of adsorption for the **first layer** is $E_1$ (higher than liquefaction).
- Heat of adsorption for **all subsequent layers** equals the heat of liquefaction $E_L$.
- No lateral interaction between adsorbed molecules.

The classical BET linear form is:

$$\frac{P / P_0}{W \left(1 - P / P_0\right)} = \frac{1}{W_m C} + \frac{C - 1}{W_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$

Where:

- $W$ = mass of gas adsorbed at relative pressure $P/P_0$ (g adsorbate / g solid)
- $W_m$ = monolayer capacity (g adsorbate / g solid)
- $C$ = dimensionless BET constant, related to the net heat of adsorption: $C \propto \exp\left[(E_1 - E_L)/RT\right]$
- $P$ = equilibrium vapor pressure
- $P_0$ = saturation vapor pressure at the analysis temperature

A plot of $\dfrac{P/P_0}{W(1 - P/P_0)}$ versus $P/P_0$ yields a **straight line** in the range $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.35$ from which $W_m$ and $C$ are obtained.

## 2.3 Kelvin Equation — Pore Size from Capillary Condensation

Mesopores fill by **capillary condensation** below $P_0$ because the curved meniscus depresses the equilibrium vapor pressure. The Kelvin equation relates pore radius to relative pressure:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_K \, R T}$$

Where:

- $\gamma$ = surface tension of the condensed liquid (N/m) — for N₂ at 77 K: $\gamma = 8.85 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m
- $V_m$ = molar volume of the condensate (m³/mol) — for N₂: $V_m = 34.7 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/mol
- $\theta$ = contact angle (assumed $\approx 0°$ for N₂ on most surfaces, so $\cos\theta \approx 1$)
- $r_K$ = Kelvin radius of the pore (m)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/(mol·K)**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

## 2.4 Washburn Equation — Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry (MIP)

For non-wetting liquids (mercury, $\theta > 90°$), pressure must be applied to intrude the pore. The Washburn equation gives the pore radius:

$$r = -\frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{P}$$

The negative sign arises because $\cos\theta < 0$ for mercury ($\theta \approx 140°$); hence $r > 0$ requires $P > 0$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table** — all symbols and units preserved for board examination.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Key Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BET Linear Form | $\dfrac{x}{(1-x)W} = \dfrac{1}{W_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{W_m C} x$ | $x = P/P_0$ | Specific surface area |
| 2 | BET Monolayer Surface Area | $S_m = \dfrac{W_m \, N_A \, a_m}{M_{ads}}$ | $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol | Convert $W_m$ to area |
| 3 | BET Constant | $C = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{(E_1 - E_L)}{RT}\right]$ | $E_1, E_L$ = heats (J/mol) | Indicator of affinity |
| 4 | Kelvin Equation | $\ln(P/P_0) = -2\gamma V_m \cos\theta / (r_K R T)$ | $r_K$ = Kelvin radius | Mesopore size |
| 5 | Washburn (MIP) | $r = -2\gamma \cos\theta / P$ | $P$ = intrusion pressure | Macropore/throat size |
| 6 | t-plot / αs-method | $V_{ads} = f(t)$ statistical thickness | $t$ = layer thickness | Micro + external area split |
| 7 | BJH (cumulative) | $\Delta V_n = f(r_{p,n})$ Barrett–Joyner–Halenda | Pore volume distribution | Mesopore PSD |
| 8 | Total Pore Volume | $V_p = 1/\rho_b - 1/\rho_s$ | $\rho_b, \rho_s$ = bulk, skeletal | Geometric porosity |
| 9 | Porosity | $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_s$ | dimensionless | Volume fraction of voids |
| 10 | Cross-sectional N₂ area | $a_m = 0.162 \text{ nm}^2 = 16.2 \times 10^{-20} \text{ m}^2$ | constant | Standard probe value |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** The standard molecular cross-section of N₂ used universally is **$a_m = 0.162$ nm²**, derived assuming a close-packed liquid monolayer of spherical molecules. Failure to state this constant in a calculation typically costs 1 mark in KTU valuation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **spray-drying of milk powder**, BET surface area is a critical release specification. A powder with $S_m > 0.5$ m²/g tends to entrap more air, oxidize faster, and dissolve poorly. In **freeze-dried instant coffee**, $S_m$ of 250–500 m²/g is the target — high enough for rapid dissolution, low enough to prevent caking.

In **porous snack foods (extrudates, puffed cereals)**, pore surface area is correlated with **crispness acoustic descriptors** and oil uptake during frying. Lower pore surface area at fixed porosity implies larger pores, which absorb less oil per unit volume — a key industrial quality lever.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the BET Equation (Kinetic–Statistical Route)

We start with the BET assumption: at relative pressure $x = P/P_0$, a fraction $\theta_0$ of the surface is bare, $\theta_1$ is covered by one molecular layer, $\theta_2$ by two, and so on. Summing all coverages:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \theta_i = 1$$

Equilibrium between the bare surface and the first layer:

$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 \cdot C \cdot x$$

Equilibrium between successive layers (all equal to the liquefied state):

$$\theta_i = \theta_{i-1} \cdot x = \theta_0 \cdot (C x) \cdot x^{i-1} \quad (i \geq 1)$$

Summing the geometric series:

$$1 = \theta_0 + \theta_0 C x \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} x^{i} = \theta_0 \left[1 + \frac{C x}{1 - x}\right] = \theta_0 \left[\frac{1 + (C-1) x}{1 - x}\right]$$

The total volume of gas adsorbed is proportional to the statistical average number of layers $\bar{n}$:

$$\bar{n} = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} i \theta_i = \frac{\theta_0 C x}{(1-x)^2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (C-1)x / (1-x)} = \frac{C x}{(1-x)[1 + (C-1)x]}$$

The total mass adsorbed $W$ is $W = W_m \bar{n}$. Substituting and rearranging:

$$\frac{W}{W_m} = \frac{C x}{(1-x)[1 + (C-1)x]}$$

Inverting and separating to yield the classic linear BET plot:

$$\frac{x}{W(1-x)} = \frac{1}{W_m C} + \frac{C-1}{W_m C} \cdot x$$

> **Validity Window:** $0.05 \leq x \leq 0.35$. Below 0.05, surface heterogeneity dominates; above 0.35, capillary condensation invalidates the assumption of independent layer build-up.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Specific Surface Area from BET

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Style Problem (Worked Model):** A 1.05 g sample of freeze-dried mango powder is subjected to N₂ adsorption at 77 K. The following equilibrium data are recorded:

| $P/P_0$ | Mass adsorbed $W$ (g N₂ / g solid) |
|---|---|
| 0.05 | 0.0186 |
| 0.10 | 0.0218 |
| 0.15 | 0.0242 |
| 0.20 | 0.0261 |
| 0.25 | 0.0278 |
| 0.30 | 0.0294 |

**Required:** Determine the BET monolayer capacity $W_m$, the BET constant $C$, and the specific surface area $S_m$.

### Step 1 — Construct the BET transformed variables

For each datum, compute $Y = \dfrac{x}{W(1-x)}$ and $X = x$:

| $x$ | $W$ (g/g) | $1-x$ | $W(1-x)$ | $x/[W(1-x)]$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.05 | 0.0186 | 0.95 | 0.01767 | 2.829 |
| 0.10 | 0.0218 | 0.90 | 0.01962 | 5.097 |
| 0.15 | 0.0242 | 0.85 | 0.02057 | 7.292 |
| 0.20 | 0.0261 | 0.80 | 0.02088 | 9.579 |
| 0.25 | 0.0278 | 0.75 | 0.02085 | 11.990 |
| 0.30 | 0.0294 | 0.70 | 0.02058 | 14.577 |

### Step 2 — Linear Regression

Let $Y = a + bX$ where $a = 1/(W_m C)$ and $b = (C-1)/(W_m C)$.

Using least-squares fit on the six points above, the slope and intercept are obtained (showing one intermediate point for illustration):

$$a = 1.208 \text{ g}^{-1} \quad ; \quad b = 44.46 \text{ g}^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $W_m$ and $C$

Adding slope and intercept:

$$a + b = \frac{1}{W_m C} + \frac{C-1}{W_m C} = \frac{1}{W_m}$$

$$W_m = \frac{1}{a + b} = \frac{1}{1.208 + 44.46} = \frac{1}{45.668} = 0.02190 \text{ g N}_2 / \text{ g solid}$$

Solving for $C$:

$$C = \frac{b}{a} + 1 = \frac{44.46}{1.208} + 1 = 36.81 + 1 = 37.81$$

> **Physical interpretation:** $C \approx 38$ indicates a strongly attractive surface (typical of polar, hydroxyl-rich food matrices like sugars and starches). High $C$ values (> 100) signal strong adsorbate–adsorbent affinity, often associated with microporous materials.

### Step 4 — Convert $W_m$ to Specific Surface Area

The specific surface area is given by:

$$S_m = \frac{W_m \, N_A \, a_m}{M_{N_2}}$$

Substituting the standard values $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol, $a_m = 16.2 \times 10^{-20}$ m², $M_{N_2} = 28.014$ g/mol, and $W_m = 0.02190$ g/g:

$$S_m = \frac{0.02190 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20}}{28.014}$$

$$S_m = \frac{0.02190 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.62 \times 10^{-19}}{28.014}$$

$$S_m = \frac{2.136 \times 10^{3}}{28.014} = 76.27 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

> **Engineering Result:** The freeze-dried mango powder exhibits a specific pore surface area of **$S_m \approx 76.3$ m²/g**, well within the range typical of high-quality fruit powders (50–150 m²/g). This justifies its rapid rehydration behaviour.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Matrix — Kelvin Equation Application

> **Problem:** Mesopore capillary condensation of N₂ at 77 K ($T = 77.36$ K) occurs in a porous biscuit at $P/P_0 = 0.80$. The surface tension of liquid N₂ is $\gamma = 8.85 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m and $V_m = 34.7 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/mol. Calculate the Kelvin pore radius (assume $\cos\theta = 1$).

Rearranging the Kelvin equation for $r_K$:

$$r_K = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{R T \ln(P/P_0)}$$

Substituting:

$$r_K = -\frac{2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 34.7 \times 10^{-6} \times 1}{8.314 \times 77.36 \times \ln(0.80)}$$

Computing numerator and denominator separately:

$$\text{Numerator} = 6.143 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J/m}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 8.314 \times 77.36 \times (-0.2231) = -143.49 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$r_K = -\frac{6.143 \times 10^{-7}}{-143.49} = 4.28 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 4.28 \text{ nm}$$

> **Result:** A relative pressure of 0.80 corresponds to a Kelvin pore radius of **4.28 nm**, which is firmly in the **mesopore** regime (2–50 nm) by IUPAC classification.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Measurement Techniques

| Technique | Principle | Pore Range | Sample Form | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BET (N₂ at 77 K) | Multi-layer physisorption | 0.4–50 nm (micro + meso) | Powder, dried, degassed | Inactive above 50 nm |
| CO₂ at 273 K | Fills narrow micropores | 0.3–1.5 nm | Fine powders | Limited to ultramicropores |
| Mercury Intrusion (MIP) | Non-wetting forced intrusion | 3.5 nm–500 µm | Rigid solids | High pressure may crush food |
| Image Analysis (SEM/TEM/µCT) | Direct pixel measurement | > 50 nm (macro) | Solid, polished | 2D projection bias |
| NMR / NMR Cryoporometry | Freezing-point depression | 1–1000 nm | Hydrated foods | Requires calibration |
| Thermoporometry (DSC) | Solid–liquid transition shift | 1–100 nm | Hydrated | Overlap with bound water |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pore Surface Area Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food Material] --> B[Pre-treatment]
    B --> B1[Drying to constant weight]
    B --> B2[Degassing under vacuum]
    B --> B3[Sieving to size fraction]
    B3 --> C{Choose Probe Gas}
    C --> C1[N2 at 77 K - standard]
    C --> C2[CO2 at 273 K - micropores]
    C --> C3[Kr at 77 K - low area]
    C1 --> D[Adsorption Isotherm Acquisition]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Plot W vs P/P0]
    E --> F[BET Linearization]
    F --> F1[Compute slope b and intercept a]
    F1 --> G[Calculate W from a and b]
    G --> H[Apply W to surface area formula]
    H --> H1[Use NA and am constants]
    H1 --> I[Specific Surface Area Sm]
    E --> J[Kelvin Equation Branch]
    J --> J1[Use capillary condensation region]
    J1 --> J2[Compute Kelvin radius rK]
    J2 --> K[Pore Size Distribution]
    I --> L[Quality Decision]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Acceptance Limits}
    M --> M1[Rehydration kinetics]
    M --> M2[Storage stability]
    M --> M3[Texture spec]
```

## 4.2 Pore Classification and Hysteresis Loop Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Food Porous Matrix] --> P1[IUPAC Pore Classes]
    P1 --> P2[Micropores w less than 2 nm]
    P1 --> P3[Mesopores 2 to 50 nm]
    P1 --> P4[Macropores w greater than 50 nm]
    P2 --> P2A[CO2 adsorption at 273 K]
    P2A --> P2B[DR or DA model]
    P2B --> P2C[Microporous volume and area]
    P3 --> P3A[N2 capillary condensation]
    P3A --> P3B[Kelvin equation analysis]
    P3B --> P3C[Mesopore PSD via BJH]
    P4 --> P4A[Mercury intrusion or image analysis]
    P4A --> P4B[Washburn equation]
    P4B --> P4C[Macropore volume and throats]
    P2C --> P5[Total Pore Surface Area Sm]
    P3C --> P5
    P4C --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Engineering Design]
    P6 --> P6A[Drying time prediction]
    P6 --> P6B[Rehydration ratio]
    P6 --> P6C[Oil uptake in frying]
```

## 4.3 Hysteresis Loop Reference (IUPAC Classification for Food Systems)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[IUPAC Hysteresis Loops] --> H1[Type H1 - Cylindrical uniform pores]
    H1 --> H1A[Example: Puffed rice extrudates]
    H --> H2[Type H2 - Ink-bottle pores]
    H2 --> H2A[Example: Freeze-dried fruits]
    H --> H3[Type H3 - Slit-shaped pores]
    H3 --> H3A[Example: Starch granules aggregates]
    H --> H4[Type H4 - Narrow slit micropores]
    H4 --> H4A[Example: Activated carbon food additives]
    H1A --> R[Engineering Implication]
    H2A --> R
    H3A --> R
    H4A --> R
    R --> R1[Interpret connectivity]
    R --> R2[Predict mass transport]
    R --> R3[Select drying protocol]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define pore surface area. State its units and explain briefly why it is a critical engineering property of food powders.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pore surface area is the total internal interfacial area per unit mass (or volume) of a porous solid that is accessible to an external fluid phase. The standard units are **m²/g** (specific) or **m²/m³** (volumetric).

For food powders, it is critical because it directly governs: (i) the rate of moisture sorption and desorption, (ii) the rehydration kinetics, (iii) the oxidative deterioration rate (more surface = more oxygen contact), and (iv) the dissolution rate in instant products. Hence, controlling pore surface area is essential for shelf-life and quality assurance in products such as instant coffee, milk powder, and freeze-dried fruits.

*(Allocation: Definition 1 m, Units 1 m, Engineering relevance 1 m.)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Differentiate between micropores, mesopores, and macropores as classified by IUPAC. Give one food-engineering example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Class | Width Range | Food Example |
|---|---|---|
| Micropores | $w < 2$ nm | Pore network in low-moisture amorphous sugar matrices |
| Mesopores | 2 nm $\leq w \leq$ 50 nm | Capillary network of freeze-dried coffee or fruits |
| Macropores | $w > 50$ nm | Cellular void structure of bread crumb or extruded snacks |

*(Allocation: Tabular differentiation 2 m, Example 1 m.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Missing the validity window** $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.35$ for BET linearization — 2-mark penalty
> - **Confusing Kelvin radius with true pore radius** — the Kelvin radius excludes the adsorbed layer thickness; full pore radius requires $r_p = r_K + t$ correction — 2-mark penalty
> - **Wrong units in final surface area** (e.g., reporting nm²/g instead of m²/g) — 1-mark penalty
> - **Forgetting Avogadro's number or the N₂ molecular area constant** $a_m = 0.162$ nm² — 1-mark penalty per constant omitted
> - **Not stating assumptions** of BET (heat of first layer, no lateral interaction) — 1-mark penalty

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat derivation, develop the BET linear equation for multilayer adsorption. Clearly state all assumptions, the validity window of relative pressure, and the physical meaning of the BET constant $C$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dehydrated food sample of mass 0.85 g was analysed by N₂ adsorption at 77 K. The BET linear fit yielded slope $b = 32.5$ g⁻¹ and intercept $a = 1.85$ g⁻¹. Calculate (i) monolayer capacity $W_m$, (ii) BET constant $C$, and (iii) specific surface area $S_m$. Use $a_m = 16.2 \times 10^{-20}$ m² and $M_{N_2} = 28.014$ g/mol.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Heat of adsorption of the first layer ($E_1$) is constant and distinct from that of subsequent layers.
2. Heat of adsorption of all layers above the first equals the heat of liquefaction ($E_L$).
3. No lateral interaction between adsorbed molecules in the same layer.
4. At equilibrium, the rate of condensation on a bare patch equals the rate of evaporation from a covered patch.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Start with surface coverages $\theta_0, \theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots$ and use the equilibrium conditions:

$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 \, C \, x, \quad \theta_i = \theta_0 \, C \, x^{i} \quad \text{for } i \geq 1$$

where $x = P/P_0$. Summing coverages to unity gives:

$$1 = \theta_0 \left[1 + \frac{C x}{1-x}\right] = \theta_0 \cdot \frac{1 + (C-1)x}{1-x}$$

The average number of adsorbed layers is:

$$\bar{n} = \frac{\sum i \theta_i}{\sum \theta_i} = \frac{C x}{(1-x)[1 + (C-1)x]}$$

Since $W = W_m \bar{n}$, the final linear form (after algebraic inversion) is:

$$\frac{x}{W(1-x)} = \frac{1}{W_m C} + \frac{C-1}{W_m C} \cdot x$$

**Validity window and physical meaning (1 mark):** The equation is linear in the range $0.05 \leq x \leq 0.35$. The constant $C = \exp[(E_1 - E_L)/RT]$ quantifies the net adsorbate–adsorbent interaction energy; higher $C$ indicates stronger surface affinity.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** From BET linear form, $a + b = 1/W_m$:

$$W_m = \frac{1}{a+b} = \frac{1}{1.85 + 32.5} = \frac{1}{34.35} = 0.02911 \text{ g/g}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The constant $C$:

$$C = \frac{b}{a} + 1 = \frac{32.5}{1.85} + 1 = 17.57 + 1 = 18.57$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Specific surface area:

$$S_m = \frac{W_m \, N_A \, a_m}{M_{N_2}} = \frac{0.02911 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20}}{28.014}$$

Numerator evaluation: $0.02911 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.753 \times 10^{22}$; multiplying by $1.62 \times 10^{-19}$ gives $2.840 \times 10^{3}$.

$$S_m = \frac{2.840 \times 10^{3}}{28.014} = 101.4 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

> **Final Answer:** $W_m = 0.0291$ g/g, $C = 18.57$, $S_m = 101.4$ m²/g. The high surface area is consistent with a porous dried food matrix, suitable for rapid rehydration.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Kelvin equation for capillary condensation in a cylindrical mesopore. State clearly how it enables the determination of pore size distribution in food materials. What is the limitation of the Kelvin equation in ultramicropores?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A porous biscuit exhibits capillary condensation of N₂ at 77 K ($T = 77.36$ K) at $P/P_0 = 0.75$. Given $\gamma = 8.85 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m, $V_m = 34.7 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), and $\cos\theta = 1$, calculate:
   (i) The Kelvin pore radius $r_K$.
   (ii) The corrected true pore radius $r_p$ if the statistical thickness of the adsorbed layer at this relative pressure is $t = 0.6$ nm.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** Consider a cylindrical pore of radius $r_K$ filled with condensed liquid forming a hemispherical meniscus of radius equal to $r_K/\cos\theta$. The mechanical equilibrium between the liquid and vapour phases is governed by the Young–Laplace relation, which states that the vapour pressure over a curved interface exceeds that over a flat surface by:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = \frac{2 \gamma V_m}{r_K R T} \cdot \cos\theta$$

For a concave meniscus (capillary condensation), the vapour pressure is depressed, so $\cos\theta > 0$ and the right-hand side carries a negative sign in the standard textbook formulation:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_K R T}$$

**Application (2 marks):** The equation is inverted to give $r_K$ at each $P/P_0$ point along the desorption branch of the hysteresis loop. The pore size distribution (PSD) is constructed by plotting the cumulative pore volume versus $r_K$.

**Limitation (1 mark):** The Kelvin equation fails for ultramicropores ($w < 2$ nm) because the macroscopic concept of surface tension $\gamma$ loses meaning at the molecular scale, and the assumption of a continuous meniscus breaks down.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**(i) Kelvin radius (3 marks):**

$$r_K = -\frac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{R T \ln(P/P_0)}$$

Substituting:

$$r_K = -\frac{2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 34.7 \times 10^{-6} \times 1}{8.314 \times 77.36 \times \ln(0.75)}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 34.7 \times 10^{-6} = 6.143 \times 10^{-7}$ J/m.

Denominator: $8.314 \times 77.36 \times \ln(0.75) = 643.0 \times (-0.2877) = -185.0$ J/mol.

$$r_K = -\frac{6.143 \times 10^{-7}}{-185.0} = 3.32 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 3.32 \text{ nm}$$

**(ii) True pore radius (4 marks):**

The Kelvin equation describes the radius of the meniscus, which is **less** than the true pore radius because the pore wall is already lined by an adsorbed film of statistical thickness $t$. The correction is:

$$r_p = r_K + t$$

Substituting $r_K = 3.32$ nm and $t = 0.6$ nm:

$$r_p = 3.32 + 0.6 = 3.92 \text{ nm}$$

> **Final Answer:** $r_K = 3.32$ nm, $r_p = 3.92$ nm. The pore lies in the mesopore range (2–50 nm) per IUPAC classification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print & Pin**

- **Definition:** Pore surface area is the internal solid–fluid interfacial area per unit mass (m²/g) accessible to a probe gas.
- **IUPAC Pore Classes:** Micropores (< 2 nm), Mesopores (2–50 nm), Macropores (> 50 nm).
- **BET Validity Window:** $0.05 \leq P/P_0 \leq 0.35$ only — never extrapolate.
- **Key BET Equation:** $\dfrac{x}{W(1-x)} = \dfrac{1}{W_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{W_m C} x$ — plot the LHS vs $x$ for linear fit.
- **Monolayer to Area Formula:** $S_m = W_m N_A a_m / M_{ads}$ — memorize $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol and $a_m = 0.162$ nm² for N₂.
- **Slope + Intercept Identity:** $a + b = 1/W_m$ — this is the most-trusted trick for BET in KTU valuation.
- **BET Constant Meaning:** $C \propto \exp[(E_1 - E_L)/RT]$ — high $C$ implies strong surface affinity.
- **Kelvin Equation:** $\ln(P/P_0) = -2\gamma V_m \cos\theta / (r_K R T)$ — applies to mesopore capillary condensation.
- **True vs Kelvin Radius:** Always add statistical thickness $t$: $r_p = r_K + t$.
- **Washburn for Macropores:** $r = -2\gamma \cos\theta / P$ — for mercury (non-wetting) intrusion.
- **Porosity Relation:** $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_s$; $V_p = 1/\rho_b - 1/\rho_s$.
- **Food Examples:** Freeze-dried coffee (250–500 m²/g), instant milk powder (0.5–2 m²/g), bread crumb (0.1–0.5 m²/g), extruded snacks (0.05–0.3 m²/g).
- **Engineering Linkage:** Pore surface area governs rehydration rate, oil uptake, oxidation rate, crispness, and BET monolayer moisture content $M_0$.
- **Common Pitfall:** Never confuse **pore surface area** with **specific surface area of a powder** (external geometric area). Pore surface area refers specifically to internal void surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
