topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT526 - ENGINEERING PROPERTIES OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laws of friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Engineering Properties of Food Materials**, **friction** is defined as the resistive force that opposes the relative motion (or tendency of motion) between two surfaces in contact. For food materials, these surfaces are typically:

1. **Food material ↔ Food material** (inter-particle friction in granular foods)
2. **Food material ↔ Processing equipment surface** (e.g., metal, polymer, wood)

The **Laws of Friction** are the empirical rules that govern this resistive force. They are foundational for designing conveyors, hoppers, silos, mixers, extruders, and packaging machinery used throughout the food processing industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> *Friction is the tangential resistance offered by one surface to the relative motion of another surface in contact with it. For food materials, the coefficient of friction is a critical engineering property used to predict flow behavior, bin wall loads, and conveying energy requirements.*

## 1.2 The Two Principal Types of Friction

| Type | Symbol | Definition | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Static Friction** | $F_s$ or $F_{max}$ | The frictional force that must be overcome to *initiate* motion from rest. | The "stickiness" before sliding begins. |
| **Kinetic (Dynamic) Friction** | $F_k$ | The frictional force acting *during* relative sliding motion. | The resistance felt while surfaces are sliding. |

> [!NOTE]
> For food materials, $\mu_s > \mu_k$ almost universally. This is why it takes more force to start moving a filled grain hopper than to keep it moving.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition Engine

Imagine you are pushing a heavy sack of **rice** across a steel tabletop in a rice mill.

- **Before it moves:** You push harder and harder, and the sack stubbornly stays still. The friction force is exactly equal to your push (until you exceed the maximum static friction). The rice grains are microscopically "locked" with the steel.
- **At the moment it breaks free:** There is a tiny jerk — this is the transition from static to kinetic friction. The coefficient drops slightly.
- **While sliding:** The force required is noticeably less and remains nearly constant. The rice grains are now *plowing* through the surface, not locked to it.

**Real-world takeaway:** The "Law of Limiting Friction" tells us there is a *maximum* static friction. Exceed it, and motion begins. This principle is exactly what determines the **angle of slide** of food grains on chutes and the **hopper wall angle** in silo design.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$):** A dimensionless scalar quantity (no units).
- **Standard reference value for steel-on-steel:** $\mu \approx 0.5$ to $0.8$ (dry).
- **Typical food-on-steel range:** $\mu \approx 0.2$ to $0.6$ depending on moisture content.
- **Standard ambient conditions for measurement:** $T = 25^\circ C \pm 2^\circ C$, $\text{RH} = 60\% \pm 5\%$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** When a question asks for the *physical meaning* of friction laws in food engineering, always link your answer to **practical equipment design** (silos, conveyors, chutes). Generic physics answers lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Classical Laws of Friction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These four laws are the absolute core of this KTU module. Memorize them verbatim, with examples.**

### **Law 1 — Proportionality to Normal Reaction (Limiting Friction Law)**

The limiting (maximum static) frictional force is **directly proportional** to the normal reaction between the two surfaces.

$$F_{max} = \mu_s \cdot N$$

where:
- $F_{max}$ = maximum static friction force (N)
- $\mu_s$ = coefficient of static friction (dimensionless)
- $N$ = normal reaction force (N)

### **Law 2 — Independence from Apparent Area of Contact**

The frictional force is **independent of the apparent (geometric) area of contact**, provided the normal load remains constant.

- *Food-engineering implication:* A flat wheat grain and an upright wheat grain on the same chute will require the *same* force to slide — surprising but true.

### **Law 3 — Dependence on the Nature of the Surfaces**

The coefficient of friction depends on the **material pair** (e.g., wheat-on-steel vs. wheat-on-wood) and on the **surface condition** (wet, dry, polished, rough).

### **Law 4 — Independence from Relative Velocity (Classical Form)**

For moderate ranges of sliding speed, kinetic friction is **independent of the relative velocity** between the surfaces. (Note: In food materials, this is an approximation; at high speeds, $\mu_k$ can vary.)

## 2.2 Coefficient of Friction: Static vs. Kinetic

For a food material resting on an inclined plane that is gradually tilted:

- The material begins to slide when the incline angle $\theta$ reaches the **angle of friction** $\phi$.
- At this critical angle:

$$\mu_s = \tan(\phi)$$

$$F_{max} = W \sin(\phi) = \mu_s \cdot W \cos(\phi)$$

where $W$ is the weight of the food material.

## 2.3 Angle of Friction vs. Angle of Repose (KTU Favorite!)

This is a **classic KTU differentiation point**:

| Property | Definition | When Measured |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Angle of Friction ($\phi$)** | The angle of an inclined plane at which a food material begins to slide on a *given surface*. | Material is in contact with a foreign surface (steel, wood, plastic). |
| **Angle of Repose ($\alpha$)** | The natural angle formed when a granular food is poured onto a *horizontal flat surface* and forms a heap. | Material is in contact with *itself* (inter-particle). |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $\alpha = \phi$. They are **NOT equal** in general. $\phi$ is for material-on-surface; $\alpha$ is for material-on-itself. They are equal only in very special cases (smooth, spherical, mono-sized particles).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as your **last-minute revision sheet** before the KTU exam.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F = \mu N$ | Fundamental friction equation | $F$ in N, $N$ in N, $\mu$ dimensionless |
| 2 | $\mu_s = \tan(\phi)$ | Static coefficient from angle of friction | $\phi$ in degrees or radians |
| 3 | $F_{max} = W \sin(\phi)$ | Max friction on inclined plane | $W$ = weight in N |
| 4 | $\mu_k = F_k / N$ | Kinetic coefficient definition | Measured during sliding |
| 5 | $F_{silo} = \mu \cdot N_{wall}$ | Wall friction in silos/bins | Critical for hopper design |
| 6 | $\tau = \mu \sigma_n$ | Shear stress form (soil/food mechanics) | $\sigma_n$ = normal stress (Pa) |
| 7 | $\phi = \alpha$ (approx.) | Empirical relation for free-flowing grains | Only valid for specific cases |
| 8 | $P_{conveyor} = \mu_s W g \cos\beta + W g \sin\beta$ | Power to pull material up inclined belt | $\beta$ = conveyor incline angle |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Silo & Hopper Design:** Wall friction determines the **hopper angle** for gravity discharge. If $\mu$ is too high, food will not flow — leading to **arching** or **ratholing**.
2. **Conveyor Belt Sizing:** The driving motor must overcome $\mu \cdot W$ (friction component) on horizontal belts, plus $W \sin\beta$ (gravity component) on inclined belts.
3. **Packaging Machinery:** Friction between food (e.g., chocolate bars) and chute walls must be controlled for proper singulation and orientation.
4. **Extrusion & Mixing:** Internal friction between food particles affects the **specific mechanical energy (SME)** input.
5. **Pneumatic Conveying:** Air-grain friction determines the **saltation velocity** and pressure drop in pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Coefficient of Friction from Inclined Plane Test

This is the **most important derivation** in this KTU module. It appears almost every year.

### Setup
Consider a food material (mass $m$) resting on an inclined plane. The plane is tilted gradually until the material just begins to slide. At this critical angle $\phi$:

- Weight component *along* the plane (pulling it down): $W \sin(\phi)$
- Weight component *perpendicular* to the plane (pressing on surface): $W \cos(\phi)$
- Normal reaction: $N = W \cos(\phi)$
- Limiting friction force: $F_{max} = \mu_s N$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At the verge of sliding:} \quad & F_{max} = W \sin(\phi) \quad \text{(force balance along plane)} \\
\text{Substituting } F_{max} = \mu_s N: \quad & \mu_s N = W \sin(\phi) \\
\text{Substituting } N = W \cos(\phi): \quad & \mu_s (W \cos(\phi)) = W \sin(\phi) \\
\text{Dividing both sides by } W \cos(\phi): \quad & \mu_s = \frac{W \sin(\phi)}{W \cos(\phi)} \\
\text{Simplifying:} \quad & \mu_s = \frac{\sin(\phi)}{\cos(\phi)} \\
\text{Final result:} \quad & \boxed{\mu_s = \tan(\phi)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The tangent of the angle at which a food material *just* begins to slide on a given surface *equals* the static coefficient of friction for that material-surface pair.

## 3.2 Derivation: Force to Pull Food on Horizontal Conveyor

A common KTU numerical problem.

**Given:** A conveyor belt carries a food mass $m$ horizontally. Coefficient of friction $\mu_s$.

**Step 1 — Identify forces on the food:**
- Weight: $W = mg$ (downward)
- Normal reaction: $N = mg$ (upward, since no vertical acceleration)
- Pulling force: $P$ (horizontal, direction of motion)

**Step 2 — Apply force balance in horizontal direction (just about to move):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F_{max} \\
P &= \mu_s N \\
P &= \mu_s (mg) \\
\boxed{P &= \mu_s \cdot m \cdot g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Power required (if belt moves at velocity $v$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{power} &= P \cdot v \\
\boxed{P_{power} &= \mu_s \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Moisture Content on $\mu$

KTU examiners frequently test how **moisture content (MC)** alters friction in food materials. Below is the standard trend matrix:

| Food Material | Surface | MC = 10% | MC = 20% | MC = 30% | Trend Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wheat | Mild steel | 0.30 | 0.36 | 0.45 | ↑ Water acts as binder, increases adhesion |
| Maize | Galvanized iron | 0.28 | 0.33 | 0.40 | ↑ Capillary forces rise with MC |
| Soybean | Plywood | 0.25 | 0.31 | 0.38 | ↑ Hygroscopic swelling |
| Rice (milled) | Stainless steel | 0.20 | 0.24 | 0.30 | ↑ Surface water film thickens |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Storage and processing equipment for high-moisture foods must be designed with **higher wall angles** to ensure flow.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A 5 kg bag of flour rests on a wooden chute inclined at $28^\circ$. The coefficient of static friction between flour bag and wood is $0.42$. Will the bag slide? If yes, what is the kinetic friction force once it begins moving ($\mu_k = 0.35$)?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Weight:} \quad & W = mg = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ N} \\
\text{Normal force:} \quad & N = W \cos(28^\circ) = 49.05 \times 0.8829 = 43.32 \text{ N} \\
\text{Gravity component along plane:} \quad & W \sin(28^\circ) = 49.05 \times 0.4695 = 23.03 \text{ N} \\
\text{Max static friction:} \quad & F_{max} = \mu_s N = 0.42 \times 43.32 = 18.19 \text{ N} \\
\text{Comparison:} \quad & W \sin(\phi) = 23.03 \text{ N} > F_{max} = 18.19 \text{ N} \\
\text{Conclusion:} \quad & \textbf{Yes, the bag will slide.} \\
\text{Kinetic friction during sliding:} \quad & F_k = \mu_k N = 0.35 \times 43.32 = \mathbf{15.16 \text{ N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Net accelerating force down the chute:**

$$
F_{net} = 23.03 - 15.16 = 7.87 \text{ N}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of Food Material on Inclined Plane (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block represents the **functional force-interaction architecture** of a food material on an inclined chute, the most commonly tested scenario in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Material of mass m] --> B[Weight W = mg acts vertically downward]
    A --> C[Normal reaction N acts perpendicular to chute surface]
    A --> D[Friction force F acts parallel to chute opposing motion]
    B --> E[Decompose weight into two components]
    E --> F[W sin phi parallel to plane drives motion downward]
    E --> G[W cos phi perpendicular to plane equals Normal N]
    C --> H{N equals W cos phi by equilibrium}
    D --> I{Limiting condition W sin phi equals mu N}
    I --> J[Sliding initiates when W sin phi exceeds mu N]
    J --> K[During motion: F k equals mu k times N]
    H --> L[Result: mu s equals tan phi]
    K --> M[Net acceleration: a equals g sin phi minus mu k g cos phi]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Friction Testing Apparatus for Food Materials

This diagram maps the **instrument architecture** used in food engineering labs to measure the coefficient of friction (e.g., the **tilted-plate method** or the **Jenike shear cell**).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sample Preparation Module
        S1[Conditioned food sample at known MC] --> S2[Placed on test surface plate]
    end
    subgraph Tilting and Measurement Module
        S2 --> T1[Manual or motorized tilting mechanism]
        T1 --> T2[Protractor / Inclinometer reads angle phi]
        T2 --> T3[Critical angle recorded at onset of sliding]
    end
    subgraph Computation Module
        T3 --> C1[Compute mu s equals tan phi]
        T3 --> C2[Compute mu k from velocity data if needed]
        C1 --> C3[Output: Coefficient of friction vs. MC plot]
        C2 --> C3
    end
    subgraph Engineering Application Module
        C3 --> AP1[Silo / hopper wall angle design]
        C3 --> AP2[Conveyor belt power sizing]
        C3 --> AP3[Chute angle for gravity flow]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `S1`, `T2`, `C3`, `AP1`, etc.) and prefixed with letters — no reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph` are used as standalone node IDs. Labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes, with no bold/italic markdown tags.

## 4.4 Schematic Description (For Mental Visualization)

If you were drawing the inclined-plane friction test on paper, you would depict:

- A **rectangular plane** tilted upward to the right at angle $\phi$.
- A **block** (representing a food mass) on the surface.
- An **arrow downward** from the block labeled $W = mg$.
- A **perpendicular arrow** into the surface labeled $N$.
- An **arrow up the slope** labeled $F$ (friction opposing the impending slide).
- A **dashed arrow down the slope** labeled $W \sin\phi$ (gravity component driving slide).
- The angle $\phi$ marked at the lower-left corner between the horizontal ground line and the inclined plane.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the laws of friction as applicable to food materials. Why is the coefficient of friction higher in moist foods compared to dry foods?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four laws of friction are:
1. The limiting frictional force is directly proportional to the normal reaction: $F = \mu N$.
2. The frictional force is independent of the apparent area of contact.
3. The coefficient of friction depends on the nature of the surfaces in contact.
4. The kinetic frictional force is independent of the relative velocity (for moderate speeds).

In moist foods, the higher moisture content creates a thin **liquid film** at the contact surface, increasing **adhesion** and **capillary forces** between the food and the equipment surface. Additionally, hygroscopic swelling of the food material increases the real area of microscopic contact. Both effects raise $\mu$ compared to dry foods.

> **[1 mark per law set + 1 mark for the moisture explanation = full 3 marks]**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the angle of friction and the angle of repose. Give one example of a food application where each is used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Angle of Friction ($\phi$) | Angle of Repose ($\alpha$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Angle of inclined plane at which food slides on a foreign surface. | Natural angle of a heap formed when food is poured on a horizontal surface. |
| **Contact** | Food ↔ Equipment surface | Food ↔ Food (particles) |
| **Application** | Designing the **chute angle** for grain handling. | Determining the **silo capacity** and flowability. |

> **[2 marks differentiation + 1 mark example = full 3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B questions carry 14 marks. The two alternatives below are independent, so a student can attempt **either** Option A **or** Option B.

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**a)** A 12 kg bag of sugar is placed on a stainless-steel conveyor belt inclined at $22^\circ$ to the horizontal. The static and kinetic coefficients of friction between the bag and the belt are $0.45$ and $0.38$ respectively. Determine:
   (i) Whether the bag will slide down the incline. (3 marks)
   (ii) The frictional force resisting motion once the bag starts sliding. (2 marks)
   (iii) The net force accelerating the bag down the slope. (2 marks)

**b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the inclined-plane method for determining the coefficient of friction of food grains. State its limitations. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Option A:**

**Part (a)(i) — Will the bag slide?**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= mg = 12 \times 9.81 = 117.72 \text{ N} \\
N &= W \cos(22^\circ) = 117.72 \times 0.9272 = 109.16 \text{ N} \\
F_{max} &= \mu_s N = 0.45 \times 109.16 = 49.12 \text{ N} \\
W \sin(22^\circ) &= 117.72 \times 0.3746 = 44.10 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $W \sin(22^\circ) = 44.10 \text{ N} < F_{max} = 49.12 \text{ N}$, the bag **will not slide**. It remains stationary. **(3 marks)**

**Part (a)(ii) — Frictional force during motion:**

Hypothetically, if sliding were to occur:

$$
F_k = \mu_k N = 0.38 \times 109.16 = 41.48 \text{ N}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Part (a)(iii) — Net accelerating force:**

For this question, the bag does not actually slide. But if it did:

$$
F_{net} = W \sin(22^\circ) - F_k = 44.10 - 41.48 = 2.62 \text{ N} \quad \text{(down the slope)}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — Inclined Plane Method:**

**Diagram (5 marks for neat diagram with proper labels):**

```
            /|
           / |  <-- Inclined plane at angle phi
          /  |
         / F |  <-- Friction force (up the slope)
        /----|
       /  W  |  <-- Food sample (block)
      /  sin |
     /  phi  |
    /________|
     W cos phi
   [horizontal surface]
```

**Procedure (1 mark):**
- Place the food material on the test surface (e.g., steel, wood, plastic).
- Gradually tilt the surface using a screw jack or motorized tilting table.
- Note the angle $\phi$ at which the material just begins to slide.
- Compute $\mu_s = \tan(\phi)$.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Only gives static $\mu$, not kinetic $\mu$.
- Surface finish of the inclined plane may differ from actual equipment.
- Difficult for sticky/pasty foods (dough, ketchup).
- Does not account for high-speed effects in real conveyors.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**a)** Define the coefficient of friction. A 200 kg wheat heap is stored in a galvanized-iron silo. The angle of friction between wheat and the silo wall is $24^\circ$. Calculate: (i) the coefficient of static friction, and (ii) the frictional force on a silo wall section supporting a normal load of 800 N. (7 marks)

**b)** Explain how moisture content, surface roughness, and sliding velocity influence the coefficient of friction of food materials. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Option B:**

**Part (a)(i) — Coefficient of static friction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_s &= \tan(\phi) \\
\mu_s &= \tan(24^\circ) \\
\boxed{\mu_s &= 0.4452}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Part (a)(ii) — Frictional force on silo wall:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \mu_s N \\
F &= 0.4452 \times 800 \\
\boxed{F &= 356.16 \text{ N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(4 marks)**

**Part (b) — Influencing Factors (7 marks):**

1. **Moisture Content (2 marks):** Higher MC increases $\mu$ because:
   - Water forms a thin adhesive film at the contact.
   - Capillary forces pull surfaces together.
   - Hygroscopic swelling increases real contact area.
   - Example: wheat $\mu$ on steel rises from $\sim 0.30$ at $10\%$ MC to $\sim 0.45$ at $25\%$ MC.

2. **Surface Roughness (2 marks):** Rough surfaces mechanically interlock with food particles, increasing $\mu$. Smooth, polished surfaces (e.g., stainless steel 304) give lower $\mu$ than rough mild steel. However, if the surface is *too* smooth, adhesion from water films can increase $\mu$ (a minimum in the $\mu$ vs. roughness curve).

3. **Sliding Velocity (2 marks):**
   - At low velocities, $\mu$ is approximately constant (classical Law 4).
   - At high velocities, frictional heating can vaporize surface water, slightly *reducing* $\mu$.
   - For sticky foods, velocity may *increase* $\mu$ due to viscous resistance.
   - Practical impact: conveyor belt speed must be chosen with awareness of $\mu(v)$ behavior.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Hence, $\mu$ is not a true material constant but a function of MC, surface pair, and velocity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\phi$** in numerical problems — always identify *what* is sliding on *what*.
> 2. **Forgetting units of angle** in $\tan(\phi)$ — use degrees only if your calculator is in degree mode; mention this explicitly.
> 3. **Writing $F = \mu N$** without specifying *which* $\mu$ (static vs. kinetic).
> 4. **Forgetting to check sliding condition** ($W \sin \phi$ vs. $\mu N$) before computing kinetic friction.
> 5. **Not drawing the free-body diagram** in 7-mark sub-questions — examiners explicitly allocate 2-3 marks for the diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Friction opposes **impending or actual** relative motion between two surfaces.
- ✅ Four classical laws: (i) $F \propto N$, (ii) independent of apparent area, (iii) depends on surface pair, (iv) independent of velocity (approx.).
- ✅ The fundamental equation: $\boxed{F = \mu N}$.
- ✅ Static coefficient: $\mu_s = \tan(\phi)$, where $\phi$ is the angle of friction.
- ✅ Kinetic coefficient: $\mu_k < \mu_s$ for almost all food materials.
- ✅ **Angle of friction = material on foreign surface; Angle of repose = material on itself.** They are NOT the same.
- ✅ Moisture content, surface roughness, and velocity all affect $\mu$.
- ✅ Inclined-plane test is the most common lab method; Jenike shear cell is used for powders.
- ✅ Applications: silos, conveyors, hoppers, chutes, packaging, extrusion.
- ✅ Standard units: $F$ in N, $N$ in N, $\mu$ dimensionless, angles in degrees or radians.
- ✅ Always identify **static vs. kinetic** friction in numerical problems.
- ✅ Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of load and properties of contacting bodies, effect of sliding velocity and contact surface temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT526 - ENGINEERING PROPERTIES OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of load and properties of contacting bodies, effect of sliding velocity and contact surface temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Friction in the Context of Food Materials?

**Friction** is the tangential resistance developed at the interface between two contacting bodies when one slides, or tends to slide, relative to the other. In food engineering, friction governs the flow of bulk solids in hoppers, the motion of food pieces on conveyors and chutes, the mixing torque in blenders, the extrusion behaviour in screw extruders, and the cutting resistance during size reduction.

For food materials, friction is classified into two broad engineering categories:

- **External (Wall) Friction** — Resistance between the food material and the surface it contacts (e.g., wheat grain on a galvanized steel chute).
- **Internal Friction** — Resistance between adjacent particles within the bulk food mass (e.g., flour particles sliding past each other in a silo).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Friction in food materials is described using the **Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion**, $\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$, where $c$ is the cohesion (Pa), $\sigma$ is the normal stress (Pa), and $\phi$ is the angle of internal friction (°). The wall friction is given by $\tau_w = \mu_w \sigma_n$ where $\mu_w = \tan\phi_w$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sliding Sacks" Picture

Imagine pulling a gunny sack of rice across a cemented floor. The harder you press it down (more load), the more it resists sliding. Now replace the sack with a sack of *oil-coated soybean* — it slides much more easily because the surface is "slimy." If you now drag the same rice sack twice as fast, the floor will feel warmer under it. **Load, contacting-surface properties, sliding velocity, and the temperature of the contact patch — these are the four master variables that dictate friction.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants used throughout this module:**
> - Mechanical equivalent of heat, $J = 4.186 \,\text{J/cal}$ (**4.186 J/cal**)
> - Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \,\text{m/s}^2$
> - Standard unit of friction coefficient $\mu$ is **dimensionless**.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Food Process Engineering

| Engineering Situation | Role of Friction |
|---|---|
| Wheat flow in storage silo | Wall friction causes "arching" and "rat-holing" |
| Flour conveying on a chute | Friction determines the minimum chute angle (angle of repose) |
| Extrusion of pasta / snack pellets | Screw-barrel friction generates heat and back-pressure |
| Chocolate tempering on a cooled belt | Temperature-dependent friction controls film thickness |
| Grain harvesting and threshing | Internal friction affects separation efficiency |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Amontons' Two Laws of Dry Friction (The Starting Point)

When a food body of weight $W$ rests on a horizontal surface and a horizontal force $F$ is applied:

**Law 1:** The friction force $F_f$ is **proportional to the normal load $N$**, independent of the apparent contact area.

**Law 2:** The friction force is **independent of the sliding velocity** (this is an idealisation; real food materials show velocity dependence — covered in §2.4).

### 2.2 Effect of Applied Load

For most food materials, an increase in normal load $N$ produces an increase in friction force $F_f$ in roughly linear fashion. However, the *coefficient of friction* $\mu$ itself may either increase, decrease, or stay constant depending on the deformation behaviour of the food:

- **Elastic foods** (e.g., apple skin, green peas) → $\mu$ slightly **decreases** with load because true contact area saturates.
- **Plastic / viscoelastic foods** (e.g., butter, dough, cheese) → $\mu$ **decreases sharply** because the contact area grows faster than the load (Bowden–Tabor junction growth).
- **Hard, brittle foods** (e.g., corn kernels, roasted nuts) → $\mu$ is **nearly constant** with load (Amontons' regime).

### 2.3 Effect of Properties of the Contacting Bodies

Three material-pair properties dominate the friction coefficient:

1. **Surface roughness ($R_a$, $R_z$)** — Greater roughness on either contacting body raises the mechanical interlocking component, especially for granular foods. Very smooth surfaces can paradoxically *raise* friction due to higher adhesion.
2. **Hardness of the food and the wall** — Soft foods (banana, fresh dough) deform plastically and create large real contact area → high $\mu$. Hard foods (almond, frozen peas) deform elastically → lower $\mu$.
3. **Adhesion and surface energy** — Foods rich in free fat or free water (oily peanuts, wet grains) form adhesive junctions with the wall; the friction coefficient can rise to 0.6–0.9.

### 2.4 Effect of Sliding Velocity

The classical **Stribeck curve** describes friction as a function of the Hersey number ($\eta v / P$):

- **Boundary lubrication regime** (low $v$ / high $P$): $\mu$ is high, governed by surface asperity contacts.
- **Mixed lubrication regime**: $\mu$ falls as the lubricant film begins to separate asperities.
- **Hydrodynamic regime** (high $v$ / low $P$): $\mu$ rises again because viscous drag in the film dominates.

For *unlubricated food–wall contact*, the simpler behaviour is:

> **As sliding velocity increases, the frictional heat input rises, the contact zone temperature rises, and the apparent friction coefficient typically decreases (for thermoplastic foods) or increases (for dried, brittle foods).**

### 2.5 Effect of Contact Surface Temperature

Frictional power dissipation at the sliding interface is

$$P_{\text{fric}} = F_f \cdot v = \mu \, N \, v$$

All of this mechanical power (assuming negligible conduction into the surroundings) appears as heat. The contact patch temperature $T_c$ rises above the bulk temperature $T_b$ by the **flash temperature** $\Delta T_f$. The combined effect of:

- **Softening / melting of fat or sugar** (chocolate, butter, ice) → $\mu$ drops.
- **Dehydration / glass transition of surface layer** (biscuit, dried pasta) → $\mu$ rises.
- **Starch gelatinisation** (extrusion) → $\mu$ rises then collapses.
- **Protein denaturation** (meat dough) → $\mu$ falls.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Symbol | Equation | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_f$ | $F_f = \mu N$ | Amontons' friction law | N |
| $F_f$ | $F_f = \mu_s N$ (static) / $F_f = \mu_k N$ (kinetic) | Static and kinetic friction | N |
| $\tau$ | $\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$ | Mohr–Coulomb internal friction | Pa |
| $\tau_w$ | $\tau_w = \mu_w \sigma_n = \sigma_n \tan\phi_w$ | Wall friction at contact | Pa |
| $Q$ | $Q = F_f \cdot v = \mu N v$ | Frictional heat power | W |
| $Q$ | $Q = \mu W v \, / \, J$ | Frictional heat in calories per second | cal/s |
| $\Delta T_f$ | $\Delta T_f = \mu P v d \, / \, (2 k J)$ | Blok's flash temperature rise | K or °C |
| $T_c$ | $T_c = T_b + \Delta T_f$ | Contact patch temperature | K or °C |
| $\Delta T_b$ | $\Delta T_b = Q t \, / \, (m c_p)$ | Bulk temperature rise over time $t$ | K or °C |
| $v_{cr}$ | $v_{cr} = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Critical velocity on a chute (no friction) | m/s |
| $\theta_{min}$ | $\theta_{min} = \arctan\mu$ | Minimum chute inclination for sliding | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used:** $W$ = load (N or kg·m/s²), $v$ = sliding velocity (m/s), $J$ = mechanical equivalent of heat (**4.186 J/cal**), $P$ = contact pressure (Pa), $d$ = contact diameter (m), $k$ = thermal conductivity of the contact (W/m·K), $m$ = mass (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K).

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Hopper design (Jenike method):** Wall friction $\phi_w$ sets the minimum hopper angle; velocity and temperature data ensure no flow stoppages during discharge.
- **Extruder screw design:** Friction between dough and barrel generates the cooking temperature; engineers predict the melt-zone profile using the $\Delta T_f$ equation.
- **Belt conveyors for fruits:** Belt speed $v$ is limited by allowable bruising, which is a function of the contact-patch temperature and the resulting skin softening.
- **Cold-chain handling of frozen foods:** Friction at $-18\,°\text{C}$ is dominated by ice-bridging; thaw cycles (warming of contact patch) can cause sudden drops in $\mu$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Basic Friction Relationship

Consider a food block of mass $m$ resting on a horizontal plane. Let the plane be pulled horizontally with force $F$. Take the block as the free body.

**Free-body diagram of the block (horizontal direction):**
- Applied force: $F$ (to the right)
- Friction force: $F_f$ (to the left, opposing motion)

**Equilibrium in the vertical direction:**
$$N - mg = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad N = mg$$

**Equilibrium in the horizontal direction (at the threshold of motion):**
$$F = F_f$$

**Empirical observation (Amontons' First Law):**
$$F_f \propto N \quad\Rightarrow\quad F_f = k N$$

The constant of proportionality is defined as the **coefficient of friction** $\mu$:

$$F_f = \mu N$$

Combining with $N = mg$:

$$F_f = \mu m g$$

This is the **fundamental friction equation** that engineers use for food-on-wall contact problems.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Frictional Heat Power

When the food slides a distance $\mathrm{d}s$ in time $\mathrm{d}t$ under a constant friction force $F_f$:

- Work done against friction:
$$W_{\text{fric}} = F_f \cdot \mathrm{d}s$$

- Power dissipated (assuming all mechanical work converts to heat at the contact):
$$P_{\text{fric}} = \frac{\mathrm{d}W_{\text{fric}}}{\mathrm{d}t} = F_f \frac{\mathrm{d}s}{\mathrm{d}t} = F_f v$$

Substituting $F_f = \mu N$:

$$P_{\text{fric}} = \mu N v$$

In **calories per second** (often used in older food engineering texts):

$$Q = \frac{\mu N v}{J} = \frac{\mu W v}{J}$$

where $W = N$ is the load in newtons and $J = 4.186\,\text{J/cal}$.

### 3.3 Derivation of Blok's Flash Temperature Equation

This is a **KTU-favourite derivation** and frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Assumptions of Blok's model (1937):**
1. The contact is a circular patch of diameter $d$.
2. All frictional heat enters the moving (cooler) body — a conservative simplification.
3. The contact patch is small compared with the slider dimensions.
4. Steady-state conduction.

**Step 1 — Heat flux entering the moving body:**
The frictional power generated over the contact area $A_c$ is $P_{\text{fric}} = \mu P A_c v$, where $P$ is the nominal contact pressure. The heat flux per unit area is

$$q = \frac{P_{\text{fric}}}{A_c} = \mu P v$$

**Step 2 — Treat the heat source as a moving point on a semi-infinite solid.** Carslaw and Jaeger's solution for a moving point source of strength $Q$ (W) on a semi-infinite solid of thermal conductivity $k$ gives, at the source location:

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{Q}{2 \pi k r} \cdot \text{(geometric factor for moving source)}$$

For a circular source of radius $r = d/2$, the moving-source result simplifies to

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{\mu P v d}{2 k}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to calorie units** (when $P$ is in Pa, $v$ in m/s, $d$ in m, $k$ in W/m·K):

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T_f = \frac{\mu P v d}{2 k J}\;}$$

This is the **flash temperature rise** at the contact patch. Multiply by $J$ in denominator if you want the result in calories. The **contact temperature** is then

$$T_c = T_b + \Delta T_f = T_b + \frac{\mu P v d}{2 k J}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** A chocolate bar of mass $0.20\,\text{kg}$ slides down an inclined stainless-steel chute at $1.5\,\text{m/s}$. The normal pressure at the contact is $P = 25\,\text{kPa}$, the contact diameter is $d = 6\,\text{mm}$, and the thermal conductivity of the chocolate–steel interface is $k = 18\,\text{W/m·K}$. The bulk temperature of the chocolate is $T_b = 28\,°\text{C}$, and the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.32$. Find (i) the frictional heat power, and (ii) the flash temperature rise and the contact patch temperature. Take $J = 4.186\,\text{J/cal}$.

**Step (i) — Frictional heat power:**

$$P_{\text{fric}} = \mu N v$$

$$N = mg = 0.20 \times 9.81 = 1.962\,\text{N}$$

$$P_{\text{fric}} = 0.32 \times 1.962 \times 1.5 = 0.942\,\text{W}$$

**Step (ii) — Flash temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{\mu P v d}{2 k J}$$

Convert $P$ to Pa: $25\,\text{kPa} = 25\,000\,\text{Pa}$.

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{0.32 \times 25\,000 \times 1.5 \times 0.006}{2 \times 18 \times 4.186}$$

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{72.0}{150.696}$$

$$\Delta T_f \approx 0.478\,°\text{C}$$

**Contact patch temperature:**

$$T_c = 28 + 0.48 = 28.48\,°\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This small rise is sufficient to begin **cocoa-butter softening** in real chocolate, raising $\mu$ further — a *positive feedback* that KTU examiners love to test in follow-up questions.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Variable Dominates?

| Food material state | Dominant friction variable | Trend of $\mu$ with that variable |
|---|---|---|
| Dry granular (flour, sugar) | Load + roughness | $\mu$ roughly constant with load, rises with $R_a$ |
| Wet granular (washed vegetables) | Adhesion | $\mu$ rises sharply with moisture film thickness |
| Oily / fatty (peanut, cocoa liquor) | Temperature | $\mu$ falls steeply as $T_c$ approaches melting point |
| Frozen (peas, fish) | Temperature & velocity | $\mu$ falls as surface ice melts from frictional heat |
| Viscoelastic (dough, cheese) | Velocity | $\mu$ falls as $v$ increases (shear-thinning) |
| Hard & brittle (corn, almond) | Load | $\mu$ roughly constant; fragmentation may suddenly lower $\mu$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Friction System

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **interactions of the four master variables** that this topic addresses. It is rendered as a system architecture because physical sketches of contact patches do not translate well into Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LOAD N] --> F[Friction System]
    B[Material Properties - Roughness - Hardness - Adhesion] --> F
    C[Sliding Velocity v] --> F
    D[Contact Temperature Tc] --> F
    F --> G[Friction Force Ff = mu N]
    F --> H[Frictional Heat Power Q = mu N v]
    F --> I[Flash Temperature Rise delta Tf]
    G --> J[Sliding / No-Sliding Decision]
    H --> I
    I --> D
    D -.feedback.-> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Four *input* variables (Load, Material Properties, Sliding Velocity, Contact Temperature) flow into the central **Friction System**.
- Three *output* variables leave the system: the friction force $F_f$, the heat power $Q$, and the flash temperature rise $\Delta T_f$.
- The **feedback loop** from $\Delta T_f$ back into Contact Temperature $T_c$ is critical: in real food systems (chocolate, butter, ice), $\mu$ is itself a function of $T_c$, creating a non-linear coupled problem.

### 4.2 Process Flow — Determining the Operating Friction Coefficient

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify the food and the wall material] --> S2[Step 2: Measure normal load N and apparent contact area]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply controlled sliding velocity v]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Record tangential force F versus time]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute mu = F slash N at each v]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Plot mu versus v to obtain the Stribeck-type curve]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Read contact temperature Tc from thermocouple at the wall]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Compute delta Tf using Blok's equation]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Tabulate mu and Tc for design use]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Forces on a Food Block on an Inclined Chute

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mass[Food Block Mass m] --> Wt[Weight W = mg acting downward]
    Wt --> CompN[Normal Component N = mg cos theta acting perpendicular to chute]
    Wt --> CompT[Tangential Component W sin theta acting down the slope]
    FricF[Friction Force Ff = mu N acting up the slope]
    CompN --- Slide{Does it slide?}
    CompT --> Slide
    FricF --> Slide
    Slide -- W sin theta greater than Ff --> SlidesYES[Slides down]
    Slide -- W sin theta less than or equal to Ff --> StaysNO[Remains at rest]
```

**Engineering takeaway from the chute schematic:**

The condition for incipient sliding is $W \sin\theta \ge \mu W \cos\theta$, giving the **angle of slide**:

$$\theta_{slide} = \arctan\mu$$

If the food's $\mu$ changes with velocity or temperature (e.g., chocolate), then $\theta_{slide}$ changes during the slide — the block can *accelerate or decelerate* on a constant slope. This is the KTU 2024 favourite "stick-slip" trick question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Amontons' two laws of dry friction. Why does the second law often fail for food materials?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Law 1 (1 mark):** The friction force between two dry surfaces is directly proportional to the normal load pressing them together and is independent of the apparent area of contact.
  $$F_f = \mu N$$
- **Law 2 (1 mark):** The friction force is independent of the sliding velocity once motion has started.
- **Why it fails for foods (1 mark):** For viscoelastic and plastic foods (dough, butter, chocolate, fresh fruit), $\mu$ is a strong function of velocity and temperature because the real contact area, surface moisture and fat-layer behaviour all change during sliding. The second law therefore holds only approximately for hard, dry, brittle foods such as roasted nuts and dried pulses.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "flash temperature" in the context of food-wall sliding contact. Write Blok's equation for the same.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Flash temperature is the transient, localised temperature rise at the small spots of real contact between two sliding bodies, occurring above the bulk temperature of the bodies. It arises because almost all the frictional work is dissipated as heat in a tiny contact patch.
- **Blok's equation (1.5 marks):**
  $$\Delta T_f = \frac{\mu P v d}{2 k J}$$
  where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction, $P$ is the contact pressure (Pa), $v$ is the sliding velocity (m/s), $d$ is the contact diameter (m), $k$ is the thermal conductivity (W/m·K), and $J = 4.186\,\text{J/cal}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *Effect of Load and Contacting Bodies*

**(a) [7 marks]**
Derive the relationship between friction force, normal load and the coefficient of friction for a food block resting on a horizontal plane. With the help of suitable examples, discuss how the coefficient of friction of three different categories of food materials varies with the magnitude of the applied normal load.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let the food block of mass $m$ rest on a horizontal plane. From the vertical equilibrium,

$$N = mg$$

When a horizontal force $F$ is applied and the block is on the verge of sliding,

$$F = F_f$$

By Amontons' first law, the friction force is proportional to the normal load:

$$F_f \propto N \quad\Rightarrow\quad F_f = \mu N$$

Substituting $N = mg$:

$$\boxed{\,F_f = \mu m g\,}$$

**Variation with load for three categories (3 marks):**

1. **Hard, brittle foods (almond, dried corn kernel, roasted peanut):** The contact is essentially elastic; the real contact area grows proportionally with load. Hence $\mu$ remains nearly constant as $N$ increases — Amontons' behaviour is followed.
2. **Plastic / viscoelastic foods (butter, cheese, fresh dough):** The food deforms plastically; the real contact area grows faster than the load. The friction force grows more slowly than $N$, and $\mu$ **decreases** with increasing load.
3. **Granular, moisture-sensitive foods (wet wheat, oil-coated soybean):** At low loads only a few particles contact the wall. As load rises, more particles come into contact, raising adhesion. The friction force grows faster than $N$, and $\mu$ **increases** with load (until crushing begins).

---

**(b) [7 marks]**
A wheat grain of mass $50\,\text{mg}$ rests on a mild-steel surface. The static coefficient of friction is $\mu_s = 0.45$ and the kinetic coefficient is $\mu_k = 0.32$. The grain starts to slide when the surface is tilted to $26\,°$.
- (i) Verify the angle of slide from the static coefficient.
- (ii) Once sliding begins, calculate the kinetic friction force, the frictional heat power if the grain slides at $0.8\,\text{m/s}$, and the heat generated in calories over $3$ seconds.
- (Take $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$ and $J = 4.186\,\text{J/cal}$.)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Verification of angle of slide (2 marks):**

The theoretical angle of slide is

$$\theta_{slide} = \arctan\mu_s = \arctan(0.45)$$

$$\theta_{slide} = 24.23\,°$$

The observed angle of $26\,°$ is **higher** than $24.23\,°$. The grain therefore begins to slide only when the tangential component of gravity exceeds the maximum static friction — a safety margin of about $1.77\,°$ is observed. This discrepancy is typical for real food grains because of slight surface adhesion and micro-roughness not captured by the ideal $\tan^{-1}\mu$ formula.

**(ii) Kinetic friction force (2 marks):**

$$F_{f,k} = \mu_k m g = 0.32 \times (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times 9.81$$

$$F_{f,k} = 1.5696 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{N} \approx 0.157\,\text{mN}$$

**Frictional heat power (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{\text{fric}} = F_{f,k} \times v = 1.5696 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.8 = 1.256 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{W}$$

**Heat generated in 3 s, in calories (1.5 marks):**

$$Q = \frac{P_{\text{fric}} \cdot t}{J} = \frac{1.256 \times 10^{-4} \times 3}{4.186}$$

$$Q = 9.00 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cal}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to **convert mass from mg to kg** before computing $mg$. Marks are deducted for unit inconsistency. Always write the unit conversion step explicitly: $50\,\text{mg} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kg}$.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Effect of Sliding Velocity and Contact Temperature*

**(a) [7 marks]**
With a neat block diagram, describe how the coefficient of friction of food materials changes with the sliding velocity. Identify the three regimes of the Stribeck curve and state, with two examples each, the food materials that fall in each regime.

**Model Solution:**

**Block-diagram explanation (3 marks):**

As the sliding velocity $v$ increases (for a given normal load $P$ and a given contacting pair), the friction coefficient first **decreases**, then passes through a **minimum**, and then **rises** again. The x-axis of the Stribeck curve is the dimensionless Hersey number $\eta v / P$ (or simply $v$ for dry contact), and the y-axis is $\mu$.

**Three regimes (3 marks):**

| Regime | Velocity range | Dominant mechanism | Food examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boundary** | Very low $v$ | Asperity–asperity contact, adhesion | Dry grain at rest on a chute; cocoa beans at start of discharge |
| **Mixed** | Moderate $v$ | Partial fluid-like film (fat, water) between asperities | Oil-coated peanuts; wet washed vegetables on a conveyor |
| **Hydrodynamic (full-film)** | High $v$ | Continuous film separates surfaces, viscous drag dominates | Molten chocolate film on a tempering belt; lubricated dough layer in an extruder barrel |

**Statement (1 mark):** Most food materials handled under normal plant conditions fall in the **boundary** or **mixed** regime; the **hydrodynamic** regime is observed only in well-lubricated or melted food systems such as chocolate enrobing.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**
Derive Blok's flash-temperature equation. A dough sheet of thickness $5\,\text{mm}$ slides on a hot steel plate at $0.4\,\text{m/s}$. The contact pressure is $18\,\text{kPa}$, the contact diameter is $4\,\text{mm}$, the thermal conductivity of the interface is $k = 22\,\text{W/m·K}$, $\mu = 0.28$, and the bulk dough temperature is $32\,°\text{C}$. Find (i) the flash temperature rise and (ii) the contact-patch temperature. Will starch gelatinisation be triggered? (Gelatinisation onset ≈ $62\,°\text{C}$.) Take $J = 4.186\,\text{J/cal}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3 marks)** — see §3.3 above for the full derivation. The final form is

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{\mu P v d}{2 k J}$$

**Numerical calculation (4 marks):**

Convert $P$ to Pa: $18\,\text{kPa} = 18\,000\,\text{Pa}$.

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{0.28 \times 18\,000 \times 0.4 \times 0.004}{2 \times 22 \times 4.186}$$

Numerator: $0.28 \times 18\,000 = 5040$; $5040 \times 0.4 = 2016$; $2016 \times 0.004 = 8.064$.
Denominator: $2 \times 22 \times 4.186 = 184.184$.

$$\Delta T_f = \frac{8.064}{184.184} = 0.0438\,°\text{C}$$

**Contact-patch temperature:**

$$T_c = 32 + 0.044 \approx 32.04\,°\text{C}$$

**Will starch gelatinisation occur? (built into step)**
$T_c = 32.04\,°\text{C}$ is far below the gelatinisation onset of $62\,°\text{C}$. Therefore **starch gelatinisation will NOT be triggered** at this sliding speed and pressure — additional external heating (from the hot plate) is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top 3 mistakes on this problem:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the denominator** of Blok's equation (most common error — costs 2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up pressure units** — KTU commonly gives pressure in kPa or MPa. Always convert to Pa before substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting $J$ in the denominator** when the examiner asks the answer in calories or when the formula is to be quoted in calorie form. If the question is silent on units, present the answer in °C and explicitly state that $J$ has been included.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Amontons' Law 1:** $F_f = \mu N$ — friction is proportional to load.
- **Amontons' Law 2:** $F_f$ is independent of velocity (idealisation only; food materials violate this).
- **Mohr–Coulomb for bulk food:** $\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$.
- **Wall friction angle $\phi_w$:** controls hopper and chute design; $\tau_w = \sigma_n \tan\phi_w$.
- **Frictional heat power:** $P_{\text{fric}} = \mu N v$ (in watts).
- **Blok's flash temperature:** $\Delta T_f = \dfrac{\mu P v d}{2 k J}$ — high-yield derivation.
- **Bulk temperature rise over time $t$:** $\Delta T_b = \dfrac{\mu N v \, t}{m c_p}$.
- **Angle of slide on a chute:** $\theta_{slide} = \arctan\mu$ — independent of mass.
- **Load effects on $\mu$:** elastic foods → constant; plastic foods → decrease; granular wet foods → increase.
- **Velocity effects on $\mu$:** described by the **Stribeck curve** — boundary, mixed, hydrodynamic.
- **Temperature effects on $\mu$:** softening/melting → $\mu$ drops; drying/glass-transition → $\mu$ rises.
- **Stribeck x-axis:** Hersey number $\eta v / P$.
- **Mechanical equivalent of heat:** $J = 4.186\,\text{J/cal}$ — always include in calorie-based numerical answers.
- **Conversion of pressure:** $1\,\text{kPa} = 10^3\,\text{Pa}$; $1\,\text{MPa} = 10^6\,\text{Pa}$.
- **Common exam trap:** always state the *regime* (boundary / mixed / hydrodynamic) when discussing velocity effects — KTU examiners award a separate mark for correct regime identification.
- **Food-specific pitfalls:** chocolate → cocoa-butter softening; wheat → moisture-induced adhesion; dough → shear-thinning; frozen foods → ice-bridging.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of water film and surface roughness, rolling resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT526 - ENGINEERING PROPERTIES OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of water film and surface roughness, rolling resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Effect of Water Film on Particulate Food Materials

### Formal Definition
A **water film** is a thin, adsorbed layer of liquid moisture that forms on the surface of food particles (grains, powders, seeds, crystalline sugar, etc.) due to hygroscopicity, surface energy interactions, and capillary condensation. When two food particles approach one another in bulk handling operations, these water films merge at contact points forming **liquid bridges** (also called **pendular bridges** or **capillary bridges**). The presence of such bridges introduces **capillary forces**, **viscous forces**, and **surface tension forces** that dramatically alter the apparent **cohesion**, **adhesion**, and **internal friction** of the bulk solid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Water film effects directly govern the flowability classification of food powders (e.g., Geldart Group C powders like cocoa, flour, fine sugar) and the caking behaviour of crystalline foods.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Wet Sand Castle" Phenomenon
Imagine you are at a beach building a sand castle. **Dry sand** simply pours off your hand and will not hold any shape because there is essentially no force holding the grains together. However, the moment you add a small amount of water (roughly **5–10% w/w**), the sand becomes mouldable into a sturdy castle. The water does NOT glue the grains chemically; instead, it forms tiny **liquid bridges** at the contact points between adjacent sand grains. These bridges pull the grains together through **surface tension** at the curved meniscus, just like a stretched rubber band pulling the grains closer. Add too much water and the bridges merge into a continuous water matrix, and the sand castle collapses. The very same physical principle governs the **handling, storage, and flow** of moist food powders, wet grains, and agglomerated food particulates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface Tension of Water (γ)** ≈ **72.8 mN/m** at 20 °C. This is the "rubber band strength" of the liquid bridge pulling two food particles together.

---

## 1.2 Effect of Surface Roughness

### Formal Definition
**Surface roughness** is the micro-scale geometric deviation of a real solid surface from an idealised perfectly smooth plane. For food materials, it is quantified by parameters such as the **arithmetic mean roughness (Rₐ)**, the **root-mean-square roughness (R_q)**, and the **peak-to-valley height (R_z)**. Surface roughness controls the **true area of contact** between two sliding or rolling bodies, and hence the **coefficient of friction (μ)**, the **adhesion force**, and the **wear behaviour** during transport and size reduction of food products.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Velcro vs. Glass Slab" Principle
Picture sliding a smooth glass plate across a polished marble countertop — the plate glides easily. Now, slide the same plate across a piece of **sandpaper**; the friction becomes enormous. The sandpaper has countless tiny **asperities** (microscopic peaks and valleys). Only the tips of these asperities actually touch the glass, but they "dig in" and resist motion. Food materials exhibit the same effect: a **rough-skinned almond** resists sliding in a chute far more than a **polished chocolate sphere** of identical mass. The actual contact area, the **real contact area Aᵣ**, is typically only **1–5%** of the apparent contact area, but those contact points bear the entire normal load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Rₐ values for food surfaces:**
> - Polished chocolate surface: **0.1–0.5 μm**
> - Wheat kernel: **5–50 μm**
> - Raw potato skin: **100–300 μm**
> - Crusty bread crust: **500–2000 μm**

---

## 1.3 Rolling Resistance

### Formal Definition
**Rolling resistance** (also called **rolling friction**) is the resisting force that opposes the motion of a body (typically a sphere, cylinder, or near-spherical food particle such as a grain, seed, or pellet) rolling over a deformable or rough surface. Unlike sliding friction, it is **not** caused by direct surface contact shear, but by **energy dissipation due to elastic/plastic/viscoelastic deformation** of the contact zone, **hysteresis losses** in the materials, and **micro-slip** at the contact interface. For a sphere of radius **r** rolling on a flat surface with a normal load **N**, the rolling resistance is given by:

$$F_r = N \cdot \frac{d}{r} = N \cdot \mu_r$$

where **d** is the depth of the deformation track (in metres) and **μᵣ** is the dimensionless **coefficient of rolling resistance**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Tired Bicycle in Soft Soil" Principle
Imagine pushing a bicycle across firm concrete — the wheels roll with almost zero effort. Now push the same bicycle across a soft, sandy beach. The wheels sink slightly into the sand, and you must apply a continuous force just to keep it moving. The sand deforms under each wheel, and energy is wasted in compressing the sand grains. The same principle applies when a **wheat kernel** rolls down a chute lined with rubber, or when a **pea** rolls over a vibrating screen. The "deformation track" in the bulk food bed is analogous to the bicycle wheel sinking into sand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Hot Topic:** Rolling resistance problems regularly appear in Module 2 ESE questions for food particle dynamics in **gravity chutes, vibratory conveyors, and rotating drum dryers**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Water-Film Induced Capillary Forces

When two spherical food particles of radii **R₁** and **R₂** approach each other with a separation **D** (much smaller than the particle radius), a **liquid bridge** forms between them. The bridge has two principal radii of curvature: the **meridional curvature (r₁)** and the **azimuthal curvature (r₂)**. By the **Young–Laplace equation**, the pressure inside the liquid bridge is lower than the surrounding gas pressure by the **Laplace pressure** ΔP.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Bridge Formation

1. **Adsorbed Moisture Film Formation:** Hygroscopic food particles (e.g., sugar, salt, milk powder) attract water molecules from humid air, forming a **multilayer adsorbed film** typically **0.5–3 nm** thick. The film thickness is governed by the **BET isotherm** and the surrounding relative humidity.
2. **Capillary Condensation:** As two particles approach within a critical separation (typically < **10 nm** for atmospheric humidity), water vapour spontaneously condenses in the gap, forming a **bulk liquid bridge** at a pressure below saturation (the **Kelvin effect**).
3. **Meniscus Formation:** The bridge develops a **concave meniscus** (when water wets the food surface with contact angle θ < 90°). The meniscus exerts a **negative Laplace pressure**, drawing the particles together.
4. **Bridge Stability:** For small bridges (the **pendular state**), each liquid bridge is a discrete connection. As moisture content increases, bridges merge into the **funicular state**, then the **capillary state** (fully saturated pores).
5. **Capillary Force Transmission:** The total attractive force between two particles is the **vector sum of the surface tension force** (along the contact line) and the **Laplace pressure force** (acting over the bridge cross-section).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Typical Units** | **Application in Food Engineering** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \gamma \left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} + \dfrac{1}{r_2} \right)$ | **Young–Laplace equation** for capillary pressure in a bridge | Pa | Pressure deficit in moisture bridges in powders |
| 2 | $F_{\text{cap}} \approx 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 2R/D}}\right]$ | **Capillary force** between two equal spheres (R = R₁ = R₂) with bridge separation D | N | Inter-particle attraction in wet flour, cocoa |
| 3 | $F_{\text{cap}} \approx 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ | **Asymptotic capillary force** for small separations (D ≪ R) | N | Strongly adhered wet particles |
| 4 | $\mu_{\text{sliding}} = \dfrac{F_f}{N} = \tau_s \dfrac{A_r}{A_{\text{app}}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{p_m}$ | **Sliding friction** with real/apparent contact area ratio | dimensionless | Friction of grains on chute surfaces |
| 5 | $A_r = \dfrac{N}{H}$ | **Real contact area** where H is the hardness of the softer material | m² | Contact mechanics of food asperities |
| 6 | $F_r = N \cdot \dfrac{d}{r}$ | **Rolling resistance** of a sphere/cylinder | N | Grain/seed rolling in chutes |
| 7 | $F_r = C_r \cdot N$ | **Compact form** of rolling resistance, where Cᵣ = d/r is the rolling resistance coefficient | N | Drum dryer particle dynamics |
| 8 | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert z(x) \vert \, dx$ | **Arithmetic mean surface roughness** | m | Quantifying fruit/vegetable skin roughness |
| 9 | $R_q = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L z(x)^2 \, dx}$ | **Root-mean-square roughness** | m | Surface profile of processed foods |
| 10 | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{F_{\text{total}}}{N} = \mu_s + \dfrac{F_{\text{cap}}}{N}$ | **Effective friction angle** with capillary contribution | dimensionless | Slope-of-repose of moist powders |
| 11 | $F_{\text{visc}} = \dfrac{3}{2}\pi \eta R^2 \dfrac{1}{D}\dfrac{dD}{dt}$ | **Viscous force** in liquid bridge (Stefan approximation) | N | Rate-dependent adhesion in wet food |
| 12 | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}$ | **Young's equation** for contact angle on food surface | dimensionless | Wettability of food surfaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Reminder for KTU Exams:** When writing Laplace pressure, use **r₁** and **r₂** as subscripts — *never* the absolute-value pipe `|r|` in tables or inline equations. The vertical pipe `|` will break markdown table syntax in your answer sheet if typed in a spreadsheet format.

---

## 2.3 Mechanics of Surface Roughness on Friction

### The Bowden–Tabor Model for Food Contact
Food surfaces in bulk handling are rarely atomically smooth. The actual contact between two food particles (or a food particle and a chute wall) occurs at a finite number of **asperity tips**. The Bowden–Tabor model splits the friction analysis into two components:

1. **Adhesion Component (F_adh):** Due to molecular bonds at the true contact area.  
   $$F_{\text{adh}} = \tau_s \cdot A_r$$  
   where **τₛ** is the shear strength of the junction and **Aᵣ** is the real area of contact.

2. **Deformation (Ploughing) Component (F_plough):** Due to asperities of the harder surface digging into the softer one.  
   $$F_{\text{plough}} \propto \dfrac{R_a}{r_{\text{asp}}} \cdot N$$

3. **Total Friction Force:**  
   $$F_f = F_{\text{adh}} + F_{\text{plough}} = \tau_s \cdot A_r + k_{\text{plough}} \cdot R_a \cdot N$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Surface roughness engineering is used in food processing to **deliberately increase** friction (e.g., rough-textured conveyor belts for inclined transport of packaged food) or **reduce** friction (e.g., polished stainless steel hoppers for free-flowing sugar).

---

## 2.4 Mechanics of Rolling Resistance — The Three Contributions

For a spherical food particle of radius **r** rolling on a flat surface under a normal load **N**, the rolling resistance arises from three physical mechanisms:

### Mechanism 1: Elastic Hysteresis (Dominant in Viscoelastic Foods)
The contact patch on a rolling sphere experiences **compressive stress at the leading edge** and **tensile recovery at the trailing edge**. Viscoelastic food materials (e.g., dough, gelatin, fresh fruit) dissipate mechanical energy as heat during the loading-unloading cycle, leaving a net force opposing motion. This is the **Goldsmith–Powell hysteresis model**.

### Mechanism 2: Plastic Deformation (For Hard, Brittle Foods)
For very hard food particles (e.g., rice grains, dried peas) rolling on a soft chute lining (e.g., rubber, fabric), the chute material undergoes **permanent plastic deformation** beneath the rolling particle, creating a **depression of depth d**. The particle must "climb out" of this depression, requiring a force:
$$F_r = N \cdot \tan(\alpha) \approx N \cdot \dfrac{d}{r}$$
where **α** is the small angle of the contact zone entry.

### Mechanism 3: Micro-slip at the Contact Interface
Even in "pure" rolling, **creep** and **spin** occur due to finite contact patch size. The tangential tractions in the contact patch do not perfectly balance, leading to a small **net braking torque** that manifests as rolling resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Rolling resistance is **critically important** in:
> - **Gravity chute design** for grain elevators (slope angle 25°–45°)
> - **Rotating drum dryers** for milk powder, detergent-grade powders
> - **Vibratory conveyors** for fragile foods (potato chips, breakfast cereals)
> - **Pneumatic conveying** line losses (rolled particles vs. suspended particles)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Capillary Force Between Two Equal Spheres

We now derive the **capillary force** between two identical spherical food particles of radius **R**, separated by a distance **D**, connected by a liquid bridge of surface tension **γ** with a contact angle **θ**.

### Setup and Geometry
Consider two spheres approaching along the x-axis. The liquid bridge is **axisymmetric** about the line joining the sphere centres. The bridge cross-section at its narrowest point (the "neck") has radius **yₙ**, and the meridional radius of curvature is **r₁** (negative for a concave meniscus when wetting). The azimuthal radius **r₂** lies in the plane perpendicular to the symmetry axis.

### Step 1: Apply the Young–Laplace Equation
The pressure drop across the meniscus is given by the Young–Laplace equation. For an axisymmetric bridge:
$$\Delta P = P_{\text{inside}} - P_{\text{outside}} = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2} \right)$$
The negative sign on **r₂** accounts for the fact that the meridional and azimuthal curvatures have **opposite signs** for a concave meniscus. For a wetting liquid (θ < 90°) on most food surfaces, **r₁ > 0** and **r₂ < 0**, making the inside pressure lower than the outside, generating **attraction**.

### Step 2: Express the Laplace Pressure Geometrically
At the narrowest neck of the bridge, the meridional radius of curvature **r₁** is, by geometric construction:
$$r_1 = \frac{y_n^2 + (D/2)^2}{2(D/2) - 2y_n \cos\theta} \approx \frac{y_n^2}{D - 2y_n \cos\theta}$$
The approximation holds for small separation (D ≪ R). The azimuthal radius at the neck is simply:
$$r_2 = -y_n$$
(the negative sign denotes the convex side of the meniscus away from the symmetry axis).

### Step 3: Compute the Laplace Pressure
$$\Delta P \approx \gamma \left( \frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{1}{y_n} \right)$$
For a small bridge (D ≪ R), the dominant term in the geometric analysis is the **1/yₙ** contribution, which simplifies to:
$$\Delta P \approx \gamma \left( \frac{2\cos\theta}{D} + \frac{1}{R} \right)$$

### Step 4: Compute the Force Contribution from Laplace Pressure
The Laplace pressure acts over the cross-sectional area **A = π yₙ²** of the neck. Substituting yₙ ≈ (RD/2)^(1/2) (from geometric mass-conservation of the bridge volume), the area is:
$$A = \pi y_n^2 \approx \frac{\pi R D}{2}$$
The force due to Laplace pressure is therefore:
$$F_{\text{Laplace}} = \Delta P \cdot A \approx \gamma \left( \frac{2\cos\theta}{D} \right) \cdot \frac{\pi R D}{2} = \pi R \gamma \cdot 2\cos\theta$$

### Step 5: Compute the Force Contribution from Surface Tension (Vector Component)
The surface tension force acts **along the perimeter** of the meniscus, directed at the contact angle **θ** relative to the surface. The vertical (attractive) component is:
$$F_{\text{tension}} = 2\pi y_n \cdot \gamma \sin\phi$$
where **ϕ** is the angle the meniscus makes with the symmetry axis. For small separations, **ϕ ≈ θ**, and using **yₙ ≈ √(RD/2)**:
$$F_{\text{tension}} \approx 2\pi \gamma \sin\theta \cdot \sqrt{\frac{RD}{2}}$$

### Step 6: Total Capillary Force
Adding both contributions and noting that the Laplace pressure term dominates for small D (since F_Laplace ∝ R, independent of D, while F_tension ∝ √D), we obtain the **dominant asymptotic expression**:
$$\boxed{F_{\text{cap}} \approx 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2R}{D}}} \right]}$$

For **D ≪ R** (typical of strongly adherent wet food particles), the bracket tends to **1**, giving the maximum capillary force:
$$F_{\text{cap,max}} \approx 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Result:** The maximum capillary force between two equal food particles of radius **R = 1 mm** with γ = 72 mN/m and θ = 0° (perfect wetting) is **F_cap ≈ 4.5 × 10⁻⁴ N (≈ 45 mgf)**. This is enormous compared to the particle's weight (≈ 0.01 mgf for a grain), which is why even slightly moist powders behave like cohesive solids and fail to flow.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Rolling Resistance Coefficient

We now derive the classical **rolling resistance equation** for a rigid sphere of radius **r** rolling on a deformable flat half-space under a normal load **N**.

### Step 1: Geometry of the Deformed Contact Zone
The sphere depresses into the deformable surface, creating a **shallow depression of depth d** beneath the contact zone. The contact zone has a **half-chord length a**, given approximately by the **Hertz contact formula**:
$$a = \left( \frac{4 N r}{3 E^*} \right)^{1/3}$$
where **E\*** is the **effective contact modulus**, given by:
$$\frac{1}{E^*} = \frac{1 - \nu_1^2}{E_1} + \frac{1 - \nu_2^2}{E_2}$$
and **E₁, E₂** and **ν₁, ν₂** are the elastic moduli and Poisson's ratios of the sphere and the deformable surface.

### Step 2: Force Balance on the Sphere
In static equilibrium, the resultant of the distributed contact pressure must be equal in magnitude to **N** (normal load) and **Fᵣ** (rolling resistance, the horizontal force needed to maintain steady rolling). Taking moments about the contact centre, the normal pressure distribution is **asymmetric** — the resultant of the pressure distribution acts **slightly ahead of the geometric centre of the contact patch** by a distance **c** (the **offset of the pressure centroid**).

### Step 3: Compute the Moment
The asymmetry of the pressure distribution creates a couple. The pressure distribution is approximately Hertzian, but shifted forward by **c**. The total moment about the geometric centre is:
$$M = N \cdot c$$
The horizontal force required to balance this moment at the rolling radius is:
$$F_r = \frac{M}{r} = \frac{N \cdot c}{r}$$

### Step 4: Introduce the "Deformation Depth" Approximation
By a geometric argument (the sphere sits in a depression of depth **d**), the offset **c** is approximately equal to the deformation depth:
$$c \approx d$$
This is the **classical rolling resistance approximation** valid for small deformations (d ≪ r).

### Step 5: Final Rolling Resistance Equation
$$\boxed{F_r = N \cdot \frac{d}{r}}$$

Defining the **coefficient of rolling resistance** as:
$$\mu_r = \frac{F_r}{N} = \frac{d}{r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** Since the deformation depth **d** is typically a fraction of a millimetre and the particle radius **r** is several millimetres, the rolling resistance coefficient for food grains is in the range **0.01–0.1**, which is **5–50 times smaller** than the typical sliding friction coefficient (**0.3–0.6**). This is why food particles, when free to roll, travel much further down a chute than when forced to slide.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Effect Summary

| **Property** | **Effect on Friction (μ)** | **Effect on Flowability** | **Effect on Caking Tendency** | **Effect on Rolling Resistance** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Increased water film thickness | **Strongly increases** (capillary bridges) | **Decreases sharply** | **Strongly increases** | Slightly increases (cohesion adds to mass) |
| Increased surface roughness (Rₐ) | **Increases** (ploughing) | Decreases (interlocks particles) | Marginal effect | **Increases** (hysteresis losses) |
| Decreased particle radius (R ↓) | Increases (more contact points) | **Strongly decreases** | **Strongly increases** | **Increases** (μᵣ = d/r ↑) |
| Increased surface tension (γ ↑) | **Increases** | Decreases | Increases | Slight increase |
| Increased contact angle (θ ↑, i.e., less wetting) | Decreases | **Increases** | Decreases | Slight decrease |
| Increased elastic modulus of food (E ↑) | Slight increase | Slight increase | Slight decrease | **Decreases** (less deformation) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define "capillary bridge" in the context of food powders. Explain how capillary bridges affect the flowability of moist food powders.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **capillary bridge** is a small volume of liquid (typically water) that forms a concave meniscus connecting two adjacent hygroscopic particles in a bulk food powder when their adsorbed moisture films merge upon close approach. The bridge generates a **negative Laplace pressure** inside the meniscus, which together with the **vertical component of surface tension** pulls the two particles together with a force that can be approximated by:

$$F_{\text{cap}} \approx 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$$

where **R** is the particle radius, **γ** is the surface tension of water, and **θ** is the contact angle. Because this force scales with particle size and **dominates over gravitational forces** for fine powders, the inter-particle attraction is sufficient to **lock particles in place**, causing severe **flow problems** such as **arching, ratholing, and caking** in silos. Hence, the **flowability of moist food powders decreases** sharply with increasing moisture content, often requiring the use of **flow conditioners (e.g., silica, tricalcium phosphate)** or **drying operations** to restore free-flow behaviour. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between "sliding friction" and "rolling resistance" in the context of food particle dynamics. Which one is more relevant for spherical grains in a gravity chute?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Sliding friction** is the resistance to motion when two surfaces slide tangentially past each other. It arises from **microscopic asperity interlocking** and **interfacial shear of contact junctions**, with the friction force given by **F_f = μ_s × N**, where **μₛ** is the coefficient of sliding friction (typically **0.3–0.7** for food grains on steel).

**Rolling resistance** is the resistance to motion of a body that rolls over a surface. It arises from **elastic hysteresis, plastic deformation of the substrate, and micro-slip** in the contact zone, with the friction force given by **F_r = μ_r × N**, where **μ_r = d/r** is the coefficient of rolling resistance (typically **0.01–0.1** for grains, an order of magnitude smaller than μₛ).

For **spherical grains in a gravity chute**, rolling resistance is the more relevant phenomenon because grains can **roll freely** under gravity, and the much smaller **μ_r** allows them to travel further down the chute with a **gentler slope angle** than would be required to overcome sliding friction. Therefore chute designers prefer inclined surfaces that **encourage rolling rather than sliding** of food particles. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive an expression for the **capillary force** between two equal spherical food particles of radius **R** separated by a small distance **D**, connected by a liquid bridge of surface tension **γ** and contact angle **θ**. Discuss the influence of bridge geometry on the maximum attractive force.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain with appropriate diagrams the **effect of water film and surface roughness on the frictional behaviour of food grains in bulk handling operations**. Discuss at least three engineering implications for hopper and chute design.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup (1 mark):**
Consider two identical spherical particles of radius **R**, with centres separated by a distance **2R + D**, where **D** is the surface-to-surface gap. A liquid bridge of water fills the gap, forming an axisymmetric concave meniscus at the neck. The meridional radius of curvature is **r₁** (positive) and the azimuthal radius of curvature is **r₂ = −yₙ** (negative), where **yₙ** is the neck radius.

**Step 2 — Apply the Young–Laplace Equation (1 mark):**
The pressure deficit across the meniscus is:
$$\Delta P = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2} \right) = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{1}{y_n} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Geometric Approximations (1 mark):**
For a small bridge (D ≪ R), the meridional radius can be approximated as:
$$\frac{1}{r_1} \approx \frac{2\cos\theta}{D}$$
and the neck radius from mass conservation of bridge volume:
$$y_n \approx \sqrt{\frac{RD}{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Force Contributions (2 marks):**
The total capillary force is the sum of two contributions:
- **Laplace pressure force** acting over the neck area **A = π yₙ²**:
$$F_{\text{Laplace}} = \Delta P \cdot A = \gamma \left( \frac{2\cos\theta}{D} \right) \cdot \frac{\pi R D}{2} = \pi R \gamma \cdot 2\cos\theta$$
- **Surface tension force** along the contact perimeter (subdominant for small D):
$$F_{\text{tension}} = 2\pi y_n \cdot \gamma \sin\theta \approx 2\pi \gamma \sin\theta \cdot \sqrt{\frac{RD}{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Expression (1 mark):**
Adding both contributions and combining into a unified form:
$$\boxed{F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2R}{D}}} \right]}$$

**Step 6 — Discussion of Maximum Force (1 mark):**
For **D → 0** (full contact), the bracket tends to **1**, giving the asymptotic maximum force:
$$F_{\text{cap,max}} \approx 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$$
This result shows that the maximum capillary force is **independent of bridge length D** and scales **linearly with particle radius R** and **linearly with surface tension γ**. For perfectly wetting water (θ = 0°, γ = 72 mN/m) on a particle of radius 1 mm, F_cap,max ≈ 4.5 × 10⁻⁴ N. As **R decreases** (finer powders), the absolute force per particle decreases, but the **force-to-weight ratio** increases as **1/R²** (since particle mass scales as R³), explaining why **very fine food powders** are notoriously cohesive.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
In bulk food handling (silos, hoppers, conveyors, chutes), the frictional behaviour of food grains is governed by a **complex interplay** of three surface phenomena: **water film capillary effects**, **surface roughness-induced ploughing**, and **viscous lubrication** by moisture films.

**Effect 1 — Water Film (2 marks):**
When food grains carry even a small amount of surface moisture (**3–8% w/w**), liquid bridges form at contact points. The **capillary force** between two adjacent grains of radius 3 mm can reach **~1.3 mN**, which is **comparable to the gravitational force on the grain (~0.28 mN)**.
