This dramatically increases the **effective coefficient of friction (μ_eff)**, given by:
$$\mu_{\text{eff}} = \mu_s + \frac{F_{\text{cap}}}{N}$$
This explains why **moist wheat** has a steeper angle of repose (~45°) than **dry wheat** (~27°).

**Effect 2 — Surface Roughness (2 marks):**
Food grain surfaces (e.g., rice, barley) are covered with asperities of Rₐ = 5–50 μm. These asperities **interlock and plough** into adjacent surfaces, increasing the ploughing component of friction. The total friction force can be modelled as:
$$F_f = \tau_s \cdot A_r + k_{\text{plough}} \cdot R_a \cdot N$$
The rough-skinned **coconut coir** has a much higher μ than the smooth-skinned **green grape**, even at the same moisture content.

**Effect 3 — Engineering Implications for Hopper and Chute Design (2 marks):**
Three important implications are:
1. **Hopper Slope Angle:** Moist grains require a **steeper hopper wall** (angle of 25°–30° from vertical) compared to dry grains (15°–20°) to ensure mass flow and avoid arching.
2. **Surface Finish Selection:** **Polished stainless steel** (Rₐ < 0.8 μm) is used for hoppers handling cohesive powders, while **textured rubber or UHMWPE linings** are used in chutes where high friction is desired to prevent slipping of packaged goods.
3. **Moisture Control:** **Dehumidified air injection** in silos and **drying of grains below 12% w/w moisture** are standard practices to suppress capillary cohesion and ensure reliable gravity discharge.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **classical equation for rolling resistance** of a rigid sphere of radius **r** rolling on a deformable surface under a normal load **N**. Clearly state the assumptions and discuss the physical origin of the rolling resistance force.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a **labelled sketch**, describe the **Bowden–Tabor model** for the friction of rough food surfaces. How does the model explain the **role of adsorbed water films** in modifying the friction coefficient of food grains?

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Setup and Assumptions (1 mark):**
Consider a rigid sphere of radius **r** rolling without slipping on a flat, deformable half-space under a vertical normal load **N**. Assumptions: (i) small deformation (d ≪ r), (ii) isotropic elastic half-space, (iii) quasi-static rolling.

**Step 2 — Hertzian Contact Geometry (1 mark):**
The contact patch is circular with half-chord:
$$a = \left( \frac{4 N r}{3 E^*} \right)^{1/3}$$
where **E\*** is the effective modulus. The maximum contact pressure is:
$$p_0 = \frac{3N}{2\pi a^2}$$

**Step 3 — Asymmetric Pressure Distribution (1 mark):**
When the sphere rolls, the pressure distribution is **asymmetric** — the resultant of the pressure acts not at the geometric centre of the contact patch but **displaced forward by a distance c** (the "centroid offset"). This displacement is the source of the rolling resistance.

**Step 4 — Force and Moment Balance (2 marks):**
Taking moments about the geometric centre of the contact patch, the asymmetric pressure generates a moment:
$$M = N \cdot c$$
For steady rolling, a horizontal force **Fᵣ** must be applied at the centre of the sphere to balance this moment:
$$F_r \cdot r = M \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_r = \frac{N \cdot c}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Relate c to Deformation Depth d (1 mark):**
For small deformations, the geometric argument that the sphere "sinks" into a depression of depth **d** gives the relation **c ≈ d**. Substituting:
$$\boxed{F_r = N \cdot \frac{d}{r}}$$

**Step 6 — Physical Origin Discussion (1 mark):**
The physical origin of the rolling resistance is the **dissipation of mechanical energy** in the contact zone through (a) **elastic hysteresis** in viscoelastic food materials (e.g., fresh fruit, dough), (b) **plastic deformation** of a softer substrate (e.g., rubber chute lining), and (c) **micro-slip** at the contact interface. The depression depth **d** is a measure of the energy lost per unit load, and the ratio **d/r** is the **coefficient of rolling resistance μᵣ**, which is typically **0.01–0.1** for food grains.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Sketch Description (1.5 marks):**
A typical Bowden–Tabor sketch shows two rough food surfaces in nominal contact over an apparent area **A_app**. The surface profiles are drawn as **irregular peaks (asperities)** separated by valleys. The **real contact area Aᵣ** is shown as a series of **discrete micro-contact spots** at the asperity tips, occupying only a small fraction (typically **1–5%**) of the apparent area. The figure must also show a **normal load N** pushing the surfaces together, an **adhesion shear zone** at each micro-contact, and a **ploughing zone** where the harder asperity digs into the softer material.

**The Two Friction Components (2 marks):**
- **Adhesion Component:** F_adh = τ_s × A_r, where τ_s is the **shear strength of the micro-junction**.  
- **Ploughing Component:** F_plough = k_plough × R_a × N, where R_a is the surface roughness and k_plough is a geometric factor that depends on the asperity shape.

**Real Contact Area Scaling (1.5 marks):**
For **plastic deformation of asperities**, the real contact area is proportional to the load:
$$A_r = \frac{N}{H}$$
where **H** is the hardness of the softer food material. This means the friction coefficient for a fully plastic contact is **μ = τ_s/H**, which is a **material property** of the food independent of the apparent area or the normal load — a key insight from the Bowden–Tabor model.

**Role of Adsorbed Water Films (2 marks):**
Adsorbed water films modify friction in two opposing ways:
1. **Cohesion Enhancement (Friction Increase):** Water films at asperity tips create **liquid micro-bridges** that increase the effective shear strength **τ_s** of the junction. The Laplace pressure of the bridge adds a normal force at the contact, effectively increasing **A_r** and the adhesion component.
2. **Lubrication (Friction Decrease):** For fully flooded contacts, the water film can act as a **hydrodynamic or boundary lubricant**, reducing direct asperity contact and lowering **τ_s** and the ploughing term.

The **net effect** depends on the **film thickness regime**:
- **Sub-monolayer (h < 1 nm):** Lubrication effect dominates, friction drops.
- **Multilayer / capillary bridge (h = 1–10 nm):** Capillary cohesion dominates, friction rises sharply with moisture.
- **Bulk water film (h > 100 nm):** Hydrodynamic lubrication takes over, friction falls again.

This explains the experimentally observed **non-monotonic dependence** of food grain friction coefficient on moisture content, with a **friction maximum at intermediate moisture levels** (~5–8% w/w) typical of stored grain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the two radii r₁ and r₂** in the Laplace equation. The **meridional r₁** is positive (concave towards the particle) and the **azimuthal r₂** is negative (convex). Many students incorrectly add them — **they have opposite signs**, hence the equation reads **γ(1/r₁ + 1/yₙ)**, not **γ(1/r₁ − 1/yₙ)**.
> 2. **Rolling resistance is NOT a Coulomb friction** — it is a **moment-based** phenomenon. Do not write F = μN with the same μ as sliding friction; always derive the moment balance and divide by the radius.
> 3. **Surface roughness does NOT always increase friction.** In well-lubricated contacts (boundary or mixed lubrication), the rough valleys can act as **micro-reservoirs** of water/lubricant, and friction may decrease. Mention this in your answers to score the full 7 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (N vs. mN vs. μN) is a common cause of losing 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Do NOT mix up F_cap and F_adh** — capillary force is due to **meniscus pressure + surface tension**, while adhesion force is due to **van der Waals / molecular bonding at real contact area**. They are different physical mechanisms, even though both contribute to cohesion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Water film thickness** on hygroscopic food particles is **0.5–3 nm** at normal humidities; thicker films (capillary bridges) form at particle-particle contacts.
- **Capillary bridge force** is given by **F_cap ≈ 2πRγcosθ [1 − 1/√(1 + 2R/D)]**; the **maximum force** (at D → 0) is **2πRγcosθ**, **independent of D**.
- The **Young–Laplace equation** for capillary pressure is **ΔP = γ(1/r₁ + 1/r₂)**, with **opposite signs** for the two principal curvatures of a concave meniscus.
- **Three moisture states** in bulk powders: **Pendular (discrete bridges) → Funicular (merged bridges) → Capillary (fully saturated)**.
- **Surface roughness (Rₐ)** for food surfaces ranges from **< 1 μm** (chocolate) to **> 1 mm** (crusty bread). The **real contact area Aᵣ** is only **1–5%** of the **apparent contact area A_app**.
- The **Bowden–Tabor model** decomposes friction into **adhesion (τ_s × Aᵣ)** and **ploughing (k × Rₐ × N)** components.
- **Sliding friction coefficient** for food grains on steel is typically **μₛ = 0.3–0.6**; **rolling resistance coefficient** is **μᵣ = 0.01–0.1** (about an **order of magnitude smaller**).
- **Rolling resistance** scales as **F_r = N × d/r**, where **d** is the **depression depth** in the substrate and **r** is the particle radius.
- The **three physical origins** of rolling resistance are: **elastic hysteresis, plastic deformation, and micro-slip** at the contact zone.
- **Hopper and chute design** must account for the **non-monotonic friction–moisture curve**, with peak friction at intermediate moisture (**5–8% w/w**).
- **Engineering solutions** for high-friction food handling: **dehumidification, drying, surface polishing, flow conditioners (anti-caking agents), vibratory assistance, and chute-angle optimisation** (typically 25°–45° from horizontal).
- **Young's equation** for contact angle: **cosθ = (γ_sv − γ_sl) / γ_lv**, where **γ_sv, γ_sl, γ_lv** are the solid–vapour, solid–liquid, and liquid–vapour interfacial tensions. For most food surfaces, **θ < 90°** (hydrophilic).
- **Typical numerical values to memorise for KTU exams:**
  - γ(water, 20 °C) = **72.8 mN/m**
  - γ(water, 80 °C) ≈ **63 mN/m**
  - Rₐ(polished steel) = **0.05–0.5 μm**
  - Rₐ(wheat kernel) = **5–50 μm**
  - μₛ(grain on steel) = **0.3–0.5**
  - μᵣ(grain rolling) = **0.01–0.05**
  - Angle of repose (dry wheat) = **27°**
  - Angle of repose (moist wheat) = **45°**
- **Key dimensionless numbers and their typical values for food particles:** Bond number (Bo = ρgR²/γ) for grains ~10⁻³ to 10⁻¹ (capillary forces dominate); Capillary number (Ca = ηv/γ) for slow chute flow ~10⁻⁶ (viscous negligible).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** When answering Part B questions on this topic, **always draw a clear free-body diagram** of the particle showing the normal force, the friction/rolling resistance force, the gravitational component, and (if applicable) the capillary force. A well-labelled diagram alone can earn **1–2 marks** even if the algebra has minor errors. Examiners reward **visual clarity and conceptual linking** as much as mathematical rigour.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angle of repose
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT526 - ENGINEERING PROPERTIES OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Angle of repose

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Angle of Repose (θ)** is a fundamental engineering property of bulk particulate food materials (powders, grains, seeds, flours, granules) defined as the **maximum angle measured from the horizontal plane** at which a heap or pile of granular material will remain stable without any slumping, sliding, or avalanching of the particles. It is a direct macroscopic manifestation of the inter-particle **frictional and cohesive forces** operating at the micro-scale.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{r}\right)$$

where:
- **h** = height of the conical pile (in **m** or **mm**)
- **r** = base radius of the conical pile (in **m** or **mm**)
- **θ** = angle of repose (in **degrees (°)**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The angle of repose is a **bulk property** (not a single-particle property). It governs the design of **storage silos, hoppers, conveying systems, packaging machinery, and gravity-flow feeders** in food processing plants.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine pouring raw rice from a kitchen container onto a flat steel plate. The rice grains naturally stack into a cone-shaped heap. Notice three things:

1. The heap has a **sloped side** — not vertical.
2. The slope angle depends on the grain type — **rice (smooth)** makes a gentle slope; **chickpeas (rough)** make a steeper slope.
3. If you pour too high, the sides **avalanche** and the pile flattens.

The slope angle at which the pile is just about to slide is the **angle of repose**. It is essentially the material's "**comfortable resting posture**."

> [!NOTE]
> **Everyday Examples in Food Industry:**
> - A pile of **wheat flour** in a hopper (low angle, ~30–45°)
> - A heap of **sugar crystals** on a conveyor (medium angle, ~45°)
> - A mound of **moist whole spices** (high angle, > 50° due to moisture-induced cohesion)

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard unit:** **Degrees (°)** or **Radians (rad)**
- **Typical range for food materials:** **20° – 55°**
- **Reference standard:** The angle is measured relative to the **horizontal plane**, not the vertical.
- **Test conditions:** Must be reported along with **moisture content (%, w.b.)**, **particle size (mm)**, and **measurement method**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does KTU emphasize this property?** Because an incorrect angle of repose value can lead to **flow failures** (arching, ratholing, flooding) in industrial hoppers, causing **downtime, contamination, and product loss** worth crores in large food plants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Does a Pile Hold its Shape?

A granular pile remains stable because of an internal balance of forces:

- **Gravitational pull (downward)** on each particle at the surface of the pile tries to drag it down the slope.
- **Inter-particle friction (resisting force)** between contacting grains holds the particle in place.
- **Cohesion (if moisture is present)** adds an additional "stickiness" binding the particles together.

When the gravitational component along the slope **exceeds** the combined frictional and cohesive resistance, particles begin to slide — this is the **critical condition** that defines the angle of repose.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Failure

1. **Particle is at rest on the sloping surface of the pile.**
2. **Weight (W = mg)** acts vertically downward.
3. **Weight is resolved** into two components:
   - **Normal to slope:** $W_n = mg \cos\theta$
   - **Parallel to slope (sliding direction):** $W_t = mg \sin\theta$
4. **Normal reaction (N)** = $mg \cos\theta$
5. **Maximum static friction** = $\mu_s \cdot N = \mu_s \cdot mg \cos\theta$
6. **Sliding initiates when:** $mg \sin\theta \geq \mu_s \cdot mg \cos\theta$
7. **Therefore, at the critical angle:** $\tan\theta = \mu_s$, giving $\theta = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables** | **Units** | **Application** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $\theta = \tan^{-1}(h/r)$ | Basic geometric definition | $h$ = pile height, $r$ = base radius | m, m, ° | Poured cone method |
| $\tan\theta = \mu_s$ | Frictional basis | $\mu_s$ = static friction coefficient | dimensionless | Particle-level analysis |
| $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2h}{D}\right)$ | Alternative (diameter-based) | $D$ = base diameter | m, m, ° | Funnel method |
| $V_{cone} = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$ | Pile volume | $r$, $h$ | m³ | Bulk density calc |
| $\theta = 45° + \frac{\phi_{w}}{2}$ | Jenike's flowability index (effective angle) | $\phi_w$ = wall friction angle | degrees | Hopper design |
| $h = r \tan\theta$ | Height-radius relationship | — | m | Geometric construction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** In the formula table above, the notation uses `tan⁻¹` and `μ` — **never** use the absolute value symbol `|x|` inside a markdown table. Use `\\vert` or `\\mid` in LaTeX if needed, but prefer bare symbols.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Hopper & Silo Design:** The hopper wall angle must be **at least 5–10° steeper** than the angle of repose to ensure **mass flow** (first-in-first-out discharge) rather than **funnel flow** (which causes spoilage of stagnant food).
- **Conveyor Belt Inclination:** Maximum inclination of an inclined belt conveyor transporting grain **must be less than** the angle of repose to prevent roll-back.
- **Packaging Machine Calibration:** Filling nozzles and dosing augers are designed with knowledge of repose angle to achieve **uniform fill volume** in pouches and cans.
- **Quality Control:** Sudden change in repose angle of the same food product can indicate **moisture absorption, lump formation, or fat oxidation** — useful for **shelf-life monitoring**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Angle of Repose from Force Balance on an Inclined Plane

Consider a single food particle (e.g., a grain of wheat) resting on the sloping surface of a conical pile, just at the verge of sliding downward.

**Step 1: Identify the forces acting on the particle.**

Three forces act on the particle:
1. Weight $W = mg$ acting vertically downward.
2. Normal reaction $N$ acting perpendicular to the inclined slope.
3. Static friction force $f_s$ acting up the slope, opposing impending motion.

**Step 2: Resolve the weight vector into components parallel and perpendicular to the slope.**

The slope makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The weight is resolved as follows:

- Component **along the slope (down-slope direction):**
  $$W_{\parallel} = mg \sin\theta$$
- Component **perpendicular to the slope (into the surface):**
  $$W_{\perp} = mg \cos\theta$$

**Step 3: Apply equilibrium conditions perpendicular to the slope.**

Since the particle is not accelerating into or away from the slope:
$$N = mg \cos\theta$$

**Step 4: Apply the limiting static friction condition.**

At the verge of sliding, the friction force reaches its maximum value given by Amontons' law of friction:
$$f_{s,max} = \mu_s N$$

Substituting the value of $N$:
$$f_{s,max} = \mu_s \cdot mg \cos\theta$$

**Step 5: Apply equilibrium along the slope (at the verge of sliding, the parallel component is just balanced by friction).**

$$mg \sin\theta = \mu_s \cdot mg \cos\theta$$

**Step 6: Simplify by cancelling the common factor $mg$ from both sides.**

$$\sin\theta = \mu_s \cos\theta$$

**Step 7: Divide both sides by $\cos\theta$ (assuming $\cos\theta \neq 0$).**

$$\frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \mu_s$$

This gives the elegant result:
$$\tan\theta = \mu_s$$

**Step 8: Solve for the angle of repose.**

$$\boxed{\theta = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The angle of repose of a food material is numerically equal to the arctangent of the static coefficient of friction between the particles. The larger the inter-particle friction, the steeper the stable pile.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Geometric Relationship in the Poured Cone Method

In the standard laboratory poured-cone method, granular food is allowed to fall freely from a fixed funnel onto a flat circular base plate. The material forms a conical pile.

**Step 1: Construct the geometry of the cone.**

The pile has:
- Base radius $r$ (in metres)
- Vertical height $h$ (in metres)
- Slant surface making angle $\theta$ with the horizontal

**Step 2: Apply the definition of the tangent function in the right triangle formed by the height, radius, and slant.**

$$\tan\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Adjacent side}} = \frac{h}{r}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the angle of repose.**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{r}\right)$$

**Step 4: Express the height in terms of angle and radius (useful for design).**

$$h = r \tan\theta$$

**Step 5: Express the angle in terms of the base diameter $D$ (since $D = 2r$).**

Substituting $r = D/2$:
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{D/2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2h}{D}\right)$$

**Step 6: Calculate the volume of the conical pile (useful for bulk density and porosity calculations).**

$$V_{cone} = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h$$

**Step 7: Worked Numerical Example.**

> **Problem:** A heap of corn flour is formed by the poured-cone method. The pile has a base diameter of **120 mm** and a height of **45 mm**. Calculate the angle of repose.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Convert all dimensions to consistent units (metres):
$$D = 0.120 \text{ m}, \quad h = 0.045 \text{ m}$$

Step 2: Calculate the base radius:
$$r = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{0.120}{2} = 0.060 \text{ m}$$

Step 3: Apply the angle of repose formula:
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{r}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.045}{0.060}\right)$$

Step 4: Compute the ratio:
$$\frac{0.045}{0.060} = 0.750$$

Step 5: Apply the inverse tangent:
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.750) = 36.87°$$

**Final Answer:** The angle of repose of the corn flour is **≈ 36.9°**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Factors Affecting the Angle of Repose

| **Factor** | **Effect on θ** | **Reason** | **KTU-relevant Food Examples** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| ↑ **Particle size** | ↓ Decreases | Larger particles have lower surface area per unit mass → less cohesion | Coarse sugar vs. icing sugar |
| ↑ **Surface roughness** | ↑ Increases | Rougher surfaces interlock more | Brown rice vs. polished rice |
| ↑ **Moisture content** | ↑ Increases | Water forms liquid bridges → capillary cohesion | Wet wheat vs. dry wheat |
| ↑ **Particle sphericity** | ↓ Decreases | Spherical particles roll easily | Peas vs. elongated rice |
| ↑ **Density of particles** | ↑ Slight increase | Higher inertial mass pressing down | Whole spices vs. hollow cereals |
| ↑ **Fat/Oil content** | ↓ Decreases (lubricating) | Acts as a lubricant between particles | Cocoa powder vs. roasted coffee |
| ↑ **Electrostatic charge** | ↑ Increases | Charged particles attract each other | Spray-dried milk powder |
| ↑ **Fines content (very small particles)** | ↑ Increases | High cohesion due to van der Waals forces | Wheat flour vs. wheat bran |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Whenever you report an angle of repose value in an exam or lab report, **always mention the moisture content and particle size**. A naked number is incomplete and may lose **1–2 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Classification of Angle of Repose Measurement Methods

The following Mermaid diagram provides a **block-level functional architecture flow** of the four primary methods used in food engineering laboratories to determine the angle of repose. This serves as a schematic representation of the procedural decision tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Sample of Food Material Ready] --> B{Method Selection Based on Sample Form}
    B --> C1[Method 1: Poured Cone Method]
    B --> C2[Method 2: Fixed Funnel Method]
    B --> C3[Method 3: Tilting Box Method]
    B --> C4[Method 4: Revolving Cylinder Method]

    C1 --> D1[Material dropped from fixed funnel]
    D1 --> E1[Forms natural conical heap on base]
    E1 --> F1[Measure height h and base diameter D]
    F1 --> G1[Compute theta equals arctan of 2h over D]

    C2 --> D2[Funnel fixed at known height above base]
    D2 --> E2[Material flows and forms cone]
    E2 --> F2[Height of cone measured by ruler]
    F2 --> G2[Base radius measured]
    G2 --> H2[Compute theta equals arctan of h over r]

    C3 --> D3[Box partially filled with material]
    D3 --> E3[Box tilted slowly until material slides]
    E3 --> F3[Angle of tilt at which sliding begins is the repose angle]
    F3 --> G3[Direct reading from protractor]

    C4 --> D4[Horizontal cylinder half-filled with material]
    D4 --> E4[Cylinder rotated slowly about its axis]
    E4 --> F4[Material surface forms an angle as it cascades]
    F4 --> G4[Angle measured as theta]

    G1 --> Z[END: Angle of Repose theta in degrees]
    G2 --> Z
    G3 --> Z
    G4 --> Z
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Force Balance on a Particle at the Verge of Sliding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Food Particle on Slope] --> W[Weight W equals mg acting vertically down]
    P --> N[Normal Force N perpendicular to slope]
    P --> F[Friction Force f opposing slide]

    W --> WP[Resolved Component: mg sin theta along slope]
    W --> WN[Resolved Component: mg cos theta normal to slope]

    WP --> EQ[At critical equilibrium: mg sin theta equals mu times N]
    WN --> NREL[N equals mg cos theta]
    NREL --> EQ
    F --> EQ
    EQ --> RES[Result: tan theta equals mu_s]
    RES --> FIN[Therefore theta equals arctan of mu_s equals Angle of Repose]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Factors Influencing the Angle of Repose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AOR[Angle of Repose of Food Material] --> F1[Particle Properties]
    AOR --> F2[Environmental Conditions]
    AOR --> F3[Process Conditions]

    F1 --> F1a[Particle Size]
    F1 --> F1b[Particle Shape]
    F1 --> F1c[Surface Roughness]
    F1 --> F1d[Moisture Content]
    F1 --> F1e[Fat or Oil Content]

    F2 --> F2a[Ambient Humidity]
    F2 --> F2b[Storage Temperature]
    F2 --> F2c[Static Electricity]

    F3 --> F3a[Method of Measurement]
    F3 --> F3b[Drop Height]
    F3 --> F3c[Funnel Orifice Diameter]
    F3 --> F3d[Vibration during Piling]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** Each of these block diagrams is a **functional mapping**, not a literal physical picture. For a real cone-shaped pile drawing, consult your lab manual. The Mermaid flowcharts above give KTU-credit-worthy **system-level understanding**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "angle of repose" as applied to food materials. List any four factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The angle of repose is the angle between the surface of a heap of granular food material and the horizontal plane, measured when the heap is at the point of incipient flow or sliding.

**Four factors (2 Marks — ½ Mark each):**
1. Moisture content of the material
2. Particle size and distribution
3. Surface roughness of particles
4. Particle shape and sphericity

*(Acceptable alternatives: fat content, electrostatic charge, method of measurement, ambient humidity.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the geometric formula used to calculate the angle of repose in the "poured cone method" and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Formula (1.5 Marks):**
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{r}\right)$$

**Meaning of terms (1.5 Marks — ½ Mark each):**
- **θ** = Angle of repose, measured in degrees
- **h** = Vertical height of the conical pile formed by the material (in metres or millimetres)
- **r** = Radius of the circular base of the pile (in metres or millimetres)

---

### 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the angle of repose (θ) and the static coefficient of friction (μₛ) for a granular food material, starting from a free-body diagram of a single particle on the verge of sliding down an inclined pile. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A heap of turmeric powder is formed using the poured cone method on a circular base plate of diameter 100 mm. The height of the cone is measured as 40 mm. Calculate: (i) the angle of repose, and (ii) the volume of the conical pile. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Solution to A(a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. The particle is treated as a rigid, point-like body.
2. The slope surface is planar and smooth.
3. Only static friction and gravity are considered (cohesion is neglected for simplicity).
4. The particle is at the verge of sliding, i.e., the friction force is at its maximum static value.

**Free-Body Diagram (1 Mark):** Draw a particle on an inclined plane showing:
- Weight $W = mg$ vertically downward
- Normal reaction $N$ perpendicular to the slope
- Static friction force $f_s$ acting up the slope

**Resolution of Weight (1 Mark):**

The weight is resolved into two components:
- Perpendicular to slope: $mg \cos\theta$
- Parallel to slope: $mg \sin\theta$

**Equilibrium Perpendicular to Slope (1 Mark):**

$$N = mg \cos\theta$$

**Limiting Friction Condition (1 Mark):**

At the verge of sliding:
$$f_s = \mu_s N = \mu_s mg \cos\theta$$

**Equilibrium Along the Slope (1 Mark):**

$$mg \sin\theta = \mu_s mg \cos\theta$$

**Final Result (1 Mark):**

After cancelling $mg$ and rearranging:
$$\tan\theta = \mu_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$$

**Interpretation:** The angle of repose is numerically equal to the inverse tangent of the static friction coefficient of the material.

---

**Solution to A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- Base diameter $D = 100$ mm = $0.100$ m
- Height of cone $h = 40$ mm = $0.040$ m

**Step 1: Compute the base radius (1 Mark):**

$$r = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{0.100}{2} = 0.050 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Apply the angle of repose formula (2 Marks):**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{r}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.040}{0.050}\right)$$

**Step 3: Compute the ratio (1 Mark):**

$$\frac{0.040}{0.050} = 0.800$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the inverse tangent (1 Mark):**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.800) = 38.66°$$

**(i) Final Answer:** $\theta \approx \mathbf{38.7°}$ (1 Mark)

**Step 5: Compute the volume of the cone (1 Mark):**

$$V = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h = \frac{1}{3} \times \pi \times (0.050)^2 \times 0.040$$

$$V = \frac{1}{3} \times 3.1416 \times 0.0025 \times 0.040 = 1.047 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) Final Answer:** $V \approx \mathbf{1.05 \times 10^{-4} \ m^3}$ **= 104.7 cm³** (1 Mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **poured cone (fixed funnel) method** for the determination of the angle of repose of a food powder. State the formula used, the procedure, and two advantages and two limitations of this method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how the **moisture content** and **particle size** of a food material affect its angle of repose. Illustrate with two suitable food examples in each case. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Solution to B(a) — Poured Cone Method (7 Marks):**

**Principle (1 Mark):** A known mass of food material is allowed to flow through a fixed funnel and form a natural conical pile on a flat circular base. The angle that the slant surface of the cone makes with the horizontal is the angle of repose.

**Apparatus (1 Mark):** Fixed funnel with a known orifice diameter, a flat circular base plate (often with concentric circles for visual aid), a measuring scale or ruler, and the food sample.

**Procedure (2 Marks):**
1. The food material is filled in the funnel, which is fixed at a known height (typically 50–100 mm) above the base plate.
2. The material is allowed to flow freely from the funnel until a stable cone is formed and the flow stops naturally.
3. The height $h$ of the cone is measured using a calibrated scale from the apex to the base.
4. The base diameter $D$ (or radius $r$) is measured.
5. The angle of repose is calculated using the formula:
   $$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2h}{D}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{r}\right)$$

**Advantages (1.5 Marks — ¾ Mark each):**
1. **Simple and inexpensive** — requires only basic glassware and a ruler; ideal for routine quality control in small and medium food industries.
2. **Quick** — a single measurement can be completed in under 5 minutes, allowing multiple replicates.

**Limitations (1.5 Marks — ¾ Mark each):**
1. **Operator-dependent** — the drop height, vibration, and rate of pouring can vary between operators, leading to poor reproducibility.
2. **Not suitable for cohesive powders** — very fine, sticky food powders (e.g., wet cocoa, sticky rice flour) may not form a stable cone and may bridge inside the funnel.

---

**Solution to B(b) — Effect of Moisture and Particle Size (7 Marks):**

**Effect of Moisture Content (3.5 Marks):**

- As the moisture content of a food material increases, a thin film of liquid water forms around each particle, creating **liquid bridges** between adjacent particles through **capillary action**.
- These liquid bridges exert an attractive force that **holds the particles together** more strongly than dry friction alone.
- The **cohesive force** added by moisture resists the down-slope gravitational pull, allowing the pile to be **steeper** before sliding.
- **Therefore, the angle of repose INCREASES with increasing moisture content.**

**Food Example 1 (1 Mark):** **Wheat grains** — dry wheat (≈10% moisture) has an angle of repose of about 27°, but at 18% moisture, the angle rises to about 35° due to increased surface stickiness.

**Food Example 2 (1 Mark):** **Granulated sugar** — dry sugar crystals flow freely with θ ≈ 35°; at elevated humidity (when the surface becomes slightly sticky), θ can rise above 45°.

*(Acceptable alternative examples: rice, soybean, ground spices, milk powder, instant coffee.)*

**Effect of Particle Size (3.5 Marks):**

- As the particle size **decreases**, the **specific surface area** (surface area per unit mass) **increases dramatically**.
- A larger specific surface area means more points of contact and more surface for **van der Waals forces, electrostatic forces, and capillary bridges** to act.
- Fine particles also tend to interlock more and have **lower flowability**.
- **Therefore, the angle of repose INCREASES as particle size decreases.**

**Food Example 1 (1 Mark):** **Wheat flour (fine)** has an angle of repose of about 50–55°, whereas **whole wheat kernels** (coarse) have an angle of repose of only about 27°.

**Food Example 2 (1 Mark):** **Icing sugar (very fine, ~10 μm)** has a high angle of repose (≈ 55–60°) and tends to clump, while **coarse table sugar (≈ 500 μm)** has a moderate angle of repose (≈ 35°).

*(Acceptable alternative examples: corn flour vs. corn kernels, salt grades, tea dust vs. tea leaves.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Angle of Repose Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit (° or rad).** Always write "**θ = 38.7°**" — not just "θ = 38.7". This is a guaranteed **½ to 1 mark** deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up angle with horizontal and vertical.** The angle of repose is measured from the **horizontal**, never from the vertical. Students sometimes write $\tan\theta = r/h$ — this is **wrong** and loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping the conversion to consistent units.** Mixing mm and cm in the same formula is a classic arithmetic error. Always convert to **metres** (or all to mm) before substituting.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** in the derivation question. Examiners explicitly look for "rigid body," "planar slope," "no cohesion" assumptions. Skipping these costs **1 mark** easily.
> 5. **Reporting angle of repose without mentioning moisture content.** Food materials are **hygroscopic**, and their θ changes with moisture. Always report θ **with** moisture content (% w.b.).
> 6. **Confusing "poured cone" with "tilting box" methods.** These are two **different** experimental techniques. Mixing them up in a method-explanation question will cost **2–3 marks**.
> 7. **Not drawing the free-body diagram** in derivation questions. A clear diagram is worth **1 mark** by itself; skipping it is a self-inflicted loss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Angle of Repose**

- ✅ **Definition:** Maximum angle (from horizontal) of a stable granular pile.
- ✅ **Unit:** Degrees (°); typical food range: **20° – 55°**.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $\theta = \tan^{-1}(h/r)$ or $\theta = \tan^{-1}(2h/D)$.
- ✅ **Friction linkage:** $\tan\theta = \mu_s \Rightarrow \theta = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$.
- ✅ **Four standard methods:** Poured cone, Fixed funnel, Tilting box, Revolving cylinder.
- ✅ **Most-used method in KTU labs:** **Poured cone method** (simple, fast).
- ✅ **Volume of pile:** $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$ (useful for bulk density calculations).
- ✅ **Key factors that INCREASE θ:** ↑ moisture, ↑ surface roughness, ↓ particle size, ↑ fines, ↑ electrostatic charge.
- ✅ **Key factors that DECREASE θ:** ↑ particle size, ↑ sphericity, ↑ fat content (lubrication), ↓ moisture.
- ✅ **Industrial relevance:** Hopper wall angle must be **> θ** to ensure mass flow and prevent arching/ratholing.
- ✅ **Always report θ with:** moisture content (% w.b.), particle size (mm), and method used.
- ✅ **Volume calculation pitfall:** Use $r$ (not $D$) in the cone volume formula; $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$ — **not** $\pi (D/2)^2 h$ directly without squaring.
- ✅ **Examiner's pet topic:** Drawing the **free-body diagram** with correctly resolved weight components on a 7-mark derivation question.

---

**END OF NOTES — Angle of Repose (PEFTT526, Module 2)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angle of internal friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT526 - ENGINEERING PROPERTIES OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Angle of internal friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Angle of Internal Friction — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Angle of Internal Friction** (denoted by the Greek letter **φ**, phi) is a fundamental engineering property of food materials — particularly **granular, particulate, and powdered foods** (e.g., rice, wheat, maize, flour, sugar, pulses, oilseeds, spices) — that quantifies the **shear resistance** developed between particles of the material when subjected to an applied normal load.

In the framework of the **Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion**, the angle of internal friction is defined as the angle whose **tangent equals the coefficient of internal friction (μᵢ)** of the bulk solid:

$$\tan(\phi) = \mu_i$$

It represents the **frictional interlocking** between individual particles within a bulk granular mass and is a critical parameter for designing **storage silos, hoppers, bins, conveying systems, and packaging machinery**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Definition (Use this verbatim):**
> *The angle of internal friction (φ) of a food material is the angle between the resultant force and the normal force acting at the point of contact between particles, such that the material begins to fail in shear when the applied shear stress equals the normal stress multiplied by tan(φ).*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sand Pile on a Beach" Intuition

Imagine you are at the beach, slowly pouring dry sand from your hand. As the sand accumulates, it forms a **conical pile**. The slope of this pile's surface (measured from the horizontal) is called the **angle of repose** — and for **dry, non-cohesive granular materials**, this angle is numerically very close to the **angle of internal friction**.

Now, here is the deeper intuition:

- If the sand particles are **rough, angular, and interlock well** (like crushed wheat kernels or broken rice), the pile stands **steep** → **high φ** (high internal friction).
- If the particles are **smooth, round, and roll easily** (like polished chickpeas or mustard seeds), the pile spreads out **flat** → **low φ** (low internal friction).
- If the sand is **wet**, surface tension between particles adds **cohesion (c)**, allowing the pile to stand even steeper than the pure friction limit would allow.

In food engineering terms: **φ tells you "how steeply can you pile this food before it collapses and starts flowing?"** This directly dictates how steep the walls of a **grain silo** or **hopper** can be.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Angle of Internal Friction (φ):** A property of the *bulk granular mass* — internal particle-to-particle friction.
> - **Angle of Repose (θ):** A property of the *piled material* on a flat surface — often used as an **indirect estimator** of φ.
> - **Angle of External Friction (φ'):** A property of the *material against a wall surface* (e.g., wheat against a steel silo wall) — used in **wall friction calculations** for hopper design.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Food Grains) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Angle of Internal Friction | **φ** | **25°–45°** (typical for cereals) | degrees (°) or radians (rad) |
| Coefficient of Internal Friction | **μᵢ = tan φ** | 0.466 – 1.000 | dimensionless |
| Standard Test Moisture (dry basis) | m.c. | 10% – 14% | % w.b. |
| Bulk Density (for context) | ρb | 600 – 850 | kg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard unit of φ in KTU examinations is **degrees (°)**. Always convert to radians only if performing trigonometric calculations. Conversion factor: $1° = \pi/180 \approx 0.01745$ rad.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mohr–Coulomb Failure Criterion — The Governing Framework

The angle of internal friction is defined operationally through the **Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion**, which states that a granular material fails (begins to flow plastically) when the **shear stress (τ)** on a potential failure plane reaches a critical value given by:

$$\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$$

Where:
- **τf** = Shear stress at failure (Pa or N/m²)
- **c** = Cohesion of the material (Pa or N/m²) — represents inter-particle bonding (e.g., due to moisture, fat, or electrostatic forces)
- **σn** = Normal (compressive) stress on the failure plane (Pa or N/m²)
- **φ** = **Angle of internal friction** (degrees)
- **tan(φ)** = Coefficient of internal friction (dimensionless)

### Logic Breakdown — Step by Step:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the failure plane:** When a granular food mass is stressed (e.g., gravity load in a silo), an internal plane of weakness develops along which sliding can occur.
2. **Step 2 — Decompose forces on the failure plane:** The force on this plane is resolved into a **normal component (σn)** pushing particles together, and a **shear component (τ)** trying to slide them past each other.
3. **Step 3 — Define the failure condition:** Sliding initiates when τ overcomes the frictional and cohesive resistance. Frictional resistance = σn · tan(φ). Cohesive resistance = c.
4. **Step 4 — Cohesionless simplification:** For most **dry food grains** (rice, wheat, maize), c ≈ 0, reducing the criterion to:
5. **Step 5 — Physical interpretation of φ:** A larger φ means particles grip each other more strongly and resist sliding, requiring a higher shear-to-normal stress ratio to initiate flow.

$$\tau_f = \sigma_n \tan(\phi) \quad \text{(for cohesionless food grains)}$$

---

## 2.2 Relationship Between Angle of Internal Friction and Angle of Repose

For **cohesionless, free-flowing granular foods**, a widely used engineering approximation is:

$$\phi \approx \theta_r$$

Where **θr** is the **angle of repose**. This relationship holds because in a freely poured pile, the only resistance to flow is inter-particle friction, which is governed by the same angle φ.

> [!NOTE]
> The angle of repose is an **approximate, indirect method** of estimating φ. For precise design (e.g., mass-flow silo design per **Jenike's method**), direct shear testing using a **Jenike shear cell** or a **direct shear apparatus** is mandatory in KTU 2024 Scheme lab work.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all examinable formulas** related to the angle of internal friction. Master these before attempting any numerical problem.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$ | Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion (general) | Pa | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $\tau_f = \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$ | Mohr–Coulomb for cohesionless grains (c = 0) | Pa | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $\mu_i = \tan(\phi)$ | Definition: coefficient of internal friction | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $\phi \approx \theta_r$ | Approximate equivalence with angle of repose (cohesionless case) | degrees | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c\,\tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Major principal stress at failure (Mohr–Coulomb in 2D stress space) | Pa | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $\tau = \mu_i \sigma_n$ | Shear stress under Coulomb friction model | Pa | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{W \cos(\alpha)}{A}$ | Normal stress on inclined failure plane (W = weight, α = plane angle) | Pa | ⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the angle of internal friction is **not just academic** — it directly governs:

- **Silo and Hopper Design (Jenike's Method):** The **hopper half-angle** must be sufficiently steep (typically greater than the angle of internal friction) to ensure **mass flow** (all material moves during discharge). If the hopper is too shallow, **arching** or **rat-holing** occurs, leading to flow stoppages and spoilage of stored grains.
- **Conveyor Inclination:** The maximum inclination angle of a **belt or screw conveyor** carrying grain cannot exceed φ, otherwise the material slides back.
- **Bin Loads:** Lateral pressures on silo walls depend on φ — incorrect φ values lead to **structural collapse** of storage structures.
- **Mixing and Blending:** In **powder mixers** (flour, spice blends), inter-particle friction affects mixing homogeneity and power consumption.
- **Pneumatic Conveying:** Pressure drops in pneumatic transport lines of flour/sugar depend on the wall friction angle, which is related to φ.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Connect:** Kerala's rice mills, coconut copra storage, and spice processing units (Cardamom, Pepper in Idukki; Cashew in Kannur) routinely apply φ-based hopper design to prevent spoilage from stagnant zones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mohr–Coulomb Failure Criterion for Food Grains

### Problem Setup:
A mass of wheat grains (treated as a cohesionless bulk solid) rests on an inclined surface making angle α with the horizontal. A block of grain of weight W rests on a horizontal failure plane of area A. Derive the expression for the shear stress at incipient failure and express the condition for sliding in terms of the angle of internal friction.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram and Force Resolution:**

Consider a grain block of weight **W** resting on an inclined failure plane. The weight acts vertically downward. Resolve W into two components:
- Normal to the failure plane: $W_n = W \cos(\alpha)$
- Parallel to the failure plane: $W_t = W \sin(\alpha)$

where α is the angle of the failure plane with the horizontal.

**Step 2 — Compute Stresses on the Failure Plane:**

The normal stress σn and shear stress τ on the failure plane of area A are:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{W \cos(\alpha)}{A}$$

$$\tau = \frac{W \sin(\alpha)}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Coulomb Friction Model:**

The shear resistance offered by inter-particle friction is:

$$\tau_{resistance} = \mu_i \cdot \sigma_n = \tan(\phi) \cdot \sigma_n$$

Sliding (failure) occurs when the applied shear stress equals or exceeds the resistance:

$$\tau \geq \tau_{resistance}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the Stress Expressions:**

$$\frac{W \sin(\alpha)}{A} \geq \tan(\phi) \cdot \frac{W \cos(\alpha)}{A}$$

Cancel W and A from both sides:

$$\sin(\alpha) \geq \tan(\phi) \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

**Step 5 — Trigonometric Simplification:**

Divide both sides by cos(α):

$$\frac{\sin(\alpha)}{\cos(\alpha)} \geq \tan(\phi)$$

$$\tan(\alpha) \geq \tan(\phi)$$

Since tan is a monotonically increasing function in the first quadrant (0° to 90°):

$$\alpha \geq \phi$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation:**

> The block of grain will begin to slide along the failure plane **only when the inclination of the failure plane equals or exceeds the angle of internal friction φ.** The angle φ is therefore literally the **critical angle** at which inter-particle sliding is initiated.

**Step 7 — Generalization to Cohesive Foods (c ≠ 0):**

For materials with cohesion (e.g., moist flour, wet masa, sticky rice), the failure condition becomes:

$$\tau = c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$$

Substituting the stress expressions:

$$\frac{W \sin(\alpha)}{A} = c + \frac{W \cos(\alpha)}{A} \tan(\phi)$$

Solving for the critical angle αc (the angle at which sliding begins for a cohesive material):

$$W \sin(\alpha_c) = cA + W \cos(\alpha_c) \tan(\phi)$$

This can be rewritten as:

$$\alpha_c = \phi + \arcsin\!\left(\frac{cA}{W}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cohesion term **cA** acts as an "extra resistance" that allows cohesive food materials to remain stable on inclines steeper than φ — explaining why wet sand (or wet rice) can form steeper piles than dry sand.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

### **Question:**
A direct shear test is conducted on a sample of dry rice. The normal load applied is **N = 50 N** on a shear box of cross-sectional area **A = 100 cm² = 0.01 m²**. The shear force at failure is measured as **T = 36.5 N**. The cohesion is negligible (c = 0). Determine:
(i) The normal and shear stresses at failure.
(ii) The coefficient of internal friction μᵢ.
(iii) The angle of internal friction φ.

### **Complete Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Normal load, N = 50 N
- Shear area, A = 0.01 m²
- Shear force at failure, T = 36.5 N
- Cohesion, c = 0

**Part (i) — Compute Stresses:**

Normal stress:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{50 \text{ N}}{0.01 \text{ m}^2} = 5000 \text{ Pa} = 5 \text{ kPa}$$

Shear stress at failure:

$$\tau_f = \frac{T}{A} = \frac{36.5 \text{ N}}{0.01 \text{ m}^2} = 3650 \text{ Pa} = 3.65 \text{ kPa}$$

**Part (ii) — Coefficient of Internal Friction:**

Since c = 0, from Mohr–Coulomb:

$$\mu_i = \tan(\phi) = \frac{\tau_f}{\sigma_n} = \frac{3650 \text{ Pa}}{5000 \text{ Pa}} = 0.73$$

**Part (iii) — Angle of Internal Friction:**

$$\phi = \arctan(\mu_i) = \arctan(0.73)$$

$$\phi = 36.16° \approx 36.2°$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value of φ = 36.2° is well within the typical range of **25°–45°** reported for dry cereal grains in standard food engineering literature (e.g., ASAE Standards for grain properties).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How to Identify Which Formula to Use

| Scenario Given in Problem | Formula to Use | Key Steps |
|---|---|---|
| Direct shear test data (N, T, A, c given) | $\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{\tau_f - c}{\sigma_n}$ | Compute stresses first, then apply Mohr–Coulomb |
| Triaxial test data (σ₁, σ₃, c given) | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45° + \phi/2\right) + 2c\tan\!\left(45° + \phi/2\right)$ | Substitute σ₁, σ₃, c; solve for φ iteratively |
| Only angle of repose is measured | $\phi \approx \theta_r$ | Valid only for cohesionless, dry, free-flowing grains |
| Bulk solid in a hopper (mass flow check) | Compare hopper half-angle δ with φ | Mass flow if δ > φ; funnel flow if δ < φ |
| Failure plane inclination problem | Derive critical angle αc = φ (for c = 0) | Resolve weight, apply Coulomb model |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Framework of Angle of Internal Friction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bulk Food Material\nGrains, Flour, Sugar, Spices] --> B[Particle-Level Properties]
    B --> B1[Particle Size]
    B --> B2[Particle Shape]
    B --> B3[Surface Roughness]
    B --> B4[Moisture Content]
    A --> C[Bulk-Level Properties]
    C --> C1[Internal Friction]
    C --> C2[Cohesion c]
    C --> C3[Compressibility]
    C1 --> D[Angle of Internal Friction phi]
    C2 --> D
    B1 --> D
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    D --> E[Measurement Methods]
    E --> E1[Direct Shear Test]
    E --> E2[Jenike Shear Cell]
    E --> E3[Triaxial Test]
    E --> E4[Angle of Repose Method]
    D --> F[Engineering Applications]
    F --> F1[Silo and Hopper Design]
    F --> F2[Conveyor Angle Selection]
    F --> F3[Bin Wall Pressure]
    F --> F4[Pneumatic Conveying]
    F --> F5[Mixing and Blending]
    A --> G[Operational Conditions]
    G --> G1[Normal Stress sigma_n]
    G --> G2[Strain Rate]
    G --> G3[Temperature]
    G --> D
```

**Reading Guide for the Diagram:**
- The **left block (B, C)** lists the *intrinsic material properties* that govern φ.
- The **right block (F)** lists the *downstream engineering applications* that depend on φ.
- The **bottom block (G)** shows *operational conditions* that modulate φ during processing.
- The **central node (D)** is the angle of internal friction — the integration point between material science and engineering design.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Mohr–Coulomb Failure Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Normal Stress axis\nsigma_n] --> B[Linear Envelope\ntau = c + sigma_n tan phi]
    B --> C[Shear Stress axis\ntau]
    C --> D[Slope of line = tan phi]
    D --> E[phi = Angle of Internal Friction]
    B --> F[y-intercept = Cohesion c]
    F --> G[For c = 0: line passes through origin\nPurely cohesionless grains]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The Mohr–Coulomb envelope is a **straight line** in the (σn, τ) plane.
- The **slope** of this line equals tan(φ) — directly giving the angle of internal friction.
- The **y-intercept** equals c — the cohesion. For most dry grains, c ≈ 0, so the line passes through the origin.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Measurement Method Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Need to measure Angle of Internal Friction?] --> Q2{Is the food\ndry and free-flowing?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Use Angle of Repose Method\nphi approx theta_r\nQuick, approximate]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is the food\ncohesive or moist?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R2[Use Direct Shear Test\nASTM D3080\nMeasures c and phi]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need design data\nfor silos or hoppers?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R3[Use Jenike Shear Cell\nIndustry standard for bulk solids]
    Q4 -->|No| R4[Use Triaxial Test\nCD or CU condition\nMost rigorous data]
    R1 --> S[Report phi in degrees]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the angle of internal friction. How is it related to the coefficient of internal friction of a food material?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The angle of internal friction (φ) is the angle between the resultant inter-particle contact force and the normal force at the point of contact, such that the material begins to fail in shear when the applied shear stress equals the normal stress multiplied by tan(φ).
- **(1 mark)** It is mathematically related to the coefficient of internal friction (μᵢ) by: tan(φ) = μᵢ. This is the slope of the Mohr–Coulomb failure envelope in the (σn, τ) plane.
- **(1 mark)** For most dry food grains (e.g., rice, wheat, maize), μᵢ ranges from **0.4 to 1.0**, corresponding to φ values of approximately **22° to 45°**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between the angle of internal friction (φ) and the angle of repose (θr) for a granular food material.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The **angle of internal friction (φ)** is a fundamental property of the *bulk granular mass* under defined normal stress conditions, measured directly via shear tests (e.g., Jenike shear cell). It is the angle whose tangent is the coefficient of internal friction between particles.
- **(1 mark)** The **angle of repose (θr)** is the angle of the free surface of a granular heap formed by pouring the material onto a horizontal surface. It is measured geometrically.
- **(1 mark)** For **cohesionless, dry, free-flowing** food grains, φ ≈ θr. However, for **cohesive or moist** foods, θr > φ because cohesion contributes to the stability of the heap, so angle of repose alone is unreliable for engineering design.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** State and explain the **Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion** for food materials. Clearly define all the terms involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a direct shear test on a sample of **dry maize (corn)**, the following data were recorded at failure:

| Trial | Normal Load N (N) | Shear Force T (N) | Shear Area A (m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 18.0 | 0.005 |
| 2 | 60 | 34.0 | 0.005 |
| 3 | 90 | 48.0 | 0.005 |
| 4 | 120 | 64.0 | 0.005 |

The cohesion c is negligible. Determine the **angle of internal friction φ** of the maize sample using graphical and analytical methods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Mohr–Coulomb Failure Criterion — Model Solution (7 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** **Statement:** The Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion states that failure (incipient sliding) in a granular material occurs along a plane when the shear stress τ on that plane reaches a critical value:
- **(1 mark)** **Equation (general form):** $\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$, where τf = shear stress at failure, c = cohesion, σn = normal stress on the failure plane, φ = angle of internal friction.
- **(1 mark)** **Physical meaning of each term:** c represents the inherent inter-particle bonding (electrostatic, moisture-induced, or fat-mediated in foods). σn·tan(φ) represents the frictional resistance, which is proportional to the normal stress.
- **(1 mark)** **Simplified form (c = 0):** For dry grains, $\tau_f = \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$. The Mohr–Coulomb envelope is a straight line through the origin with slope tan(φ).
- **(1 mark)** **2D/3D generalization:** In principal stress space, the failure condition becomes: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2(45° + \phi/2) + 2c \tan(45° + \phi/2)$, where σ₁ and σ₃ are major and minor principal stresses.
- **(1 mark)** **Significance for food materials:** This criterion is the foundation of **Jenike's hopper design** method, used to determine the minimum hopper angle for mass flow of grains, flour, and powdered foods.
- **(1 mark)** **Validity:** Valid for **cohesionless to moderately cohesive** food powders and grains under quasi-static loading conditions. Not valid for very fine powders (< 10 μm) where van der Waals forces dominate.

**(b) Direct Shear Test Data Analysis — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute Normal and Shear Stresses for Each Trial:**

Since A = 0.005 m² is constant for all trials, compute σn = N/A and τf = T/A:

| Trial | σn = N/A (Pa) | τf = T/A (Pa) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30/0.005 = 6000 | 18.0/0.005 = 3600 |
| 2 | 60/0.005 = 12000 | 34.0/0.005 = 6800 |
| 3 | 90/0.005 = 18000 | 48.0/0.005 = 9600 |
| 4 | 120/0.005 = 24000 | 64.0/0.005 = 12800 |

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Apply the Linear Regression (Best-Fit Line) Since c = 0:**

For c = 0, the Mohr–Coulomb envelope passes through the origin: τf = σn · tan(φ).

The best-fit slope (forced through origin) is:

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{\sum \sigma_n \tau_f}{\sum \sigma_n^2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Calculate the Sums:**

Numerator:

$$\sum \sigma_n \tau_f = (6000)(3600) + (12000)(6800) + (18000)(9600) + (24000)(12800)$$

$$= 21.6 \times 10^6 + 81.6 \times 10^6 + 172.8 \times 10^6 + 307.2 \times 10^6 = 583.2 \times 10^6$$

Denominator:

$$\sum \sigma_n^2 = (6000)^2 + (12000)^2 + (18000)^2 + (24000)^2$$

$$= 36 \times 10^6 + 144 \times 10^6 + 324 \times 10^6 + 576 \times 10^6 = 1080 \times 10^6$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Compute tan(φ):**

$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{583.2 \times 10^6}{1080 \times 10^6} = 0.54$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Compute φ:**

$$\phi = \arctan(0.54) = 28.4°$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Verify with Each Trial (Consistency Check):**

| Trial | τf / σn | φ = arctan(τf/σn) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3600/6000 = 0.600 | 31.0° |
| 2 | 6800/12000 = 0.567 | 29.5° |
| 3 | 9600/18000 = 0.533 | 28.1° |
| 4 | 12800/24000 = 0.533 | 28.1° |

The slight variation confirms the linear Mohr–Coulomb model. **Final answer: φ ≈ 28.4°** for dry maize, consistent with ASAE-published values of 25°–32° for shelled corn.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of the angle of internal friction** for granular food materials with a suitable real-world analogy. Discuss any three factors that affect the angle of internal friction of a food material. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical silo of diameter **D = 4 m** stores **wheat** with bulk density **ρb = 750 kg/m³** and angle of internal friction **φ = 30°**. The wheat height is **H = 10 m**. Using **Janssen's equation**, calculate the **vertical pressure σv** at the bottom of the silo. Take the Janssen coefficient K = 0.5 and the radius of the silo R = 2 m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Concept of Angle of Internal Friction — Model Solution (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** **Definition with analogy:** The angle of internal friction (φ) is the angle that defines the shear strength of a bulk granular food material under applied normal stress. *Real-world analogy:* Imagine pouring dry rice onto a table. The rice forms a conical pile whose slope is the **angle of repose** (≈ φ for dry rice). If you try to push the rice sideways with a paddle, you must overcome the frictional interlocking between rice grains — this resistance is quantified by tan(φ), the coefficient of internal friction. The steeper the angle φ, the "grippier" the material.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Factor 1 — Moisture Content:** As moisture content increases, a thin liquid film forms between particles, creating **cohesive capillary bridges**. This generally **increases** the effective shear strength initially (stickiness helps pile stability), but at very high moisture, water acts as a **lubricant**, reducing φ. For most grains, φ peaks at 12%–16% moisture content and then decreases.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Factor 2 — Particle Size and Shape:** Larger, **angular, and rough-surfaced** particles (e.g., broken rice, cracked wheat) exhibit **higher φ** due to mechanical interlocking. Smaller, **rounded, and smooth** particles (e.g., polished chickpeas, soybean) have **lower φ** because they roll and slide past each other easily.
- **(1 mark)** **Factor 3 — Normal Stress (Pressure Level):** φ is generally **not constant** with normal stress. For many food grains, φ **decreases slightly** as σn increases, due to particle crushing and surface smoothing under high pressure. This is captured by the **non-linear Mohr–Coulomb envelope** (curved instead of straight) for food powders.
- **(1 mark)** Additional valid factors (1 mark each if any of the following is mentioned): Temperature (affects fat content softening in oilseeds); Fat/Oil content (lubricates particles, reducing φ); Storage time (compaction increases contact area, may increase φ).

**(b) Janssen's Equation Calculation — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Janssen's Equation (standard form):**

$$\sigma_v = \frac{\rho_b \cdot g \cdot R}{\mu \, K} \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\mu \, K \, H}{R}\right)\right]$$

Where:
- σv = vertical pressure at depth H (Pa)
- ρb = bulk density = 750 kg/m³
- g = acceleration due to gravity = 9.81 m/s²
- R = hydraulic radius of silo = D/4 = 4/4 = 1 m (for a circular silo, R_hydraulic = D/4)
- μ = coefficient of wall friction (use tan(φ) as an approximation if wall friction data not given) = tan(30°) = 0.577
- K = Janssen's lateral pressure ratio (Janssen coefficient) = 0.5
- H = height of stored material = 10 m

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on R:** Different textbooks use different definitions. Here we use the **hydraulic radius** R = D/4 = 1 m for a circular cross-section. Some texts use R = D/2 (full radius). Both forms appear in KTU papers — check the formula sheet provided in the question.

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute the exponent:**

$$\frac{\mu K H}{R} = \frac{(0.577)(0.5)(10)}{1} = 2.885$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute the exponential term:**

$$\exp(-2.885) = 0.0559$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute the bracketed term:**

$$1 - \exp(-2.885) = 1 - 0.0559 = 0.9441$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Compute the numerator of the prefactor:**

$$\rho_b \cdot g \cdot R = 750 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 7357.5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Compute the denominator of the prefactor:**

$$\mu \cdot K = 0.577 \times 0.5 = 0.2885$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Compute σv:**

$$\sigma_v = \frac{7357.5}{0.2885} \times 0.9441$$

$$\sigma_v = 25504 \times 0.9441 = 24080 \text{ Pa} \approx 24.1 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Final Answer with Verification:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_v \approx 24.1 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Verification:** Compare with the hydrostatic pressure (no silo effect): $\sigma_{hydro} = \rho_b g H = 750 \times 9.81 \times 10 = 73,575$ Pa = 73.6 kPa. The Janssen value (24.1 kPa) is much lower, confirming that the **silo arching effect** (where walls carry part of the load) significantly reduces the floor pressure — this is the engineering significance of φ in silo design.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Angle of Internal Friction Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students often mix cm² and m² when computing stress from load and area. Always convert area to **m²** before computing stress in Pa. (1 m² = 10,000 cm²).
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Degrees to Radians:** When using trig functions in calculators, ensure the calculator is in **degree mode** (not radian mode) for KTU problems. A common error gives φ = 0.504 rad instead of 28.4°.
> 3. **Confusing φ with θr:** Examiners frequently award partial credit only if the student explicitly states the **assumption c = 0** and the **condition (cohesionless, dry, free-flowing)** before equating φ ≈ θr.
> 4. **Mohr–Coulomb Equation Errors:** Students sometimes write τf = c + σn / tan(φ) — this is WRONG. The correct form is τf = c + σn · tan(φ).
> 5. **In Triaxial Test Problems:** Do not forget to convert σ₃ to major stress correctly. The equation is $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2(45° + \phi/2) + 2c\tan(45° + \phi/2)$, not $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan(45° + \phi/2) + 2c\tan(45° + \phi/2)$ (the square is on the tan term, not the angle).
> 6. **Janssen's Equation R confusion:** Always clarify whether R = D/2 (geometric radius) or R = D/4 (hydraulic radius) — the choice depends on the formula version given in the question.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Angle of Internal Friction (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- 🔹 **Definition:** Angle φ is the angle whose tangent equals the coefficient of internal friction μᵢ = tan(φ). It quantifies inter-particle frictional resistance in a bulk granular food.
- 🔹 **Governing Equation (Mohr–Coulomb):** $\tau_f = c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$ — for dry grains, c = 0, so $\tau_f = \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$.
- 🔹 **Typical Range:** φ = **25°–45°** for most food grains; μᵢ = **0.4–1.0**.
- 🔹 **Approximate Equivalence:** For **cohesionless, free-flowing, dry** grains: **φ ≈ angle of repose (θr)**.
- 🔹 **NOT the Same As:** Angle of repose (θr) and angle of external/wall friction (φ') — these are related but distinct properties.
- 🔹 **Measurement Methods:** (1) Direct shear test, (2) Jenike shear cell, (3) Triaxial test, (4) Angle of repose method (indirect).
- 🔹 **Factors Increasing φ:** Angular particle shape, rough surface texture, moderate moisture (12%–16%), fine particle size.
- 🔹 **Factors Decreasing φ:** Smooth/rounded particles, high moisture (lubrication), high oil/fat content (lubrication), high temperature (fat softening in oilseeds).
- 🔹 **Engineering Applications:** (1) Silo and hopper half-angle design (mass flow if hopper half-angle > φ); (2) Maximum conveyor inclination; (3) Lateral bin wall pressure (Janssen's equation); (4) Powder mixing and pneumatic conveying.
- 🔹 **Standard Symbols:** φ (phi) for angle of internal friction; c for cohesion; σn for normal stress; τf for shear stress at failure; μᵢ for coefficient of internal friction.
- 🔹 **Janssen's Equation:** Used to compute **vertical silo pressure** considering wall friction — φ directly appears via μ = tan(φ) in the formula.
- 🔹 **Direct Shear Test Procedure:** Apply normal load → measure peak shear force → compute τf and σn → plot τf vs σn → slope = tan(φ), intercept = c.
- 🔹 **Critical Slip Condition:** A grain block on an inclined plane at angle α slides when α ≥ φ (for c = 0).
- 🔹 **Cohesion Effect:** Cohesive foods (moist flour, sticky rice) can pile at angles **steeper than φ** because c contributes additional stability.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, angle of internal friction problems almost always appear as either (a) a **direct shear test data interpretation** question (7 marks) or (b) a **Janssen's equation silo design** problem (7 marks). Practice both types thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure distribution in storage structures and compression chambers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT526 - ENGINEERING PROPERTIES OF FOOD MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure distribution in storage structures and compression chambers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Distribution in Storage Structures** is the study of how vertical, horizontal, and wall-normal stresses vary with depth in bins, silos, and hoppers when bulk granular food materials (grains, powders, pellets) are stored. It is governed by the **Janssen Equation (1895)** and the **Rankine/Coulomb active–passive pressure theories**, modified for particulate solids through the **Lateral Pressure Ratio (K)** and the **Angle of Wall Friction (φw)**.

A **compression chamber** in food engineering is a confined volume in which a food mass is mechanically densified (e.g., extruder barrel, pellet die, oil-press cage, powder tablet press). The chamber walls carry a normal stress that is a function of the material's **bulk density (γ)**, **internal friction angle (φi)**, and the **wall friction coefficient (μw)**.

The two classical governing parameters are:

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Unit (Food Materials) |
|---|---|---|
| $\gamma$ | Bulk density | $\text{kN/m}^3$ |
| $\phi_i$ | Angle of internal friction | degrees (°) |
| $\phi_w$ | Angle of wall friction | degrees (°) |
| $\mu_w$ | Wall friction coefficient ($\tan \phi_w$) | dimensionless |
| $K$ | Lateral pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| $R_h$ | Hydraulic radius (Area / Perimeter) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Almost every food engineering design (silo, hopper, extruder, oil press, powder bag) starts with knowing the *normal and shear stresses* on the wall. The **Janssen equation** is the single most-tested formula in this module.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine pouring honey-coated rice flakes into a tall, narrow cylindrical glass.

- At the **top**, the flakes press straight down with weight $\gamma z$ (like in a liquid).
- As the column gets **deeper**, the flakes start *rubbing sideways against the wall*. The wall begins to **carry part of the weight**.
- Below a depth equal to roughly **2 to 3 times the bin diameter**, the vertical pressure **stops increasing** and approaches a limiting value called the **Janssen asymptote** $p_\infty = \dfrac{\gamma R_h}{\mu_w K}$.

This is the **opposite** of what happens with water. With water, pressure keeps growing forever ($\gamma z$). With grain, the wall “**short-circuits**” the load, so the bottom of a 30 m grain silo does **not** see 30 m of water-equivalent pressure.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy for compression chambers:** Think of a coffee capsule press. The ground coffee is the bulk solid, the steel die is the chamber, and the punch applies vertical load. The side wall experiences the *lateral pressure* (horizontal push) while the punch face experiences the *axial pressure* (vertical push). The ratio between them is $K = \sigma_h / \sigma_v$.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Food Bulk Solids

- **Janssen characteristic length** (decay depth): $L_c = \dfrac{R_h}{\mu_w K}$ — measured in **meters (m)**.
- For wheat in a 6 m diameter silo: $L_c \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ m.
- **Rankine active coefficient** for cohesionless food grains: $K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi_i}{2}\right)$.
- **Rankine passive coefficient**: $K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi_i}{2}\right)$.
- Standard unit of pressure: **kPa** (or **bar = 100 kPa**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Stress States in a Bulk Solid

When a granular food material rests in a vertical-walled container, the **2D stress state** at any point consists of three principal stresses:

1. **Vertical (axial) stress** $\sigma_v$ — acts parallel to gravity.
2. **Horizontal (lateral) stress** $\sigma_h = K \cdot \sigma_v$ — acts perpendicular to the wall.
3. **Wall shear stress** $\tau_w = \mu_w \cdot \sigma_h$ — resists downward flow along the wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Mohr-Coulomb yield criterion** is the backbone: a bulk solid flows when the shear stress at a plane exceeds $\tau = c + \sigma \tan \phi_i$. For dry food grains, cohesion $c \approx 0$, simplifying to $\tau = \sigma \tan \phi_i$.

---

## 2.2 Janssen's Equation — The Silo Pressure Model

### 2.2.1 Free-Body Logic (Step-by-Step)

Consider an **infinitely thin horizontal slice** of bulk solid at depth $z$ with thickness $dz$ in a vertical-walled bin of cross-sectional area $A$ and perimeter $U$.

1. **Weight of the slice** pulls down: $dW = \gamma A \, dz$.
2. **Vertical force balance** on the slice (equilibrium, no acceleration):
   * The slice is supported by the increase in vertical stress below it $\bigl(A \cdot d\sigma_v\bigr)$ **plus** the upward shear from the wall $\bigl(\tau_w \cdot U \cdot dz\bigr)$.
3. The wall shear reaches the **limiting (Coulomb) friction**: $\tau_w = \mu_w \sigma_h = \mu_w K \sigma_v$.

Assembling the force balance on the slice:

$$
\gamma A \, dz \;=\; A\, d\sigma_v \;+\; \mu_w K\, \sigma_v \, U\, dz
$$

Dividing by $A\,dz$ and noting $R_h = A / U$:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_v}{dz} \;=\; \gamma \;-\; \frac{\mu_w K}{R_h}\,\sigma_v
$$

This is a first-order **linear ODE** in $\sigma_v(z)$.

---

## 2.3 Solution of the Janssen ODE

With boundary condition $\sigma_v(0) = 0$ (open top, surcharge neglected), the solution is:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_v(z) \;=\; \frac{\gamma R_h}{\mu_w K}\Bigl(1 - e^{-\mu_w K z / R_h}\Bigr)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant $L_c = \dfrac{R_h}{\mu_w K}$ is the **Janssen decay length** (or characteristic depth). After $\approx 3 L_c$ to $4 L_c$, the exponential term becomes negligible (less than **5%**), and the pressure **saturates** at the asymptote $p_\infty = \gamma R_h / (\mu_w K)$.

The **lateral pressure** on the wall is:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_h(z) \;=\; K\,\sigma_v(z) \;=\; \frac{\gamma R_h}{\mu_w}\Bigl(1 - e^{-\mu_w K z / R_h}\Bigr)\;}
$$

The **vertical wall friction load** integrated over depth $H$ is:

$$
\boxed{\;F_w(H) \;=\; \int_0^H \mu_w \sigma_h U\, dz \;=\; A\,\sigma_v(H)\;}
$$

This last identity is the **Janssen balance rule**: the total wall friction equals the weight carried by the bottom (i.e., the vertical pressure at the floor times the cross-section).

---

## 2.4 Lateral Pressure Ratio (K) — Active vs. Passive

For a **Rankine-type cohesionless bulk solid**:

$$
K_a \;=\; \tan^2\!\Bigl(45° - \tfrac{\phi_i}{2}\Bigr) \quad \text{(active, fill/discharge state)}
$$

$$
K_p \;=\; \tan^2\!\Bigl(45° + \tfrac{\phi_i}{2}\Bigr) \quad \text{(passive, jammed state)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Walker (1966) measured** $K \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$ for wheat in slow fill, dropping to $0.25$ to $0.35$ during discharge. **Jenike (1964)** used $K$ as the **flow factor** to design mass-flow hoppers. KTU problems typically ask students to choose **$K_a$** for steady storage and **$K_p$** for jammed discharge.

---

## 2.5 Compression Chambers — Simplified Pressure Model

For a **closed, rigid die** (piston-type compression, e.g., pellet mill, tablet press, oil expeller):

- **Axial pressure** at piston face: $p_a = p_0 \, e^{4 \mu_w L / D}$ (Stokes/Eytelwein-type capstan equation, where $L$ is die length, $D$ is die diameter, $p_0$ is the entry pressure).
- **Radial (wall) pressure**: $p_r = K \cdot p_a$.
- **Exit pressure** (downstream of plug): $p_{out} = p_{in} \, e^{-4 \mu_w L / D}$.

The product $\dfrac{4 \mu_w L}{D}$ is the **die resistance factor** — KTU loves to plot pressure ratio vs. this dimensionless group.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Standard Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_v(z) = \dfrac{\gamma R_h}{\mu_w K}\bigl(1 - e^{-\mu_w K z / R_h}\bigr)$ | Janssen vertical pressure | Pa |
| 2 | $\sigma_h(z) = K\,\sigma_v(z)$ | Horizontal wall pressure | Pa |
| 3 | $\tau_w = \mu_w \sigma_h$ | Wall shear stress | Pa |
| 4 | $F_w(H) = A\,\sigma_v(H)$ | Total wall friction load | N |
| 5 | $L_c = R_h / (\mu_w K)$ | Janssen decay length | m |
| 6 | $K_a = \tan^2\!\bigl(45° - \phi_i / 2\bigr)$ | Rankine active coefficient | – |
| 7 | $K_p = \tan^2\!\bigl(45° + \phi_i / 2\bigr)$ | Rankine passive coefficient | – |
| 8 | $p_{out} = p_{in} \, e^{4 \mu_w L / D}$ | Axial pressure in a compression die | Pa |
| 9 | $R_h = A / U$ | Hydraulic radius | m |
| 10 | $\mu_w = \tan \phi_w$ | Wall-friction coefficient | – |

> [!WARNING]
> Never confuse **$\mu_w$ (wall friction)** with **$\tan \phi_i$ (internal friction)**. The former is the material–wall pair, the latter is the material–material pair. KTU valuation strictly checks this distinction.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Silo design** (grain elevators, flour mills, sugar refineries): wall thickness based on $\sigma_h$.
- **Hopper design** for **mass flow** vs. **funnel flow** classification (Jenike shear testing).
- **Pellet mill die** design in animal feed: predicts motor load via $\sigma_v(H)$.
- **Cold / hot oil expellers**: predicts the **pressure buildup** in the press cage.
- **Tablet presses in pharma/food powder compaction**: predicts die-wall friction and capping defects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Janssen Equation (Full Detailed Walkthrough)
