# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the physiological and psychological aspects of subjective texture measurement. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

*Physiological aspect* refers to the biological sensory mechanisms by which the human body detects the mechanical, auditory, and tactile properties of food during oral processing. It involves mechanoreceptors in the teeth, tongue, palate, and jaw muscles that respond to force, deformation, vibration, and flow. For example, biting a carrot activates periodontal kinesthetic receptors, signalling hardness to the brain.

*Psychological aspect* refers to the cognitive and emotional interpretation of the physiological signals. It is influenced by expectations, colour, aroma, brand, prior experience, and cultural background. For example, a slightly soft apple may still be perceived as fresh if its colour and aroma are acceptable, but rejected if it appears overripe.

**Keywords to remember:** *mechanoreceptors, kinesthetic, cognitive, expectation.*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between firmness and hardness with reference to food materials. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hardness | Firmness |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Definition | Resistance to localized plastic deformation | Resistance to penetration by a defined probe |
| Engineering units | Pressure (N/mm² or Pa) | Force (N) |
| Typical test | Indentation (Brinell, Vickers) | Penetration (Magness–Taylor) |
| Example | Indentation of cheese | Firmness of an apple |

In KTU 2024 convention, **hardness is normalized to contact area**, while **firmness is the raw force**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Describe the Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) test in detail, with a labelled force–time curve and the seven classical TPA parameters. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
TPA, developed by Bourne (1978), is a two-bite compression test that imitates the action of the human jaw on a food sample. The food is compressed twice to a defined strain (typically 30–75 %) using a flat-plate or probe of defined geometry. The force–time (or force–distance) response is recorded, and seven classical texture parameters are computed from it.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Calibrate the Texture Analyzer (load cell + crosshead).
2. Place a defined-geometry sample (cylinder/cube) on the platform.
3. First compression: probe descends at constant speed (0.5–2 mm/s) to a defined strain. Peak force = Hardness.
4. Hold briefly (optional dwell).
5. Reverse: probe retracts. Negative area = Adhesiveness.
6. Second compression: probe descends again. Second peak and area are used for cohesiveness and springiness.

**Labelled Force–Time Curve (2 marks):**

| Curve Point | Meaning |
|-------------|---------|
| Peak F1 | Hardness |
| Area A1 (under first bite, positive) | Work of first compression |
| Area A2 (under second bite, positive) | Work of second compression |
| Area A3 (negative, on retraction) | Adhesiveness |
| Distance L1 (deformation to F1) | Original strain |
| Distance L2 (recovered to F2) | Recovery |
| Area A1-0 (upstroke to F1) | Instant recovery energy |

**Seven TPA Parameters (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Formula | Unit |
|-----------|---------|------|
| Hardness | $H = F_1$ | N |
| Cohesiveness | $C = A_2 / A_1$ | — |
| Adhesiveness | $A_{dh} = \vert A_3 \vert$ | N·s |
| Springiness | $S = L_2 / L_1$ | — |
| Gumminess | $G = H \times C$ | N |
| Chewiness | $Ch = H \times C \times S$ | mJ |
| Resilience | $R = A_{1-0} / A_1$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write Chewiness = Gumminess or confuse the area subscripts. Always label A1, A2, A3, A1-0 carefully on the diagram — missing labels cost 1–2 marks.

#### (b) Explain the mechanics of hardness. Derive the Brinell hardness formula for a food material. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Hardness is the resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation caused by an indenter under a known load. In food engineering, this is often measured by penetration or indentation, and the hardness has units of pressure (N/mm² or Pa).

**Brinell Test Setup (1 mark):**
A spherical indenter of diameter $D$ is pressed into the food with load $P$. A circular indent of diameter $d$ and depth $h$ is formed. The Brinell Hardness Number (BHN) is:

$$ BHN = \frac{P}{A} $$

where $A$ is the contact (curved) surface area of the indent.

**Geometric Derivation (4 marks):**

The depth of the spherical cap:

$$ h = \frac{D}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2} $$

The curved surface area of the spherical cap:

$$ A = \pi D h $$

Substituting $h$:

$$ A = \pi D \left[ \frac{D}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2} \right] $$

Multiplying the bracket by 2:

$$ A = \frac{\pi D}{2}\left[D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right] $$

Final Brinell Hardness:

$$ BHN = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left[D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right]} $$

**Validity condition (1 mark):** $0.25 < d/D < 0.50$ for accurate Brinell hardness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write the BHN formula as $P/(πDh)$, missing the factor of 2 — this is **wrong** by a factor of 2 and will cost 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) What are the imitative measurements used for food texture? List the equipment used and explain the dynamic test for evaluation of food texture. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Imitative Measurements (1 mark):**
Imitative measurements are instrumental methods that mechanically simulate the action of the mouth (biting, chewing, cutting, puncturing) on food to obtain numerical texture attributes.

**Equipment Used (2 marks):**

| Equipment | Application |
|-----------|-------------|
| Universal Testing Machine / Texture Analyzer (TA-XT2, TA-HD) | TPA, penetration, compression, three-point bend |
| Kramer Shear Press | Multi-blade shear of vegetables, meats |
| Warner–Bratzler Shear device | Meat tenderness |
| Penetrometer (Magness–Taylor) | Apple, pear firmness |
| Puncture tester | Fruit skin strength |
| Ottawa Cell | Texture under compression with juice flow |
| Volodkevich Bite Tenderometer | Simulated bite |
| Back Extrusion rig | Yogurt, gels |

**Dynamic Test (4 marks):**

*Definition:* Dynamic tests measure texture under **rapid, transient, or oscillating** loading conditions — they capture the response of foods to impact and vibration, which is more relevant to the *first bite* experience than slow static tests.

*Types of dynamic tests:*

1. **Impact Test:** A striker of known mass $m$ is dropped from height $h$ onto the food. The peak impact force and the indent volume $V$ are measured. Dynamic hardness:

$$ H_d = \frac{m g h}{V} $$

2. **Vibration (Oscillatory) Test:** A sinusoidal strain of small amplitude is applied at varying frequency. The storage modulus $G'$ (elastic) and loss modulus $G''$ (viscous) are measured. The ratio $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ indicates gel strength and crispness.

3. **Acoustic Emission (AE) Test:** During the TPA or impact test, an acoustic sensor detects the sound bursts from micro-fractures. The total acoustic energy:

$$ E_{AE} = \int V^2(t) \, dt $$

correlates with **perceived crispness**. A high count of high-amplitude AE events = crispy/crunchy food.

*Application:* Dynamic tests are used for **crispy and crunchy foods** (chips, biscuits, breakfast cereals, extruded snacks) where the texture is dominated by fracture and vibration, not slow deformation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Do not write only "impact test" — you must mention at least two dynamic tests (impact + vibration or impact + acoustic) to get full marks. Static tests (penetration, compression) are **not** dynamic tests.

#### (b) Discuss the effect of age, water content, and temperature on the texture of foods. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. Effect of Age (Storage Time) — 2 marks:**

During storage, foods undergo biochemical and physicochemical changes that alter texture:
- **Fruits and vegetables:** Pectinolytic enzymes (pectin methylesterase, polygalacturonase) degrade pectin in the cell wall → progressive **softening** (e.g., banana ripening, tomato softening).
- **Cereal products:** Starch retrogradation (re-association of amylose and amylopectin) causes **staling** and firmness increase (e.g., bread staling).
- **Meat:** Post-mortem proteolysis tenderizes meat; later, oxidation toughens it.
- **Dairy:** Age-induced syneresis in cheese increases firmness.

Mathematical model (first-order kinetics):

$$ H(t) = H_\infty - (H_\infty - H_0)\, e^{-k t} $$

**2. Effect of Water Content (Moisture) — 2.5 marks:**

Water acts as a **plasticizer** in food biopolymers. Increasing water content disrupts hydrogen bonding between polymer chains, increasing free volume and chain mobility, which **decreases hardness**.

Empirical relationship:

$$ H = H_{\text{ref}} \cdot e^{k(M_{\text{ref}} - M)} $$

For example, dried fruits (low $M$) are much firmer than fresh ones; fresh cucumber (high $M$) is softer than dried cucumber.

Above a **critical moisture content**, water exists in a free, mobile state and the food becomes soft. Below it, the matrix is glassy and brittle.

**3. Effect of Temperature — 2.5 marks:**

Temperature affects texture through multiple mechanisms:

- **Glass transition ($T_g$):** When a food is cooled below $T_g$, the amorphous matrix becomes **glassy and brittle** (crunchy). Above $T_g$, it is **rubbery and flexible** (chewy). Crossing $T_g$ produces a sharp texture change — this is exploited in snack manufacturing.
- **Starch gelatinization:** Heating starch in water above 60–80 °C causes granules to swell and lose crystallinity → soft, paste-like texture (e.g., cooked rice).
- **Fat melting:** In chocolate, heating above 32–35 °C melts cocoa butter → loss of hardness and snap.
- **Protein denaturation:** Heating egg white or meat denatures proteins → textural change.

Arrhenius-type relation for thermal softening:

$$ H(T) = H_{\text{ref}} \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{\text{ref}}}\right)} $$

**Summary Table:**

| Factor | Effect on Hardness | Mechanism |
|--------|---------------------|-----------|
| ↑ Age (storage) | ↓ (most foods) | Enzyme activity, retrogradation |
| ↑ Water content | ↓ | Plasticization |
| ↑ Temperature | ↓ | Glass transition, gelatinization, melting |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only "moisture softens food" without giving the plasticization mechanism or the exponential relationship. A minimum of **one formula** and **one example per factor** is expected for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 KTU Mark-Loss Traps in Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing Gumminess with Chewiness** — gumminess = $H \times C$ (no springiness), chewiness = $H \times C \times S$.
> 2. **Missing one of the three aspects** of subjective measurement — physiological, psychological, mechanical.
> 3. **Writing $HB = P/(\pi Dh)$** instead of $2P/[\pi D(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$.
> 4. **Confusing static and dynamic hardness** — dynamic hardness uses kinetic energy, not load.
> 5. **Writing Adhesiveness as a positive area** — it is the **negative** area on retraction; use the magnitude.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Subjective texture measurement** has three aspects: **physiological** (receptors/jaw muscles), **psychological** (expectation/cognition), **mechanical** (deformation behaviour).
- **Imitative measurements** mechanically simulate the human mouth — they include **TPA**, **dynamic tests**, and **mechanical tests** (firmness, hardness).
- **TPA is the most-tested topic** — know all **7 parameters**: Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, Chewiness, Resilience.
- **TPA uses a two-bite compression cycle** with a flat plate or probe; peak force in first bite = hardness.
- **Cohesiveness = A₂/A₁** (ratio of areas); **Springiness = L₂/L₁** (ratio of distances).
- **Gumminess = H × C** (semi-solids); **Chewiness = H × C × S** (solids).
- **Hardness** is force per unit area (Pa or N/mm²); **firmness** is the raw force (N) — they are **not** the same.
- **Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)** for food: $HB = 2P \,/\, [\pi D(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$.
- **Dynamic hardness** uses impact energy $mgh$ divided by indent volume: $H_d = mgh/V$.
- **Dynamic tests** (impact, vibration, acoustic emission) are used for **crispy/crunchy foods**; static tests are used for **soft foods**.
- **Magness–Taylor penetrometer** is the industry standard for apple/pear firmness (8 mm plunger, 8 mm penetration).
- **Warner–Bratzler shear** is the standard for meat tenderness.
- **Effects on texture:**
  - ↑ Age → softening (fruits/veg) or firming (bread staling) → first-order kinetics.
  - ↑ Water content → ↓ hardness (plasticization) → exponential relation.
  - ↑ Temperature → ↓ hardness (glass transition, gelatinization, fat melting) → Arrhenius relation.
- **Crossing $T_g$** produces the sharp brittle-to-rubbery texture change — a key concept in snack and freeze-dried food design.
- **Acoustic emission energy** correlates with perceived **crispness** — high AE = crispy food.
- **Szczesniak's 1963 classification** identified 13 mechanical texture attributes, of which KTU tests focus on **hardness, cohesiveness, springiness, adhesiveness, gumminess, chewiness, and resilience**.
- **Valid Brinell test range:** $0.25 < d/D < 0.50$; typical indenter $D$ for food: 1–10 mm; load: 0.5–50 N.
- **Standard TPA strain** is **30–75 %** of original sample height; speed: **0.5–2 mm/s**.
- **Fracturability** (a separate parameter in some TPA variants) is the force at the first significant break in the curve on the first bite — not the same as hardness.

### High-Yield Memory Mnemonic

> **"HARD GUM CHEWS RESILIENTLY AFTER SPRING"**
> → **H**ardness, **A**dhesiveness, **R**esilience, **D** (consistency), **G**umminess, **C**hewiness, **A** (adhesiveness), **S**pringiness.

### Last-Minute Formula Flash-Card

| # | Formula | Use |
|---|---------|-----|
| 1 | $H = F_1$ | TPA Hardness |
| 2 | $C = A_2 / A_1$ | TPA Cohesiveness |
| 3 | $S = L_2 / L_1$ | TPA Springiness |
| 4 | $G = H \times C$ | Gumminess |
| 5 | $Ch = H \times C \times S$ | Chewiness |
| 6 | $HB = 2P / [\pi D(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$ | Brinell Hardness |
| 7 | $H_d = mgh / V$ | Dynamic Hardness |
| 8 | $H = H_{\text{ref}} \, e^{k(M_{\text{ref}} - M)}$ | Moisture–Hardness |
| 9 | $H(T) = H_{\text{ref}} \, e^{(E_a/R)(1/T - 1/T_{\text{ref}})}$ | Temperature–Hardness |
| 10 | $H(t) = H_\infty - (H_\infty - H_0) e^{-k t}$ | Age–Hardness |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colour, measuring equipment, L*a*b system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT526 - ENGINEERING PROPERTIES OF FOOD MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Colour, measuring equipment, L*a*b system ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Colour in Food Materials? In the context of Food Engineering, **colour (color)** is defined as the visual perceptual property corresponding in humans to the categories of *red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo,* and *violet* — derived from the visible spectrum of light interacting with the photoreceptors of the eye, and modified by the physical, chemical, and structural characteristics of the food matrix. > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition** > Colour of a food material is a *psycho-physico-chemical* attribute that results from the spectral composition of the light reflected, transmitted, or emitted by the food, as interpreted by the human observer under standardized illumination and viewing conditions. ### 1.2 Why Colour Matters in Food Engineering Colour is often the **first quality attribute** a consumer evaluates. It directly influences: - **Acceptability & purchase intent** (e.g., browning in bread, red in tomatoes) - **Perceived freshness and ripeness** - **Process monitoring** (e.g., baking, frying, drying, fermentation) - **Adulteration detection** (e.g., saffron purity, paprika quality) - **Correlation with nutritional compounds** (e.g., carotenoids, anthocyanins, chlorophylls) ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Colour as a Food's Signature" > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Fingerprint of Light** > Imagine you are a *detective* examining a food sample under a "flashlight of pure white light." The food *absorbs* some wavelengths and *reflects* others. The reflected wavelengths are like a **fingerprint** — unique to that food's pigments (chlorophyll, carotenoids, anthocyanins, myoglobin, etc.) and structure. A **colour measuring instrument** is simply a sophisticated detective that mathematically decodes this fingerprint into three numbers (L*, a*, b*) — telling us exactly "how dark/light," "how red/green," and "how yellow/blue" the food is. ### 1.4 Standardized Viewing Conditions To ensure reproducible measurements, the **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage)** has standardized: - **Illuminants**: D65 (average daylight, 6500 K), A (incandescent tungsten, 2856 K), C (overcast sky) - **Standard Observer**: 2° (1931) or 10° (1964) field of view - **Geometry**: 45°/0°, 0°/45°, d/0° (diffuse) These standards make measurements **independent of the human eye**, enabling objective colour communication. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Components of Colour Perception Any colour measurement depends on three physical/observer factors: 1. **Light Source (Illuminant)** — defines the spectral power distribution $S(\lambda)$ falling on the sample 2. **Object (Sample)** — characterized by its reflectance, transmittance, or absorbance $R(\lambda)$ 3. **Observer** — characterized by the colour matching functions $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tristimulus Principle**: Any colour can be matched by mixing three primary stimuli (Red, Green, Blue) — the foundation of all modern colorimetry. ### 2.2 The CIE XYZ Tristimulus Values Before the L*a*b* system, colour is fundamentally expressed through three numbers **X, Y, Z** derived by integrating the product of the illuminant spectrum, the sample reflectance, and the observer's matching functions: $$ X = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, R(\lambda)\, \bar{x}(\lambda)\, d\lambda $$ $$ Y = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, R(\lambda)\, \bar{y}(\lambda)\, d\lambda $$ $$ Z = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, R(\lambda)\, \bar{z}(\lambda)\, d\lambda $$ where $k$ is the normalizing constant: $$ k = \dfrac{100}{\int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, \bar{y}(\lambda)\, d\lambda} $$ ensuring $Y = 100$ for a perfect white diffuser. ### 2.3 The CIE L*a*b* (CIELAB) Colour Space The **CIELAB** system, recommended by CIE in **1976**, is a perceptually uniform colour space designed so that *equal numerical differences correspond to approximately equal perceived colour differences*. It is the **gold standard for food colour measurement** today. The three axes are: | Axis | Symbol | Range | Physical Meaning | Food Interpretation | |------|--------|-------|------------------|---------------------| | **Lightness** | **L*** | 0 → 100 | Black (0) to White (100) | Darkness/lightness of food | | **Red–Green** | **a*** | −128 → +127 | Negative = green; Positive = red | Ripeness, freshness | | **Yellow–Blue** | **b*** | −128 → +127 | Negative = blue; Positive = yellow | Caramelization, oil oxidation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept**: The L*a*b* system was deliberately designed to be **perceptually uniform** — meaning that the human eye perceives a unit change in any direction as roughly the same magnitude of colour difference. This is critical for food quality control. ### 2.4 Derived Colour Quantities (Most Frequently Asked in Exams!) From L*, a*, b*, food engineers commonly compute: #### (a) Chroma (C*) — Colour Saturation $$ C^{*} = \sqrt{(a^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{2}} $$ *Represents the vividness or intensity of the colour. Higher C* → more saturated colour.* #### (b) Hue Angle (h°) — Colour Tone $$ h^{\circ} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{b^{*}}{a^{*}}\right) \quad \text{with} \quad 0^{\circ} \le h^{\circ} < 360^{\circ} $$ | Hue Angle Range | Colour Family | Food Example | |-----------------|---------------|--------------| | 0° – 30° | Red | Ripe tomatoes, strawberries | | 30° – 60° | Orange | Carrots, oranges | | 60° – 90° | Yellow | Bananas, lemon | | 90° – 180° | Green | Spinach, broccoli | | 180° – 270° | Cyan/Blue | Blueberries | | 270° – 360° | Magenta/Red | Beetroot, red cabbage | #### (c) Total Colour Difference (ΔE*) — Most Critical in Quality Control $$ \Delta E^{*} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}} $$ where $\Delta L^{*}=L^{*}_{sample}-L^{*}_{std}$, etc. > [!TIP] > **Industry Benchmarks for ΔE***: > - ΔE* < 1: Difference not perceptible to the human eye > - 1 < ΔE* < 2: Perceptible only on close observation > - 2 < ΔE* < 3.5: Perceptible at a glance > - 3.5 < ΔE* < 5: Clearly noticeable difference > - ΔE* > 5: Strong colour difference (often used as rejection threshold in food QC) #### (d) Whiteness Index (WI) $$ WI = 100 - \sqrt{(100 - L^{*})^{2} + (a^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{2}} $$ *Used for milk powder, flour, sugar, white rice, starches.* #### (e) Yellowness Index (YI) $$ YI = \dfrac{142.86 \cdot b^{*}}{L^{*}} $$ *Used for oils, fats, bakery products to monitor Maillard browning and oxidation.* #### (f) Browning Index (BI) $$ BI = \dfrac{100\,(x - 0.31)}{0.172} $$ where $$ x = \dfrac{(a^{*} + 1.75\,L^{*})}{(5.645\,L^{*} + a^{*} - 3.012\,b^{*})} $$ *Widely applied in bread crusts, fried foods, dried fruits, and coffee.* ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table) | Parameter | Formula | Unit / Range | Application | |-----------|---------|--------------|-------------| | Tristimulus X | $X = k\!\int S(\lambda)R(\lambda)\bar{x}(\lambda)d\lambda$ | — | Precise colorimetry | | Tristimulus Y | $Y = k\!\int S(\lambda)R(\lambda)\bar{y}(\lambda)d\lambda$ | 0–100 | Brightness | | Tristimulus Z | $Z = k\!\int S(\lambda)R(\lambda)\bar{z}(\lambda)d\lambda$ | — | Blue stimulus | | Lightness L* | $L^{*}=116\,f(Y/Y_n)-16$ | 0–100 | Lightness perception | | Redness a* | $a^{*}=500\,[f(X/X_n)-f(Y/Y_n)]$ | −128 to +127 | Redness/greenness | | Yellowness b* | $b^{*}=200\,[f(Y/Y_n)-f(Z/Z_n)]$ | −128 to +127 | Yellowness/blueness | | $f(t)$ | $f(t) = t^{1/3}$ if $t>0.008856$ else $7.787\,t + 16/116$ | — | Cube-root compression | | Chroma C* | $\sqrt{(a^{*})^{2}+(b^{*})^{2}}$ | 0–~150 | Saturation | | Hue angle h° | $\arctan(b^{*}/a^{*})$ | 0–360° | Colour family | | ΔE* | $\sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2}+(\Delta a^{*})^{2}+(\Delta b^{*})^{2}}$ | 0–∞ | Total colour difference | | WI | $100-\sqrt{(100-L^{*})^{2}+a^{*2}+b^{*2}}$ | 0–100 | White products | | YI | $142.86\,b^{*}/L^{*}$ | — | Oils, browning | | BI | $[100(x-0.31)]/0.172$ | — | Browning | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Exam Trap**: Students often confuse **L*a*b* (1976, CIE)** with **Hunter Lab (L, a, b — 1958)**. The Hunter system uses **square-root** transformations (e.g., $L=10\sqrt{Y}$), while CIELAB uses **cube-root** transformations. The notation and units are different! KTU 2024 syllabus mandates **CIELAB** unless otherwise specified. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of L* from Y/Y_n (Cube-Root Compression) The CIELAB system requires the relative tristimulus values $X/X_n$, $Y/Y_n$, $Z/Z_n$, where subscript $n$ denotes the illuminant on a perfect white diffuser. **Step 1 — Define the nonlinear function $f(t)$**: $$ f(t) = \begin{cases} t^{1/3}, & \text{if } t > 0.008856 \\[4pt] 7.787\,t + \dfrac{16}{116}, & \text{if } t \le 0.008856 \end{cases} $$ **Step 2 — The cube-root compression** approximates the human eye's nonlinear response to luminance (Stevens' power law). Above the linear threshold $0.008856$ (corresponding to $L^*=8$), $f(t)=t^{1/3}$; below it, a linear ramp maintains continuity of slope at the join point. **Step 3 — Compute L***: $$ L^{*} = 116\, f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16 $$ **Step 4 — Compute a***: $$ a^{*} = 500\,\left[ f\!\left(\dfrac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right] $$ **Step 5 — Compute b***: $$ b^{*} = 200\,\left[ f\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\dfrac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right] $$ The factor 116 ensures $L^*=100$ when $Y/Y_n=1$ (perfect white), and the factor 16 places the origin at true black. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard) > **Problem**: A tomato sauce sample is measured under D65 illuminant and 10° observer. Tristimulus values obtained are: > $X = 42.30, \ Y = 28.55, \ Z = 18.72$ > Reference white: $X_n = 82.10, \ Y_n = 88.40, \ Z_n = 95.20$ > A standard (control) sample has: $L^{*}_{std}=48.5,\ a^{*}_{std}=32.0,\ b^{*}_{std}=25.0$ > **Compute (i) L*, a*, b* of sample, (ii) Chroma, (iii) Hue angle, (iv) ΔE* against standard.** #### Step i — Compute L*, a*, b* First compute ratios: $$ \dfrac{X}{X_n} = \dfrac{42.30}{82.10} = 0.5152 $$ $$ \dfrac{Y}{Y_n} = \dfrac{28.55}{88.40} = 0.3229 $$ $$ \dfrac{Z}{Z_n} = \dfrac{18.72}{95.20} = 0.1966 $$ All three ratios are **above 0.008856**, so the cube-root branch applies. Cube roots: $$ (0.5152)^{1/3} = 0.8006 $$ $$ (0.3229)^{1/3} = 0.6856 $$ $$ (0.1966)^{1/3} = 0.5812 $$ Now: $$ L^{*} = 116 \times 0.6856 - 16 = 79.53 - 16 = 63.53 $$ $$ a^{*} = 500 \times (0.8006 - 0.6856) = 500 \times 0.1150 = 57.50 $$ $$ b^{*} = 200 \times (0.6856 - 0.5812) = 200 \times 0.1044 = 20.88 $$ #### Step ii — Chroma (C*) $$ C^{*} = \sqrt{(57.50)^{2} + (20.88)^{2}} = \sqrt{3306.25 + 436.0} = \sqrt{3742.25} = 61.18 $$ #### Step iii — Hue Angle (h°) $$ h^{\circ} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{20.88}{57.50}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.3631) = 19.96^{\circ} \approx 20.0^{\circ} $$ Since $a^*>0$ and $b^*>0$, the colour lies in the **first quadrant** (red-to-yellow region), so $h^\circ \approx 20°$ corresponds to a **vivid red**, perfectly consistent with tomato sauce. #### Step iv — Total Colour Difference (ΔE*) $$ \Delta L^{*} = 63.53 - 48.5 = 15.03 $$ $$ \Delta a^{*} = 57.50 - 32.0 = 25.50 $$ $$ \Delta b^{*} = 20.88 - 25.0 = -4.12 $$ $$ \Delta E^{*} = \sqrt{(15.03)^{2} + (25.50)^{2} + (-4.12)^{2}} $$ $$ \Delta E^{*} = \sqrt{225.9 + 650.25 + 16.97} = \sqrt{893.12} = 29.88 $$ > **Interpretation**: ΔE* ≈ 30 indicates a *very strong* colour difference between the processed sample and the standard — the sample is much redder (Δa* = +25.5) and lighter (ΔL* = +15.0). In a ketchup QC line, this would be flagged for **rework or reformulation**. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Colour Parameter | Quality Concern | Best Parameter | Typical Food Application | |-----------------|----------------|--------------------------| | Surface darkening during drying | L* | Raisins, dried mango | | Ripeness | a* | Tomato, apple, mango | | Caramelization / Maillard | b* + BI | Bread crust, biscuits | | Oil oxidation | YI | Frying oil, ghee | | Whiteness / purity | WI | Milk, sugar, flour | | Fermentation monitoring | h° (Cu+ ion indicator in wine) | Wine, vinegar | | Batch-to-batch consistency | ΔE* | All processed foods | | Pigment stability | C* | Juice, saffron, paprika | ### 3.4 Mathematical Justification for Perceptual Uniformity (Brief) The Hunter Lab system had a problem: equal steps in X, Y, Z did **not** produce equal perceived colour differences. The CIELAB system applied **cube-root compression** to model the Munsell value function and the human visual system's nonlinear luminance response. The result is a roughly **Euclidean colour space** where simple distance formulas (like ΔE*) approximate the human perceptual difference — critical for industrial QC. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The CIELAB Colour Solid (Schematic) ```mermaid graph TD axisA[Colour Space Origin] axisA -->|L* axis vertical| lightTop[White L*=100] axisA -->|downward| blackBot[Black L*=0] axisA -->|a* +ve right| redPos[Red a*=positive] axisA -->|a* -ve left| greenNeg[Green a*=negative] axisA -->|b* +ve front| yellowPos[Yellow b*=positive] axisA -->|b* -ve back| blueNeg[Blue b*=negative] redPos --> tomato[Tomato h approx 20 deg] yellowPos --> carrot[Carrot h approx 60 deg] greenNeg --> spinach[Spinach h approx 120 deg] blueNeg --> berry[Blueberry h approx 270 deg] ``` ### 4.2 Colour Measurement Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD sample1[Food Sample] --> prep[Sample Preparation
homogenize, slice, grind] prep --> inst[Place in Instrument] inst --> choice{Measurement Type?} choice -->|Reflectance| col1[Colorimeter / Spectrophotometer] choice -->|Transmittance| col2[Spectrophotometer with cuvette] choice -->|Appearance| col3[Computer Vision System] col1 --> spec[Spectral Scan 380 to 780 nm] spec --> xyz[Compute Tristimulus X Y Z] xyz --> lab[Transform to L* a* b*] lab --> derived[Derive C* h deg Delta E* WI YI BI] derived --> qc[Quality Control Report] col2 --> xyz col3 --> rgb[RGB Image] rgb --> convert[Convert RGB to L* a* b*] convert --> lab ``` ### 4.3 Comparison of Colour Measuring Equipment ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Category1[Tristimulus Colorimeters] colA1[Hunter Labscan] colA2[Minolta CR-400] colA3[X-Rite ColorFlex] end subgraph Category2[Spectrophotometers] colB1[Shimadzu UV-2600] colB2[Jasco V-770] colB3[Perkin Elmer Lambda] end subgraph Category3[Computer Vision] colC1[Flatbed Scanner with Calibrated Light Box] colC2[CCD Camera in Image Analysis Booth] end subgraph Category4[Specialised] colD1[Dellavalle HunterLab ColorQuest XE] colD2[Konica Minolta CM-700d Handheld] end Category1 --> output[Output L* a* b* + Spectral Data] Category2 --> output Category3 --> output Category4 --> output ``` ### 4.4 L*a*b* vs RGB vs Munsell — Conceptual Map ```mermaid graph TD root[Colour Space Family] root --> rgb[RGB Additive System
Monitors, Cameras] root --> munsell[Munsell System
Hue Value Chroma
Visual Ordering] root --> ciexyz[CIE XYZ 1931
Foundation Space] root --> cielab[CIELAB L*a*b* 1976
Perceptually Uniform] root --> cielch[CIELCh Polar Form
L C h deg] ciexyz --> cielab cielab --> cielch rgb --> convert2[Matrix Transform] --> cielab munsell --> interp[Polynomial Interpolation] --> cielab ``` ### 4.5 Equipment Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD need{Quality Requirement?} need -->|Quick QC| tristim[Use Tristimulus Colorimeter] need -->|Research and Pigment Analysis| spectro[Use Spectrophotometer] need -->|Online In-Process| vision[Use Computer Vision] need -->|Field and Portable| handheld[Use Handheld Colorimeter] tristim --> specs1[Hunter Lab, Minolta CR series] spectro --> specs2[Shimadzu, Jasco, Konica] vision --> specs3[Imaging Booth with Calibrated Lighting] handheld --> specs4[Minolta CM series, X-Rite RM series] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]** *Define the CIELAB L*a*b* colour system. What is the significance of the cube-root transformation used in computing L*, a*, and b*?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **CIELAB (1976)** system is a perceptually uniform colour space defined by three coordinates: - **L*** — Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white) - **a*** — Red–green axis (positive = red, negative = green) - **b*** — Yellow–blue axis (positive = yellow, negative = blue) The values are computed from tristimulus X, Y, Z using a **cube-root transformation** (e.g., $L^*=116\,(Y/Y_n)^{1/3}-16$). This transformation **mimics the nonlinear response of the human eye** to light (Stevens' power law) and ensures that equal numerical differences in the space correspond to approximately equal perceived colour differences — enabling objective colour communication in food QC. *(Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for L*/a*/b* meaning, 1 for cube-root purpose)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** *List any three colour measuring instruments used in food industries and state one specific application of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Instrument | Application | |------------|-------------| | 1. Tristimulus Colorimeter (e.g., Hunter Labscan) | Rapid QC of tomato sauce redness (a*) | | 2. UV-Vis Spectrophotometer with integrating sphere (e.g., Shimadzu UV-2600) | Spectral characterization of pigments (chlorophyll, carotenoids) in juices | | 3. Computer Vision System (CCD camera + image booth) | Non-destructive sorting of apples, peaches based on skin colour | *(1 mark per instrument with correct application)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** Derive the expressions for the L*, a*, b* coordinates of the CIE L*a*b* system starting from the tristimulus values X, Y, Z. Clearly state the role of the cube-root function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A milk powder sample is measured against a reference white standard under D65/10° conditions. Tristimulus values: $X=78.5,\ Y=82.0,\ Z=89.0$; reference white: $X_n=Y_n=Z_n=100.0$. Reference milk powder control: $L^*_{std}=95.0,\ a^*_{std}=-1.2,\ b^*_{std}=4.5$. Compute (i) L*, a*, b* of the sample, (ii) Chroma, (iii) Whiteness Index, (iv) Total colour difference ΔE* from the standard. Comment on the acceptability of the sample. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]** The L*a*b* system is derived from the tristimulus values X, Y, Z of the sample and the corresponding values $X_n, Y_n, Z_n$ of a perfect white diffuser under the same illuminant. **Step 1 — Compute the normalized ratios:** $$ x = \dfrac{X}{X_n}, \quad y = \dfrac{Y}{Y_n}, \quad z = \dfrac{Z}{Z_n} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the nonlinear cube-root function $f(t)$:** $$ f(t) = t^{1/3} \quad \text{if} \quad t > 0.008856 $$ $$ f(t) = 7.787\,t + \dfrac{16}{116} \quad \text{if} \quad t \le 0.008856 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the three L*a*b* coordinates:** $$ L^{*} = 116\, f(y) - 16 $$ $$ a^{*} = 500\,[f(x) - f(y)] $$ $$ b^{*} = 200\,[f(y) - f(z)] $$ **Step 4 — Role of cube-root function (1 mark):** The cube-root transformation models the **nonlinear response of the human visual system** to luminance (Munsell value function). It compresses dynamic range and ensures **perceptual uniformity** — a unit step in any direction produces an approximately equal perceived difference. The linear branch below 0.008856 ensures the derivative is continuous at the join, preventing abrupt jumps in L* for very dark samples. --- **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]** **(i) Computation of L*, a*, b***: $$ x = \dfrac{78.5}{100} = 0.785, \quad y = \dfrac{82.0}{100} = 0.820, \quad z = \dfrac{89.0}{100} = 0.890 $$ All three ratios > 0.008856, so apply cube root: $$ x^{1/3} = 0.9225, \quad y^{1/3} = 0.9368, \quad z^{1/3} = 0.9629 $$ $$ L^{*} = 116 \times 0.9368 - 16 = 108.67 - 16 = 92.67 $$ $$ a^{*} = 500 \times (0.9225 - 0.9368) = 500 \times (-0.0143) = -7.15 $$ $$ b^{*} = 200 \times (0.9368 - 0.9629) = 200 \times (-0.0261) = -5.22 $$ **(ii) Chroma:** $$ C^{*} = \sqrt{(-7.15)^{2} + (-5.22)^{2}} = \sqrt{51.12 + 27.25} = \sqrt{78.37} = 8.85 $$ **(iii) Whiteness Index:** $$ WI = 100 - \sqrt{(100 - 92.67)^{2} + (-7.15)^{2} + (-5.22)^{2}} $$ $$ WI = 100 - \sqrt{(7.33)^{2} + 51.12 + 27.25} = 100 - \sqrt{53.73 + 78.37} = 100 - \sqrt{132.10} = 100 - 11.49 = 88.51 $$ **(iv) ΔE*:** $$ \Delta L^{*} = 92.67 - 95.0 = -2.33 $$ $$ \Delta a^{*} = -7.15 - (-1.2) = -5.95 $$ $$ \Delta b^{*} = -5.22 - 4.5 = -9.72 $$ $$ \Delta E^{*} = \sqrt{(-2.33)^{2} + (-5.95)^{2} + (-9.72)^{2}} = \sqrt{5.43 + 35.40 + 94.48} = \sqrt{135.31} = 11.63 $$ **Comment (1 mark)**: ΔE* ≈ 11.63 is **far above the typical rejection threshold of 3–5** for milk powder. The sample is *significantly different* from the standard — it is darker, has a noticeable greenish tint, and is bluer than the reference. This batch **does NOT meet QC specifications** and should be rejected or reprocessed. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Explain the construction and significance of the CIE L*a*b* colour space. Discuss, with equations, how **chroma (C*)**, **hue angle (h°)**, and **total colour difference (ΔE*)** are derived from L*, a*, b*. State the threshold values of ΔE* commonly used in food industry QC. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the working principle, components, and applications of: (i) a **tristimulus colorimeter** and (ii) a **spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere**, for colour measurement of food materials. Compare their relative advantages. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Construction of L*a*b* colour space (3 marks):** The CIELAB space is a **three-dimensional Cartesian space** with: - A vertical **L* axis** ranging 0 (black) to 100 (white) - A horizontal **a* axis** with positive = red, negative = green - A horizontal **b* axis** with positive = yellow, negative = blue - The axes meet at an achromatic point (L* = 50, a* = 0, b* = 0) - The space is **perceptually uniform** — designed so that Euclidean distance approximates human colour perception **Significance (1 mark):** - Independent of the device (objective) - Perceptually uniform (scientifically meaningful) - Widely used in food QC, printing, paint, plastics, pharmaceuticals **Derivations (2 marks):** $$ C^{*} = \sqrt{(a^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{2}} \quad \text{[Saturation]} $$ $$ h^{\circ} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{b^{*}}{a^{*}}\right) \quad \text{[Hue, 0 to 360 deg, quadrant adjusted]} $$ $$ \Delta E^{*} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^{2} + (\Delta a^{*})^{2} + (\Delta b^{*})^{2}} \quad \text{[Total difference]} $$ **ΔE* thresholds (1 mark):** - < 1: Not perceptible - 1–2: Perceptible on close inspection - 2–3.5: Perceptible at a glance - 3.5–5: Clearly noticeable - > 5: Major difference (typical rejection threshold for food) --- **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]** **(i) Tristimulus Colorimeter (3 marks):** - **Principle**: Uses three or more filtered photodetectors whose spectral sensitivities approximate the CIE $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$ colour-matching functions. - **Components**: Light source → Sample port → Three filtered photodiodes → ADC → Microprocessor → Display of L*, a*, b*, X, Y, Z. - **Geometry**: Typically 45°/0° or 0°/45°. - **Applications**: Rapid QC of sauces, dough, confectionery, dairy, meat, packaging. - **Advantages**: Fast (≈ 1 s), portable, rugged, simple to operate. **(ii) Spectrophotometer with Integrating Sphere (3 marks):** - **Principle**: Illuminates the sample with monochromatic light (swept across 380–780 nm) and collects the diffusely reflected light using an integrating sphere (typically barium-sulphate coated). The sphere integrates light from all directions. - **Components**: Broad-band light source (tungsten + deuterium) → Monochromator (grating/prism) → Beam splitter (reference beam) → Integrating sphere (sample + reference ports) → Detector → Computer. - **Applications**: Pigment analysis in juices, oils, wines; transmission measurement of clear liquids; spectral fingerprinting of food additives. - **Advantages**: Provides complete spectral data; allows choice of illuminant/observer post-measurement; higher accuracy; supports derived calculations like ΔE*, WI, YI, BI. **Comparison (1 mark):** | Feature | Tristimulus Colorimeter | Spectrophotometer | |---------|------------------------|-------------------| | Speed | Fast | Slower | | Spectral data | No | Yes (380–780 nm) | | Cost | Low | High | | Best for | Routine QC | R&D, pigment analysis | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Colour & L*a*b* Questions** > > 1. **Mixing up Hunter Lab with CIELAB** — Hunter uses $L=10\sqrt{Y}$ and $a=17.5(Y-X)/\sqrt{Y}$; CIELAB uses cube-root. Examiners deduct full marks if the wrong system is used without stating the source. **Always specify "CIELAB 1976"**. > > 2. **Forgetting the branch of the $f(t)$ function** — For dark samples where $Y/Y_n < 0.008856$, the **linear** branch $7.787\,t+16/116$ MUST be used, not the cube root. Ignoring this gives wrong L* for very dark foods (e.g., chocolate, roasted coffee). > > 3. **Wrong quadrant for hue angle** — $\tan^{-1}(b^*/a^*)$ returns values in $-90°$ to $+90°$. You must adjust by adding 360° (or 180°) for the correct quadrant. Example: $a^*<0, b^*>0$ means $h^\circ$ in the second quadrant (yellow-green); add 180° if using a simple $\tan^{-1}$. > > 4. **Forgetting units and illuminant** — Examiner will deduct marks if you do not specify the **illuminant (D65/A/C) and observer (2°/10°)** when stating measurement conditions. > > 5. **No interpretation of ΔE*** — A computation question without a final qualitative statement (acceptable/reject) typically loses 1–2 marks. > > 6. **In Part B derivations, skipping the cube-root role** — Examiners almost always award 1 mark specifically for explaining *why* the cube root is used (perceptual uniformity). Do not omit this. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Colour, Measuring Equipment & L*a*b\* System** > > ✅ **Definition**: Colour is a psycho-physico-chemical attribute determined by light source + sample + observer. > > ✅ **Tristimulus values X, Y, Z** are the foundation of all CIE colour systems, computed by integrating $S(\lambda) \cdot R(\lambda) \cdot \bar{x}(\lambda)$ etc. over 380–780 nm. > > ✅ **CIELAB (1976)** is the **standard** for food colour measurement. Three axes: **L*** (lightness), **a*** (red–green), **b*** (yellow–blue). > > ✅ **Cube-root transformation** is used to approximate the nonlinear human visual response and ensure **perceptual uniformity**. > > ✅ **Chroma C*** = $\sqrt{a^{*2}+b^{*2}}$ → colour saturation/vividness. > > ✅ **Hue angle h°** = $\arctan(b^*/a^*)$ with quadrant correction → colour family (0° red, 90° yellow, 180° green, 270° blue). > > ✅ **ΔE*** = $\sqrt{\Delta L^{*2}+\Delta a^{*2}+\Delta b^{*2}}$ is the **single most used metric in food QC** for batch-to-batch comparison. > > ✅ **ΔE* thresholds to memorize**: < 1 (not visible), 1–2 (close inspection), 2–3.5 (glance), 3.5–5 (clear), > 5 (major). > > ✅ **Whiteness Index (WI)** is used for milk powder, sugar, flour, starch; **Yellowness Index (YI)** for oils and Maillard products; **Browning Index (BI)** for bread, fried foods, coffee. > > ✅ **Equipment categories**: Tristimulus colorimeters (fast QC), Spectrophotometers (spectral analysis), Computer vision systems (non-destructive sorting). > > ✅ **Standardized conditions** MUST be reported: **D65 illuminant + 10° observer** is the most common for food. > > ✅ **Hunter Lab ≠ CIELAB** — Different transformation (square root vs. cube root). Do not interchange. > > ✅ **Examiner focus areas**: Derivation of L*a*b* from XYZ (with cube-root justification), computation of C*, h°, ΔE*, interpretation of ΔE* in QC context, and discussion of equipment choice based on application.
